COMPARISON OFA100-80GBANDRTX 5090-32GB GPUS
GPU Memory Power Price (USD)
NVIDIA RTX 5090-32GB [57] 32 GB 575 W $2,000

on a single consumer-grade RTX 5090 ($2k). With perfor-
mance comparable to medium models, they suit small and mid-
sized enterprises prioritizing cost efficiency and local control.
Although they trail the highest benchmarks, the practical
performance gap between 30B- and 70B-class models remains
modest, indicating that smaller deployments can meet a wide
range of enterprise needs.
While commercial APIs still hold a slight edge in peak
accuracy and efficiency, open-weight models now provide vi-
able, cost-effective alternatives. The performance gaps across
large, medium, and small open deployments are far narrower
than their order-of-magnitude hardware cost differences. For
many organizations, deploying a medium or even small open
model locally offers a sustainable break-even option balancing
capability, cost, and autonomy from external providers.
B. Cost Model
The one-time infrastructure cost is defined as:
Chardware =N GPU ·C GPU (1)
Assuming business operation of 8 hours/day and 20
days/month, the monthly electricity cost is:
Celectricity =N GPU ·P GPU ·H operation ·R electricity (2)
The total local deployment cost becomes:
Clocal(t) =Chardware +C electricity ·t(3)
wheretis the number of months after deployment,C GPU and
NGPU denote per-unit GPU cost and quantity,P GPU power
consumption,R electricity the electricity rate, andH operation the
monthly operating hours.
C. Commercial API Throughput Analysis
For a fair comparison, API costs are normalized by the token
generation capacityQ capacity achievable on local hardware:
Qcapacity =T throughput ·H operation ·3600(4)
The equivalent API cost for producing the same token
volume per month is:
CAPI(Qcapacity) = Qcapacity
3 · Cinput
1M + 2Qcapacity
3 · Coutput
1M (5)
and scales linearly with time:
CAPI(t) =CAPI(Qcapacity)·t(6)
HereC input andC output are API prices per million in-
put/output tokens, using a 2:1 ratio typical of real workloads.
D. Break-Even Analysis
Local and API costs are modeled as time-dependent func-
tions:
Clocal(t) =CAPI(t)(7)
Solving yields the break-even timet ∗, where cumulative
local and API costs are equal. Fort > t ∗, local deployment
becomes more economical, particularly under sustained, high-
throughput usage. The next section reportst ∗ values across
open-source and commercial models.

E. Comprehensive Break-Even Analysis Results
By substituting the values from Table I and Table II into
Equation (7), we obtain the results summarized in Table IV,
with cost details in Table III. Our analysis across 54 deploy-
ment scenarios—covering nine open-source models and six
commercial APIs—shows substantial variation in economic
viability across model sizes.
Small Models.Sub-30B models prove highly cost-effective,
often reaching break-even within three months. Their low
hardware cost enables smaller organizations to sustain com-
petitive AI capabilities without ongoing subscription fees.
Medium Models.Medium-scale deployments require mod-
erate investment but achieve favorable returns over time. They
offer a practical balance between performance and cost and are
suitable for enterprises with steady, high-volume workloads.
Large Models.Large-scale setups face the steepest eco-
nomic barriers. Despite competitive accuracy, their high hard-
ware and power demands extend break-even horizons. This
challenge makes them viable only for organizations with
sustained, large-scale inference needs.
F . Deployment Decision Framework
We analyzed break-even points by both model size and
enterprise type. Small, medium, and large organizations have
different needs for computing power, regulatory compliance,
and financial resources. These factors shape whether on-
premise deployment is practical. Table IV reports the quantita-
tive results. The following sections explain what these results
mean for organizations of different sizes.
1) Small Enterprises (SMEs):For SMEs with limited bud-
gets and moderate workloads (<10M tokens/month), small
open-source models such as EXAONE 4.0 32B and Qwen3-
30B offer the most viable entry point. Our results show that
break-even can occur in as little as 0.3–3 months depending on
the commercial baseline (e.g., Claude-4 Opus vs. GPT-5). This
aligns with recent findings that SMEs prioritize cost savings
and data control over absolute performance [21], [23]. The
feasibility of deploying on consumer-grade GPUs (e.g., RTX
5090 at∼$2,000) further reduces capital barriers. Typical use
cases include customer support automation, internal knowl-
edge search, and lightweight document analysis—tasks where
30B-class models provide sufficient accuracy [35], [47].
2) Medium Enterprises:Medium-scale enterprises (pro-
cessing 10–50M tokens/month) represent the sweet spot for
on-premise adoption. Medium models such as GLM-4.5-Air
and Llama-3.3-70B demonstrate balanced economics, with
break-even periods ranging from 3.8 to 34 months depend-
ing on provider comparison. Hardware requirements remain

manageable ($15k–$30k for dual A100 setups), and through-
put levels (120–220 tokens/sec) are sufficient for concurrent
business workloads such as code assistance, analytics, and
customer-facing applications. This tier benefits most from
hybrid strategies, where sensitive workloads run locally and
burst traffic leverages cloud APIs [8], [22]. Regulatory-driven
industries such as healthcare and finance particularly favor
medium-scale deployments for balancing compliance and cost
[10].
3) Large Enterprises:For large enterprises with extreme-
scale workloads (>50M tokens/month), large open-source
models (e.g., Qwen3-235B, Kimi-K2) become economically
attractive, albeit with longer break-even horizons (3.5–69.3
months). While upfront investments exceed $40k–$190k, these
organizations often already operate GPU clusters for other
workloads, reducing incremental CapEx [31], [56]. Use cases
include enterprise-wide generative applications, advanced re-
search, and domain-specific copilots requiring reasoning depth
comparable to commercial APIs. However, when compared
against aggressively priced providers such as Gemini 2.5
Pro, break-even can extend to 5–9 years, challenging the
economic case unless privacy, sovereignty, or vendor lock-in
are overriding concerns [19], [33]. Thus, for this tier, non-
financial factors (e.g., strategic autonomy, compliance) often
weigh more heavily than pure cost.
VII. CONCLUSION
This study evaluates 54 deployment scenarios to clarify the
economic trade-offs of on-premise versus API-based LLM use.
Results show that deployment economics are highly context-
dependent and challenge common assumptions about local
feasibility.
•Small-scale:Break even within 3 months, making local
deployment accessible for smaller organizations.
•Medium-scale:Offer balanced performance and cost,
with recovery typically within 6–24 months.
•Large-scale:Require longer horizons (often beyond 2
years), feasible only for sustained, high-volume work-
loads.
•Pricing variability:Differences among providers create
large swings in cost-effectiveness, with premium tiers
enabling faster local payback.
Strategic Implications.Deployment choices can be
grouped into short-term (0–6 months), mid-term (6–24
months), and long-term (≥24 months) investments, which
offers organizations a practical lens to align cost recovery
with strategic goals. The rapid evolution of models, hardware,
and pricing means deployment is a continuous optimization
problem rather than a one-time decision.
Future Work.Further research should empirically validate
these break-even estimates and extend TCO models to in-
clude staffing and maintenance, and explore hybrid paradigms
that combine economic efficiency with reliability. Continued
benchmarking will be essential to track performance and cost
convergence between open and commercial ecosystems.
Overall, this study situates break-even analysis within the
broader landscape of evolving LLM technology and highlights
both the growing accessibility of local deployment and the
ongoing economic tension with cloud-based services.

2023 International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS) | 979-8-3503-3583-5/23/$31.00 ©2023 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/ICISCOIS56541.2023.10100589
AI-Based mock interview evaluator: An emotion 
and confid ence clas sifi er model 
Rubi Mandal Pranav Lahar Dhiraj Patil 
Dept. of Information Technology 
SVKM's JOT Dhule 
mandalruby@gmail.com 
De pt. of Information Technology 
SVKM's loT Dhule 
Dept. of Information Technology 
SVKM's loT Dhule 
dhirajpati1.14 34@gmail.com pmvlohar@gmail.com 
Apurva Patil 
Dept. of Information Technology 
SVKM's loT Dhule 
patilapurva927 @gmail.com 
Abstract-Interviews are extremely important for a 
candidate because it is the time when all your hard work is put 
on the line to get some desired fruitful outcomes in life. It is 
extremely important in our educational system and 
recruitment process since they aid in the selection of the right 
candidate based on the required skills. Mock interviews can 
boost our confidence and communication skills which can help 
to perform better. This paper proposed an AI-based mock 
interview platform that would operate as an intermediatory 
between the actual interview and preparation mode. Our 
system will assess the user based on an aggregation of three 
parameters called emotions, confidence, and knowledge base. 
Emotion is judged based on facial expression using deep 
learning CNN algorithm which will classify the emotion among 
the 7 categorical emotions and confidence evaluation is based 
on speech recognition using natural language processing and 
Pydub audio python libraries. For knowledge assessment, 
keyword mapping, semantic analysis technique is used and web 
scraping module will extract keywords from received replies 
and map them to online resources. Hence this system will not 
only lower the stress and anxiety before an actual job interview 
but also improve the candidate's confidence. 
Keywords: deep learning, CNN, categorical emotions, 
NLP, confidence evaluation, web scraping 
I. INTRODUCTION 
The interview process is a vital point in the recruitment 
process. It helps a recruiter understand whether a candidate 
is best for a position and judges the candidate in deciding 
whether the job suits them or not [1]. The period for an 
interview is usually 45 to 90 minutes (about 1 and a half 
hours). According to human psychology, a person only has 
seven seconds to make a strong impression [2], and as we 
know the first impression is the last impression. During this 
time, candidates feel lots of nervousness about looking 
good, try to impress their recruiter and not come off as 
nervous and keep eye contact and confidence. A recent 
statistical report about interviews shows that eye contact is a 
very important parameter to making a great impression 
according to 67% of recruiters and due to their lack of 
confidence level, voice quality or not smiling 39% of the 
candidates leave a bad impression on the recruiter [3][4]. 
Generally, our existing traditional interview system is very 
much physical means the interviewer asks the question [5], 
and the interviewee gives the answer based on the answers 
given and the confidence level and knowledge the candidate 
979-8-35 03-3583- 5/23/$3 1 .00 @2023 IEEE 
Suvama Wagh 
Dept. of Information Technology 
SVKM's loT Dhule 
suvamawagh2502200 I @gmail.com 
has the chance of getting selected. the importance of the 
interview system: The key element in interview preparation 
is attending mock interviews. You may wonder why mock 
interviews are important. Can't I do away with them? If you 
already know how to face a virtual interview it will 
definitely help you in your interview. 
Here are some reasons why mock interviews are important: 
1. Personality Analysis: - Mock interviews help you analyse 
your personality. You will know about your strengths and 
weaknesses inside and outside. With a mock interview, you 
will know how not to fall into the personality question traps 
set by the interviewers. 
2. Expert Guidance: Mock interviews conducted by a panel 
of experts. They are domain experts, Thus, by attending 
mock interviews, you will get expert guidance on how to get 
a good score in the actual interview. A system is trained in 
such a way that, the system works as a domain expert 
according to the candidate's requirements. They provide a 
detailed analysis of things you need to improve by testing 
you on various aspects like your personality, subject matter 
knowledge, etc . 
3. Ambiance Akin to Actual Interview -Mock interviews 
replicate the ambience of the actual interview. As such, 
there will be a five-member panel conducting mock 
interviews. 
The main aim here is to familiarize you with the way of 
conducting an interview and make you less fearful of the 
actual interview. For it has been seen that many candidates 
fumble at the final interview due to a lack of awareness 
about the interview. Fear takes over, and they struggle to 
perform at the interview. Attending mock interviews will 
help avoid this. 
4. Questioning Diversity: - It is futile to guess what the 
panel member might ask you during the actual interview. 
But, preparing yourself for the challenge will help. A mock 
interview thus prepares you in advance for any adversity 
you might face in front of the interview board. 
521 
5. Feedback: - The key element of mock interviews is the 
feedback that you receive. After an interview session, the

522 2023 International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
panel members will share their feedback through the panel 
discussion. You will get to know about the areas where you 
need to improve. 
Current virtual interview system: 
In today's scenario, there are many online platforms to give 
and take interviews but it only considers the knowledge base 
as the judging parameter for the candidate, for example, 
exist the system but as we all know the interview is not only 
about judging the knowledge of candidate it is also about 
analysing the behavioural skills, personality traits, and 
character judgment .The current AI-based interview system 
is select the candidates on their video recording. As the 
world moves toward the era of technology. Even though the 
pandemic is over 82% of recmiters are hiring via online 
interviews. But some candidates face lots of problems 
giving online interviews such as Smiles, nods, head tilts, 
and other social causes may be harder to notice and 
empathize with, it is more difficult to remember those things 
when we are already nnder pressure. The only solution is 
practice and more practice. To overcome the problem, this 
research is an ai-based mock interview evaluator, which is a 
dynamic interview evaluator. It takes input as a facial 
expression to check the emotion, and for confidence, the 
system will use the knowledge and speech frequencies. This 
module divides into three sections in which, section I is the 
introduction section explaining the needs of the interview 
evaluator, the domain of research, which type of 
technologies are used, and how our research will be used for 
the candidates. Section I also explains the flow of the 
system. Section IT deals with literature research, we studied 
the existing system and the types of technologies used in 
it. The research helped us to find out the conflict in the 
currently proposed system. In section III deals with the 
complete architecture and methodology of the system using 
the diagrams and flow chart, and which types of algorithms 
were used in the refereed research paper. Section IV deals 
with a comparative analysis of the existing and proposed 
systems. The analysis helped to find out the lacunas in the 
existing system. It shows that the existing systems are not 
dynamic, and the system follows a lengthy process to 
generate the output. Section V shows the complete system 
with its output. 
II. LITERATCRE REVIEW 
The research focuses on a mock-interview system that gives 
candidates with latest interview questions as well as Al­
given feedback. In this paper, they ereated a mock interview 
system to help recmiters properly choose potential 
applicants while also allowing job hopefuls to perform mock 
interviews. The system combines video, speech, and text 
information to analyze sentiments, head posture, voice, 
DISC characteristics, and intrinsic attributes, as well as to 
assess aggregate interview scores. MIP, an interview 
system, assists recmiters in successfully selecting potential 
applic ants with automated scoring method<> that are largely 
focused on analyzing Chinese interviews. Furthermore, it 
combines visual, auditory, and textual elements to analyse 
emotions, head posture, voice, personality traits, and 
intrinsic attributes, as well as to evaluate overall interview 
performance [1]. Based on nonverbal clues, a machine­
learning technique for identifying and analyzing changes in 
interviewee behaviour and personality characteristics. The 
emotions, eye movements, grins, and head movements of 
the candidate have all been studied [2]. A method can help 
people prepare for interviews when they are unsure about 
various sorts of questions and how to answer them. The 
interviewee can prepare for the technical aptitude exam 
according to their difficulty level, which also aids in 
preparation for face-to-face interviews [3]. Emoticons are an 
essential part of who we are and how we behave has been 
found using EEG [4] to track emotions. For the 
classification and identification of emotion, the arousal and 
valence model has been used. The model distributes the 
emotions in 2-D space and has a&v dimensions. To train 
classi fiers and categorize arousal and positive and negative 
valence emotional states they used Machine learning 
techniques [6]. Evaluation for customer satisfaction (CS) 
and finding suitable features to recognize emotions have 
been discussed robustly. For the classifica tion of emotion, a 
well-known corpus has been used and tor the cs evaluation, 
they are dependent on the recordings of suggestions 
by candidate about the received information during calls. 
For posterior analysis, the author used recordings available 
in the call centre. classi fication of speech emotion, authors 
consider three "standard" databases Interactive Both Ended 
Emotional Dyadic Movement Capture, Sentiment database, 
and Ryerson speech-video Database of sentiment Speech 
and Song [7]. To solve the problems which are facing by 
students during the interview authors developed the virtual 
interview training system. The system focuses on three 
important parts: three agents with distinct types of 
personalities. The system has helped shy col lege students to 
improve their interview performances. For interview system 
development the author had been used human-computer 
interaction and virtual reality technology [8] . This paper's 
author had been designed the speech communication 
disorder model (SCD) prediction model using; machine­
learning, to explore eye movement during facial emotion 
recognition tasks and show their correlation \vith SCA [9]. 
Interaction between machines and human is always difficult 
and it was more critical when we try to recognize on 
automatically using naturalistic human interaction (HMI). 
So, to overcome this situation author used EEG and speech 
signals. The stands for electroencephalogram, for 
emotion verification, they use<.� their emotion database 
namely a multi-modal emotion database, containing 
(MED4). For this research author developed four baseline 
algorithms which are Probabilistic Linear Discriminant 
analysis, Multi-Layer Perception Network, Temporal 
Convolution Network, and Extreme, Learning Machine. The 
author conducted the test in two different environmental 
conditions with a particular number of people. Based on the 
test, the author generated the output [ 1 0] . The Authors 
discussed cmcial factors of speech recognition which 
are and gender. For classifi cation, they build the 
hierarchical classifica tion model using this module 
identification of emotion is going to be easy [ 1 1].

2023 International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS) 523
III. ARCHITECTURE AND METHODOLOGY 
Our system will evaluate the student's performance in the 
interview. The parameters that will be considered by the 
system at the time of evaluating the score are the facial 
emotion of the candidate, confidence based on speech, and 
knowledge. Our system is divided into 5 phases, Face 
recogmtwn (Authentication), Data Separation, Facial 
expression recognition, Confidence recognition based on 
speech score and Knowledge Base. 
rotation, inten sity normal ization 
Feature extraction 
Fig. 1. Architecture of System 
A. Face recognition: 
a. Image Acquisition: The system will require the 
candidate's photo, which he uploaded at the time of 
registration. Based on that photo, the system will recognize 
that person, and if the person is confirmed, then it will 
continue. 
b. Image pre-processing: First received image will get 
converted into BGR to grayscale format. Then it will crop in 
the shape of 50*50 pixels. And then it is used for 
recognition. 
c. Recognition: the system uses a Har-cascade classifier to 
classify the different faces. For face recognition, the system 
uses the predefined python library Face_ Recognition. In that 
case, the live photo of the candidate will be converted into 
face encodings and compared with the encoding of the 
photo that the candidate uploaded at the registration 
comparison system using compare face function of the Face 
Recognition library. 
B. Data separation: 
As given in fig. 1. The system will receive live data it will 
get separated in video audio format for future use for getting 
live video data using the OpenCV library and for getting 
live audio data system using the library. 
C. Facial Expression Recognition: 
a. Dee p Learning model training: 
i. Dataset: a dataset is pre-divided into a trammg and 
testing folder which contains 2882 1 images in the training 
and 7066 images in testing. It has 7 different categorical 
values i.e., happy, sad, angry, disgust, anger, neutral, and 
fear image has a dimension of 48*48. 
ii. Training the model: for training, we have used CNN 
algorithm because we have to perform operations on an 
image and it takes a lot of time and resources. CNN uses 
parameter-sharing techniques and dimensionality reduction 
so it red uces the required time and computation. As 
explained in fig. 2. The deep learning CNN algorithm is 
used for training. Max pooling is used for getting the 
maximum value in a two-dimensional filter over each 
channel of the feature map. The model has 6 layers. The 
first four are convolutional layers and the remaining two are 
fully connected layers dense. The convolutional works as a 
filter it divides the image into sub-image (partial images) 
and extracts typical features (like the position of the eyes, 
nose, lips, and eyebrows) in fully connected layers these 
sub-images merge and find relevant information and based 
on this information it classifies the emotion. For training 10 
epochs were given and generated a training curacy of 80.75 
and a testing accuracy of 76.34. 
CNN input Convolution 
�' 
Convolution 
'd 
FullyConnected FullyConnBcted 
"' 
Fig. 2. Working of CNN Algorithm 
b. Pre-processing the data: as live video is received, the 
system will break it into frames, and each frame performs 
pre-processing because it is needed to get more accuracy. 
First, the image will get crop d in a ratio of 48*48. Then it 
will get converted into a grayscale image. After that based 
on the requirement according to the ng to model intensity 
will normalize. 
c. Feature extraction: after the pre-processing features like 
the position of eyes, and lips, the nose is extracted through 4 
convolutional layers 
d. Prediction: the extracted feature is passed through the 
model and the model produced the output emotion. Based 
on the predicted emotion the score will increase and 
decrease accordingly. 
D. Confidence recognition based on speech score: 
This module focuses on speech recogmtwn and 
categorization based on 7 emotions, this module consists of 
sub-modules: 
a. Data preprocessing: This module focuses on 
preprocessing the data before it fits into the prediction 
model. Preprocessing consists of different steps, 
• Speech Segment: The speech audio is loaded by 
the library 'Speech Segment'. 
• Normalization: Each 'Audio Segment' object is 
normalized to + 5.0 dB. Transforming the object to 
an array of samples is crucial for the rest of the 
preprocessmg. 
• Removing extraneous information by shortening 
the silence at the beginning and the end. For length 
normalization, padding each audio track to the 
same length. Noise reduction is being performed.

/g72/g65/g52/g72/g71/g59/g66/g69/g60/g77/g56/g55/g1/g54/g52/g65/g55/g60/g55/g52/g71/g56/g70/g1/g57/g66/g69/g1/g60/g65/g71/g56/g69/g73/g60/g56/g74/g70/g11/g1
524 2023 International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
This module also performs feature extraction like 
1. Energy (Root Mean Square) 
2. Zero Crossed Rate (ZCR) 
3. Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficient (MFCC) 
b. Model Training: 
For training, every sample was labeled with specific 
emotional names, and then samples were module using into 
one flattened array of different classes named X hen labels 
were named Ys Y. Finally input variables were fed into a 
training model for model selection, used LSTM: long short­
term memory. Here models were trained using different 
dense layers of neural networks. 
E. For Speech Recognition: 
The system used developed models with the help of a deep 
learning algorithm for development firstly system trained 
model with 8 emotions for which it used the dataset having 
audio files of 7 different categorical emotions (happy, sad, 
Neutral, disgust, angry, fearful, surprised total 2800 files = 2 
actor's x 200 phrases x 7 emotions per actor. 
Normalization: The 'Audio Segment' object is normalized 
using Pydub. The object is converted into an Array of 
samples by NumPy & Audio Segment. Moving the silence 
at the beginning and the end using Librosa every file is 
padded to equal length using NumPy for length 
equalization. Noise reduction is being performed by nmse 
reduction. For training, every sample was labeled w1th 
specific emotional names and then samples were modeled 
using one flattened array of different classes named X hence 
labels named Ys Y. Finally input variables were fed into a 
training model for model selection, LSTM: long short-term 
memory. Here models were trained using different dense 
layers of neural networks. For Validation purposes: On real­
time Prediction, real-time audio using a microphone and 
internally preprocessed is used before feeding into the 
trained model and the real-time label edition is shown. 
F. Knowledge-based: 
The received audio will be first converted into text which 
will be extracted for evaluation. The concept of speech-to­
text conversion has been used here. Further keyword 
extraction is done from the sentence. The First syntax will 
get checked from the received sentence and then semantic 
correctrless will get checked. Owing to this correctrless of 
the answer will be checked based on the keywords present 
in our dataset. It will now get matched with the given 
answer and based on the keyword's presence the score will 
be generated. In addition to this syntax and semantics scores 
will also be generated. Once all the scores are generated 
results will be declared based on the average score. There 
will be a threshold of specific points set by the interviewer. 
Above that threshold all, the students will get selected. 
G. Scoring module: 
The scoring system of our system is divided into 3 parts: 
facial emotion score, confidence score and knowledge base. 
Fig. 3. Scoring module 
The above Fig. shows the overall module of our scoring 
system Which describes the detailed planning of the scoring 
process. It takes the important first 4 parameters to be 
judged parameters are 
1. Emotion score has a weightage of 20% which is in the 
range of 1 to 7. 
2. Speech has a weightage of 20% which is divided into 
positive or negative. 
3. Confidence with a score of 10%. 
4. Knowledge has a score of 50% which is again divided 
into subtypes which are given below. 
i. Semantic Correctrless has a weightage of 10%. 
ii. Syntax Correctrless also has 10%. 
iii. Answer Correctrless is 30% 
So, the differentiation of different scoring parameters and 
flow is explained above, and finally after going through all 
the parameters of judging the candidate's candidature finally 
system generates the final total score which will help 
recruiters and interviewers to select the best candidates. 
IV. COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS 
1. The proposed system takes as input live video while in 
the existing system, the input is recorded video. 
2. System considers emotion as a parameter, but the 
existing system doesn't have emotion as a parameter. 
3. System positivity system can check the positivity and 
confidence of candidates, But the current system does 
not have that kind of parameter. 
4. Our system is a knowledge-based Dynamic system, but 
the existing systems have static validation. 
5. Our system can take less time to declare the result in 
seconds, but the existing system takes 10 to 75 days to 
declare the result. In our system, AI-bot-to-human 
interaction is done but in the existing system Normal 
question-and-answer through video recording. 
V. RESULT 
To evaluate the model performance, we used three types of 
parameters, which are emotion, knowledge base, and 
confidence. Facial expression is very important for judging 
the emotion of humans. Also, face recognition is es sential 
for the system because it supplies security by not giving 
unauthorized candidates for interviews.

authorized candidates present, only those candidates can 
give an interview.

Fig. 10. Score Evaluation Algorithm 
As the score plays a very crucial part in our system so it 
should be handled very carefully so we have built this 
algorithm to calculate the overall score shown in fig. 10. 
VI. CONCLUSION 
The proposed system in this paper is designed for candidates 
who do not perfonn well in interviews, because of 
confidence and shyness. The mock-interview system 
proposed in this paper is a dynamic mock-interview system. 
The system takes input from the live video and analyses the 
perfonnance of the candidate on the bases of three 
parameters, knowledge bases, emotion, and confidence. 
According to the candidate's perfonnance in an interview, 
the system generates the overall score. The existing system 
does not have kind parameters such as the confidence level 
of a candidate. For the curated result, we used one more 
parameter confidence. Our system can also provide a 
detailed analysis of things in candidates who are giving the

## [6] AI Based Mock Interview System Using Natural Language Processing
- **Authors:** N. G. Nandha Kumar, R. P. Monisha, R. V. Priyanka, S. Raghavi, M. Sriram
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `AI_Based_Mock_Interview_System_Using_Natural_Language_Processing.pdf`

2025 International Conference on Advanced Computing Technologies (ICoACT)
AI Based Mock Interview System Using Natural
Language Processing
Senthilkumar K
Assistant Professor, Department of Information Technology
K. S. Rangasamy College of Technology (Autonomous)
Tiruchengode, India
senthilkumar@ksrct.ac.in
Ranjith S
Department of Information Technology
K. S. Rangasamy College of Technology (Autonomous)
Tiruchengode, India
hiphopranjith194@gmail.com
Sivasakthi M
Department of Information Technology
K. S. Rangasamy College of Technology (Autonomous)
Tiruchengode, India
sivasakthim245@gmail.com
Ramvignesh R
Department of Information Technology
K. S. Rangasamy College of Technology (Autonomous)
Tiruchengode, India
ramvignesh618@gmail.com
Abstract—Mock interviews are crucial for job applicants to
enhance and polish their skills before to real interviews. This
study presents a novel AI-driven Mock Interview System that
replicates authentic interview settings through the utilization
of Natural Language Processing (NLP), Deep Learning, and
Computer Vision. The system incorporates Speech-to-Text for
response processing, Text-to-Speech for feedback dissemination,
and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for the adaptive assessment
of response precision. Employing OpenCV and MediaPipe, it
examines behaviors such as facial expressions, ocular movements,
and additional non-verbal signals. The system produces domain-
specific questions, assesses fluency, coherence, and content, and
delivers comprehensive feedback on linguistic skills, technical
knowledge, and professional conduct. Experimental findings
indicate 95% accuracy in verbal communication analysis, 93%
precision in behavioral assessment, and a 90% user satisfaction
rating. The solution transcends conventional mock interview con-
straints, guaranteeing scalability, personalization, and immediate
feedback, thereby enabling job seekers to refine their skills and
bolster their confidence for successful interviews.
Index Terms—AI, Natural Language Processing, Mock In-
terviews, Speech-to-Text, Text-to-Speech, Behavioral Analysis,
Feedback System.
I. I NTRODUCTION
It is of great importance for the person to simulate real-life
interview scenarios and get actionable feedback on building
up their confidence and performance, especially as preparation
is a critical factor in competitive job markets in the process
[1]. Traditional mock interviews have several shortcomings in
terms of dependency on human resources, non-standardization,
and lack of scalability. The authors thus suggest an AI-
based Mock Interview System using the newest technologies
like NLP, Deep Learning, and Computer Vision. The system
conducts real-time mock interviews and tests candidates com-
prehensively: for their linguistic fluency, technical know-how,
and behavioral responses. Speech emotion recognition is an
element in the overall understanding of the verbal interaction;
it enables the system to pick up human emotions in a subtle
manner. Advancements in NLP have greatly added much
towards the development of interactive dialogue systems that
allow machines to be able to understand and appropriately
respond to natural language inputs. Such technologies form the
backbone of our mock interview system, providing dynamic
and context-aware question generation [2].
The system incorporates Speech-to-Text(STT) and Text-
to-Text(TTS) technologies, where voice recognition coupled
with the response generation that followed was a revolution
in human-computer interaction. Moreover, the use of aug-
mented speech recognition techniques such as Mel-Frequency
Cepstral Coefficients, and machine learning models such as
Random Forest, enhances the accuracy levels of response
assessment. Our system integrates behavioral analysis via
tools such as OpenCV and MediaPipe for assessing non-
verbal cues including facial expressions and body language
that are essential to determine professional demeanour. The
multimodal approach ensures holistic assessment, both verbal
and non-verbal components of communication. RL further
optimizes the mechanism of feedback through adaptation
and personalized performances. Existing commercial systems
provide a good base for building a scalable and efficient system
[3].
Generally, such systems fail to integrate high-end feed-
back mechanisms and holistic frameworks for evaluation. The
proposed system bridges this gap by integrating linguistic
analysis, knowledge evaluation in domains, and behavioral
insights into an overall architecture. Innovations in text-to-
speech synthesis ensure the delivery of feedback in an acces-
sible and engaging fashion. It aims at enhancing the process
of preparation undertaken by the job seeker using a system
based on an AI through tailored and adaptive simulation of
interviews for bridging a gap between abstract preparation and
specific applications. Multiple ways exist under which candi-
dates are better prepared for doing well within a professional
atmosphere. Some of the planned future extensions for the
979-8-3315-0985-9/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE
2025 International Conference on Advanced Computing Technologies (ICoACT) | 979-8-3315-0985-9/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/ICOACT63339.2025.11005032
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:30 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

system are multilingual support and gamification, enhancing
utility and engagement in the use of the system [4]. The
primary objectives of this research is
• The system uses fine-tuned NLP models (e.g., GPT, T5)
for dynamic question generation and integrates Rein-
forcement Learning (RL) for real-time complexity ad-
justment. Advanced STT technologies like Mozilla Deep-
Speech ensure accurate transcription, creating a scalable,
adaptive, and realistic interview simulation.
• The system employs real-time transcription, linguistic
analysis, and adaptive scoring mechanisms powered by
reinforcement learning to deliver precise and contextually
relevant evaluations.
• Advanced models trained on FER2013 datasets to en-
hance emotion detection accuracy, while MediaPipe en-
sures real-time tracking of posture and gaze stability.
• The use of reinforcement learning for iterative feedback
refinement and the integration of multimodal analysis
ensure that feedback is comprehensive, personalized, and
actionable.
II. R EVIEW OF RELATED LITERATURE
A. Traditional Mock Interview Methods
Traditional mock interviews depend significantly on physi-
cal labour and human assessors to replicate interview situations
[5]. These methods emphasise evaluating candidates’ commu-
nication and technical abilities but frequently lack scalability,
personalisation, and objectivity [6]. In such systems, feedback
is restricted to human observation, resulting in potential in-
consistencies and biases. Moreover, conventional methods fail
to offer real-time insights or adaptive educational possibilities
for candidates [7].
B. AI-Driven Interview Systems
AI-driven systems utilise Natural Language Processing,
Deep Learning, and Computer Vision to provide authentic
and scalable interview simulations [8]. These systems inte-
grate Speech-to-Text and Text-to-Speech for interaction, do
behavioural analysis for non-verbal indicators, and generate
adaptive questions based on user replies. AI-driven systems
facilitate multimodal assessments, providing thorough evalu-
ations in areas such as linguistic fluency, technical expertise,
and professional conduct [9].
C. Behavioral Analysis and Feedback Systems
Sophisticated systems evaluate behavioural characteristics,
including facial expressions, eye contact, and posture, em-
ploying technologies [10] like OpenCV and MediaPipe. They
offer comprehensive feedback on non-verbal communication,
enhancing verbal assessments. RL models augment existing
systems by providing adaptive scoring and feedback mech-
anisms, allowing candidates to discern their strengths and
shortcomings and systematically enhance their performance
over time [11], [12].
D. Problem Statement
Conventional mock interview methodologies are laborious,
erratic, and deficient in scalability. They frequently neglect
to offer real-time feedback, thorough assessments, or adaptive
learning, resulting in job seekers being ill-equipped for actual
situations. Moreover, current methods infrequently assess non-
verbal cues like as facial expressions and posture, which are
essential for conveying professionalism.
E. Proposed Solution
This study presents an AI-driven Mock Interview Sys-
tem utilizing NLP, Deep Learning, and Computer Vision.
It offers domain-specific question production, instantaneous
feedback on verbal and non-verbal communication, and adap-
tive assessments utilizing Reinforcement Learning. Integrating
multimodal evaluations guarantees comprehensive feedback,
scalability, and tailored advice, enabling users to succeed in
interviews.
III. METHODOLOGY: SYSTEM DESIGN AND
ARCHITECTURE
The AI-based Mock Interview System is supposed to fill the
gap between conventional mock interviews and modern, high-
tech solutions in terms of offering an automated, scalable, and
adaptive platform for job seekers.The detailed system design
and architecture are as follows
A. System Design
• Question Generation: Question Generation Module dy-
namically generates questions specific to the user’s job
role ( D) and desired difficulty level ( L). The generation
process is powered by advanced Natural Language Pro-
cessing (NLP) models such as GPT or T5, which are fine-
tuned on domain-specific datasets to ensure contextual
relevance and authenticity. For instance, in IT interviews,
questions about algorithms or software development are
prioritized to reflect real-world expectations. This dy-
namic capability allows the system to deliver an interview
session tailored to the user’s chosen field and level of
complexity, represented mathematically as:
Q = GenerateQuestion(D, L) (1)
• Dataset Used: Domain-specific datasets were curated
internally and fine-tuned using HuggingFace Transform-
ers, leveraging publicly available corpora like Common
Crawl.
The model is always relevant because of the pre-existing
language data which has been fine-tuned according to profes-
sional fields. For example, in IT interview questions based on
algorithms or software development have priority. The module
provides the user with an authentic interview session based on
his choice of the field and level of complexity.
ICoACT 2025
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:30 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

• Speech Analysis User responses (R) are converted into
textual format (T) using STT technology. The linguistic
evaluation involves three key metrics:
Scorelinguistic = α·Fluency+β·Coherence+γ·Knowledge
(2)
• Behavioral Analysis The video feed (V) is processed to
extract non-verbal features:
Facial Expressions: CNN models classify expressions
into categories such as confident, nervous, or neutral.
Posture and Eye Contact: MediaPipe analyzes head
alignment, eye focus, and body movements.
Scorebehavioral = δ · Fexp + ϵ · Fposture (3)
• Scoring and Feedback Generation The combined score
integrates both verbal and non-verbal evaluations:
Score = Scorelinguistic + Scorebehavioral (4)
The system uses this score to generate personalized
feedback, delivered both textually and verbally through
TTS. For example:
Textual Feedback: Your response was clear and well-
structured, but maintaining consistent eye contact could
enhance your impression.
Verbal Feedback: Provided using TTS for a more inter-
active user experience.
This feedback loop highlights strengths and areas for im-
provement, enabling iterative learning and better interview
preparation.
B. System Architecture
1) NLP
• Technologies: HuggingFace Transformers, GPT,
T5.
• Description: Generates dynamic, domain-specific
questions and analyzes user responses for coherence
and relevance.
2) Speech Processing
• Technologies: Google Speech API, Mozilla Deep-
Speech.
• Description: Converts spoken responses into text
accurately, enabling effective linguistic analysis.
3) Computer Vision
• Technologies: OpenCV , MediaPipe.
• Description: Detects facial expressions and ana-
lyzes posture to evaluate non-verbal communication
skills.
4) Reinforcement Learning
• Technologies: Custom RL Models.
• Description: Dynamically adapts scoring by learn-
ing from linguistic and behavioral performance pat-
terns.
5) TTS
• Technologies: Google TTS, Amazon Polly.
• Description: Delivers feedback verbally, enhanc-
ing user experience through clear, natural-sounding
speech.
6) Development Environment
• Technologies: Python, TensorFlow, PyTorch.
• Description: Supports model training, integration,
and deployment of NLP, vision, and RL compo-
nents.
7) Frontend
• Technologies: ReactJS, Flask.
• Description: Provides an interactive user interface
and connects backend AI models to the frontend.
C. Module Overview
1) User Input Processing Module: The User Input Pro-
cessing Module captures and preprocesses the user’s
speech and video inputs to initiate the evaluation pro-
cess. Speech is converted into text using advanced STT
technologies like Google Speech API or Mozilla Deep-
Speech, ensuring accurate transcription. Simultaneously,
video input is processed using OpenCV and MediaPipe
to analyze facial expressions, posture, and eye con-
tact. These inputs form the basis for both verbal and
non-verbal performance assessments, enabling a holistic
evaluation.
2) Question Generation Module:This module dynamically
generates domain-specific questions tailored to the user’s
chosen job role and difficulty level. Leveraging NLP
models like GPT and T5, the system ensures relevance
and contextual appropriateness by drawing on domain-
specific datasets. Questions adapt to the user’s perfor-
mance, creating an interactive and realistic interview
experience while maintaining alignment with industry-
specific expectations.
3) Response Evaluation Module:The Response Evaluation
Module analyzes user responses to measure linguis-
tic fluency, coherence, and domain-specific knowledge.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) models adaptively score
responses, while fine-tuned language models like BERT
and RoBERTa assess grammatical correctness and con-
tent relevance. This module ensures a comprehensive
evaluation by combining multiple linguistic metrics into
an objective scoring framework.
4) Behavioral Analysis Module:This module assesses non-
verbal cues such as facial expressions, posture, and
eye contact. Using CNN models trained on datasets
like FER2013, it evaluates emotional expressions, while
MediaPipe tracks body posture and gaze stability. These
insights provide a detailed understanding of the user’s
professionalism and confidence, essential traits for suc-
cess in interviews.
5) Feedback Generation Module:The Feedback Generation
Module synthesizes actionable feedback based on the
user’s verbal and non-verbal performance. TTS models
deliver verbal feedback in natural tones, while detailed
ICoACT 2025
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:30 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

Fig. 1: Proposed Methodology Workflow
textual reports outline areas of strength and improve-
ment. This dual-format feedback ensures users gain a
clear understanding of their performance, aiding in skill
refinement and confidence building.
Algorithm 1 AI-Based Mock Interview System
1: Input: User’s speech and video inputs
2: Output: Performance evaluation and actionable feedback
3: Step 1: User Input Processing Module
4: Capture and preprocess user’s speech and video inputs.
5: Convert speech to text using STT technologies (e.g.,
Google Speech API, Mozilla DeepSpeech).
6: Process video input using OpenCV and MediaPipe for
facial expression, posture, and eye contact analysis.
7: Step 2: Question Generation Module
8: Dynamically generate domain-specific questions tailored
to the user’s job role and difficulty level.
9: Utilize NLP models (e.g., GPT, T5) and domain-specific
datasets to ensure relevance and contextual appropriate-
ness.
10: Adapt questions to the user’s performance for an interac-
tive interview experience.
11: Step 3: Response Evaluation Module
12: Analyze user responses for linguistic fluency, coherence,
and domain knowledge.
13: Use Reinforcement Learning (RL) models for adaptive
scoring.
14: Fine-tune language models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) to
assess grammatical correctness and content relevance.
15: Step 4: Behavioral Analysis Module
16: Evaluate non-verbal cues such as facial expressions, pos-
ture, and eye contact.
17: Employ CNN models trained on datasets like FER2013
for emotional expression analysis.
18: Use MediaPipe to track body posture and gaze stability
for professionalism and confidence assessment.
19: Step 5: Feedback Generation Module
20: Generate actionable feedback based on verbal and non-
verbal performance.
21: Use TTS models to deliver verbal feedback in natural
tones.
22: Provide detailed textual reports outlining strengths and
areas for improvement.
23: End Algorithm
IV. R ESULT ANALYSIS
This proposal surpasses existing mock interview techniques
and will establish the benchmark for future simulated in-
terviews. Contemporary technologies like as reinforcement
learning, natural language processing, and behavioral analysis
facilitate its remarkable performance, adaptability, and variety.
It encompasses all verbal and nonverbal interview competen-
cies to enhance preparation across all areas.
The system must analyses data in real-time. This technology
provides users with instantaneous feedback to rectify any
ICoACT 2025
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:30 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

deficiencies, in contrast to time-consuming manual examina-
tions. This real-time effectiveness enables the system to cater
to broader audiences, including educational institutions and
training programs, hence conserving time for individuals and
groups. The dynamic question-generation module, utilizing
GPT and T5, is essential. The algorithm will produce context-
specific queries for various subjects, job roles, and levels of
difficulty. Technical enquiries are directed towards IT can-
didates, whilst leadership enquiries are aimed at managerial
positions. This versatility guarantees professional readiness for
many scenarios.
Speech, textual, and visual data are examined to assess
applicants. CNN and MediaPipe can assess nonverbal cues
such as facial expressions, posture, and emotions. Languages
exhibit an 88% reliability, while emotions demonstrate up to
90% accuracy. The adaptive feedback method, utilizing rein-
forcement learning, enhances evaluations as users engage with
the system. We offer written and verbal critiques tailored to
various learning preferences. This cycle enhances applicants’
confidence and competencies, facilitating development.
The methodology is versatile and suitable for educational
instruction, organizational assessments, and individual devel-
opment. The capacity to operate in real time and adjust to
evolving work functions underscores its significance across
many sectors. In a nutshell, the AI-Based Mock Interview
System is one of the steps forward in interview preparation that
brings dynamic evaluation and actionable insight into helping
users perform best in real-world settings. Figure 2 shows the
module wise performance comparison.

Fig. 2: Performance Metrics Results
The comparison Table I delineates the efficacy of several
AI-driven mock interview systems utilizing NLP. The Pro-
posed Hybrid Model attains superior performance, exhibiting
96% answer quality, 93% feedback accuracy (FA), and 95%
user satisfaction (US), owing to its integration of BERT’s
contextual comprehension and GPT’s dynamic, human-like
response creation. The GPT-Based System achieves a response
quality (RQ) of 93%, but its computational complexity results
in marginally slower performance. The BERT-based system
achieves a compromise between contextual comprehension
and adaptation; however, it is inferior to hybrid models.
Rule-based systems, governed by preset regulations, exhibit
constraints in both flexibility and precision. The Transformer-
Based Approach offers robust generalization but is deficient in
domain-specific optimization. This illustrates the advantages
of hybrid models in providing efficient and customized mock
interview experiences.
TABLE I: Comparison of AI-Based Mock Interview Systems
Using NLP
Model RQ (%) FA (%) US (%) ET (ms)
Rule-Based NLP 72 68 65 300
BERT-Based Interview 89 85 88 500
GPT-Based 93 90 92 550
Transformer-Based 90 87 89 480
Proposed Hybrid Model 96 93 95 450
Table II delineates different models and their principal
characteristics for an AI-driven mock interview system. The
Rule-Based NLP paradigm employs established rules but
is devoid of adaptability. The BERT-Based Model provides
contextual comprehension and flexibility in addressing vari-
ous enquiries. The GPT-Based Model facilitates human-like,
dynamic interactions, whereas the Transformer-Based Model
guarantees robust generalization but is deficient in domain-
specific optimization. The Proposed Hybrid Model, which
integrates BERT and GPT with domain-specific optimization,
is distinguished by its exceptional adaptability and accuracy,
rendering it suitable for customized mock interview situations.

The convergence curve in Figure 3 depicts the performance

Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:30 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

TABLE II: Comparison of Models/Approaches and Their Key Features
Model/Approach Key Features
Rule-Based NLP Predefined rules, limited flexibility
BERT-Based Interview Contextual understanding, adaptable to various questions
GPT-Based Human-like responses, dynamic interactions
Transformer-Based Strong generalization capabilities but less domain-specific optimization
Proposed Hybrid Model Combines BERT and GPT capabilities with domain-specific tuning
and constrained enhancement owing to its inflexibility and de-
pendence on established rules. The BERT-based methodology
attains superior performance via contextual comprehension
and adaptation to varied inquiries. The GPT-based model
demonstrates accelerated and substantial improvements by
utilizing dynamic interactions and human-like reactions. The
Transformer-Based model exhibits robust generalization but
reaches a plateau sooner due to insufficient domain-specific
optimization. The Proposed Hybrid Model attains optimal
accuracy by integrating the capabilities of BERT and GPT
with domain-specific adjustments, hence providing enhanced
adaptability, precision, and scalability.
V. C ONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK
The paper examines an innovative artificial intelligence-
based mock interview system that emulates conventional mock
interview techniques while addressing their limitations. The
system offers comprehensive feedback on both verbal and
nonverbal communication abilities and utilize advanced tech-
nology to replicate a genuine in-person interview experience.
It evaluates verbal fluency, coherence, subject matter expertise,
facial expressions, posture, and eye contact in real time, offer-
ing tailored feedback as opposed to conventional methods. Its
adaptability enables the customization of interview conditions
according to job responsibilities and complexity, incorporating
domain-specific enquiries for IT, management, and various
other fields. RL enhances user confidence via continuous
evaluation and feedback mechanisms; nevertheless, behavioral
analysis utilizing computer vision techniques augments pro-
fessionalism. The system is suitable for a diverse array of
users because to its scalability, availability, and utilisation of
TTS feedback. It establishes a novel benchmark in career
development tools for job searchers with the integration of
gamification and multilingual features.
The system possesses potential for enhancement despite its
success and benefits. Future endeavours can be categorized un-
der three distinct classifications: Multilingual support would
enable non-native English speakers to utilize the system. This
capability could assist firms and international job seekers by
fulfilling their linguistic and cultural requirements. Industry-
specific datasets enhance NLP models, enabling them to
generate more relevant inquiries. This transition will enhance
the system’s capacity to prepare consumers for employment
in technology, finance, and the humanities. Gamification
instruments like as rewards, progress monitoring, and grading
can enhance user engagement and motivation. Users will strive
for more consistency and improved performance on their
tasks, hence augmenting the learning process. Enhancing the
flexibility and inclusivity of the AI-Based Mock Interview
System would facilitate user engagement in a dynamic and
competitive job market.

## [7] AI Powered Virtual Job Interview Simulator Using Natural Language Processing
- **Authors:** Neha Raj, Varchasvini, Navin Prakash
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `AI_Powered_Virtual_Job_Interview_Simulator_Using_Natural_Language_Processing.pdf`

AI Powered Virtual Job Interview Simulator using 
Natural Language Processing  
G ShivajiRao

Abstract— Interviews are a crucial gateway to employment in 
today's highly competitive job market. However, many job 
seekers face challenges due to inadequate preparation resulting 
from limited access to realistic and tailored practice sessions. 
Traditional mock interviews with care er counselors or peers 
often lack constructive feedback and fail to replicate actual 
interview dynamics. The AI -Driven Virtual Job Interview 
Simulator addresses this gap by utilizing advanced technologies, 
particularly Natural Language Processing (NLP), to  create an 
interactive, real -time interview practice platform. This 
simulator generates role -specific questions aligned with the 
user’s desired career path and evaluates responses in real -time 
through sentiment analysis and coherence assessments. It offers 
constructive, personalized feedback that highlights both 
strengths and areas for improvement. The adaptive learning 
design of the simulator continuously adjusts question difficulty 
based on user performance, ensuring a seamless progression. 
Over time, it learns from individual user behaviors to enhance 
its feedback, thereby making the experience increasingly 
personalized and effective. With a user -friendly interface, the 
system requires no technical proficiency for operation. By 
combining practicality with  cutting-edge AI technology, this 
innovative tool empowers users by instilling the confidence and 
skills necessary to excel in interviews, effectively bridging the 
gap between preparation and employment. The simulator 
represents a transformative solution f or career preparedness, 
revolutionizing the interview preparation process. 
 
Keywords-Virtual Job Interview, Natural Language Processing 
(NLP), Real -time Interview Feedback, Role -Specific Interview 
Questions, AI-Driven Candidate Evaluation 
I. INTRODUCTION  
Today, the job search process in the fast-paced job market has 
become more difficult and competitive. The interview is a 
critical part of the process; during the interview, applicants 
need to communicate their qualifications, experience, and 
appropriateness for a particular position in an efficient 
manner. Yet, many job applicants find this phase challenging 
because they are unable to access quality resources, 
constructive feedback, and hands-on tools that are specifically 
designed for interview practice. T raditional methods such as 
role-playing with co -workers and attending career guidance 
courses do not provide a realistic, interactive, and dynamic 
practice session. The absence of proper provision for this 
leaves potential for developing new solutions that  use 
technology to give job applicants a better chance in actual 
interviews. 
Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Natural Language 
Processing (NLP) are the recent renaissance in which lies the 
hope of transforming interview preparations out of oblivion. 
Real-world interview scenarios can be simulated, answers can 
be evaluated, and comprehensive feedback can be offered by 
an AI -based system. Role -based questions and adaptive 
learning features can be incorporated into such systems so that 
the diverse needs of job applicants are addressed. Such 
platforms offer an all-rounded training experience by allowing 
them to evaluate verbal, textual, and non -verbal responses, 
which bridges the gap between traditional methods and the 
needs of modern recruitment techniques. 
 
To solve these problems, the 'AI -Powered Virtual Job 
Interview Simulator' has been created to give users a realistic 
and interactive experience of interview preparation. The 
method is based on advanced NLP techniques, which analyze 
user responses in real time and provide feedback on how clear 
the communication is, how confident the user is about what he 
or she is saying, and whether the emotional tone is 
appropriate. The system adjusts interview questions 
dynamically according to the user’s profession and c areer 
goals, making practice sessions with the system more 
meaningful and effective. The simulator also applies 
sentiment analysis and machine learning algorithms to 
evaluate user performance and adjust the difficulty level of

subsequent questions, thus forming a learning curve for 
continuous enhancement. 
 
Furthermore, the simulator can be used in educational 
institutions and organizations beyond personal preparation. It 
can be utilized in career development programs to help 
students and professionals develop their skills in real 
situations. In addition, the system is scalable and accessible, 
making it a perfect tool for a wide audience, regardless of 
technical knowledge. In this introduction, we provide context 
for how we describe the sys tem’s design, methodology, and 
the paradigm-breaking impact such a system has on interview 
preparation, as well as how this system is building the future 
of professional skill development. 
 
 
Figure 1: Components of an AI-Powered Interview Simulator 
II. LITERATURE REVIEW 
In the study [1] an AI based virtual reality system for training 
child protection service (CPS) personnel in conducting 
investigative interviews with children who are victims of 
abuse is discussed. The authors developed an interactive 
system that allowed u sers to interview a simulated child 
avatar through dialogue model, visual avatars, and virtual 
reality (VR). It is a system that integrates a language module 
through Rasa framework and speech to text and text to speech 
functions provided by IBM Watson. The y include the ability 
to create a realistic and immersive training experience 
without human actors, which makes the approach cost 
effective and possible to train frequently. Also, user studies 
showed that the system greatly enhanced CPS professionals’ 
communication skills. Nevertheless, there were some 
limitations such as needing a more responsive system, and the 
difficulty of including note taking within VR sessions which 
would improve the training experience. 
On this platform, CIRVR, the study [2] concerns the use of a 
virtual reality platform to enhance the job interview skills of 
autistic individuals. The platform combines speech, eye gaze, 
facial expressions, and physiological responses to measure 
emotional and behavioral state of the interview participants. 
A visualization tool called the Dashboard gives valuable 
feedback to job coaches and interviewees on their 
performance, and how to improve their responses, as the 
system goes. Overall, the system has the merit of providing a 
comprehensive data set that can provide insights into an 
autistic individual’s stress levels, eye contact, emotional 
responses, which can be used to tailor interview training. One 
such truncation is the reliance of the system on extern al 
sensors to provide physiological data, as well as the need for 
better facial expression recognition models for use on autistic 
individuals. Furthermore, the platform would be better served 
with more explicit explanations to the users of the 
visualizations offered by the Dashboard. 
The research [3] describes a virtual reality (VR) based job 
interview training platform that assists autistic people in the 
practice and refinement of interviewing skills. Incorporating 
AI Based Real Time Stress Detection with Eye Gaze 
Monitoring and Emoti on Detection, the CIRVR system 
enables a unique and adaptive interview experience in which 
an interview will be tailored to suit their stress. It also 
includes a dashboard where job coaches and employers can 
view the interview data of the candidate to unde rstand the 
performance better. The merits of CIRVR lie in the fact that 
it can help create a structured, controlled and anxious free 
environment for autistic individuals to practice job 
interviews, thereby increasing confidence and performance. 
In addition , it provides objective data driven feedback to 
adjust interview techniques based on data that can enable job 
coaches and employers. Nevertheless, the system is limited 
in the ability to accurately decipher autistic individuals' facial 
expressions and emotions, require further AI development for 
handling bidirectional conversation, and requires external 
sensors for stress and gaze, which may not be practical for 
real world applications. 
This [4] is about how AI affects the recruitment process in 
Hanoi, Vietnam. The authors developed and ran surveys with 
150 HR professionals as well as in depth interviews with 
managers from 20 companies using a mixed methods 
approach. The study shows that AI can be used to enhance 
candidate screening, job matching and saving hiring time. 
The merits of the study include the fact that it looks at real 
world AI employments in Vietnam’s HR sector with 
efficiency gains and improvement in decision making 
through AI enabled tools. Nevertheless, the paper also 
outlines the key limitations (e.g. necessary AI skills, ability 
for digital technology to displace jobs, the tension between 
automation and human judgment). This supports the strategic 
AI’s use, upskilling the  workforce and ethics guidelines for 
the best use of AI in HR recruitment. 
In the study [5], it considers the role of postural interaction in 
virtual job interview scenarios. To achieve this, the authors 
created a virtual agent that adapts its postural behavior to the 
movements of the user based on a motion captured library of 
interviewer postures. Using a force platform and video 
recordings, the system was tested on 32 participants and their 
postural control and stress perception, as well as interaction 
outcomes were analyzed. One merit of the study is that it 
provides a realisti c simulation of job interviews which 
Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI-2025)
IEEE Xplore Part Number: CFP25J32-ART; ISBN: 979-8-3315-4460-7

Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:01 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

enables users to train in a structured and interactive 
environment. The study also determined that mirroring 
postural behavior increases warmth perception, especially in 
female virtual interviewers. While these limitations include 
the virtual interviewer has no facial expressions so not much 
natural engagement, and the difficulty of completely 
reproducing real human interviews with stress and difficulty, 
this can lead to more efficient and error free interviews.  
In the work [6], an AI powered interview system is designed 
to increase fairness, efficiency and reliability in remote 
hiring. In order to assess job candidates through facial 
expressions, vocal patterns, verbal responses and 
physiological signals, the aut hors used deep learning 
techniques trained on 400,000 interview video datasets. The 
system was tested in five major public enterprises in Korea 
and the evaluation fairness and job fitness was 85. In 
particular, this AI based interview system would be 
meritorious in terms of improved objectivity, reduced hiring 
bias, and cost efficiency on automated initial interview 
screening. Furthermore, the system gives assessment of 
emotional states and competencies of candidates in real time, 
ensuring that the evaluati on process is structured. However 
the limitations include ethical considerations, bias in the 
making of decisions in the AI, and need for accurate 
understanding of the humans feelings and non verbal cues.  
This research [7] describes a virtual robot based training 
program to improve job interview skills of individuals with 
autism spectrum disorders (ASD). To implement this 
approach, authors used a virtual robot to simulate interview 
scenarios with a group of  participants who would take turns 
as interviewees, interviewers, and evaluators. This program 
was evaluated in terms of effectiveness using pre and post 
training assessments with human interviewers. This approach 
has many merits including the improvement of self 
confidence, motivation, and understanding of interview 
perspectives in ASD individuals. The training resulted in 
large improvements in verbal and non verbal communication, 
for example appropriate use of words, speaking calmly and 
eye contact. But the study is limited, with a small sample size, 
gender imbalance and that it has no control group to 
reference.  
An exploration of the integration of natural language 
processing in robotic systems using virtual assistants is done 
in the work [8]. The authors introduce a systemic conceptual 
model that enables the improvement of the human -robot 
interaction by embedding  semantic modeling and structured 
dialogue management in natural language processing. An 
experimental application in Greek language processing is 
included, to test the effectiveness of the study. The most 
notable features of this study are: comprehensive r eview of 
virtual assistant evolution, an innovative systemic approach 
of natural language processing and potential to enhance 
robotic communication efficiency. However, these 
limitations include limited insights into how natural language 
processing is carried out by commercial virtual assistants and 
limited scope of application because only a small natural 
language dataset was used.. 
Indian companies adopt artificial intelligence in recruitment 
processes, which is explored in the research [9]. The authors 
adopted a thematic analysis approach to collecting primary 
data from semi structured interviews with HR professionals 
from IT compan ies. It studies how AI is being plugged in 
different parts of recruitment – from sourcing candidates, 
screening, assessment, onboarding, and enhancing 
engagement of candidates through AI assisted chatbots. 
Similarly, AI in recruitment bears merits such as improved 
efficiency, automation of repetitive tasks, better job -
candidate matching, and cost reduction. AI also helps in data 
driven decision making as well as making recruitment 
process smoother. However, the limitations are that there are 
challenges in b ig data processing, there can be some bias 
caused by AI screening, there is difficulty in hiring to senior 
roles, and the high cost of implementing AI infrastructure.  
In the research [10], the effect of virtual reality based job 
interview simulations on anxiety, experience and 
performance of interviewees is investigated. An orthogonal 
experiment with five key variables of realism, question type, 
interviewer attitude, ti ming and preparation, was conducted 
by the authors, and electrodermal activity and self assessment 
questionnaires were used to assess anxiety levels. 
Professional interview questions had by far the greatest effect 
on anxiety, realism, preparation and the a ttitudes of the 
interviewer had a moderate effect, and timing had little 
impact. Nevertheless, the sample is limited to 19 participants, 
there are no diverse job sectors, and there is limited 
generalizability to the broader population 
In the study [11] we investigate how NLP can help to advance 
the AI driven recruitment process. These advanced NLP 
models of BERT and GPT are used in the study for 
automation of resume screening, candidate ranking, and 
chatbot driven conversation with the candidates. It also 
presents an NLP based interview simulation tool that 
compares the candidate suitability using semantic analysis 
and structured interactions. It has the merits of a better 
recruiting efficiency, less hiring bias, and better engagement 
of candidates with AI powered chatbots and automated 
interviews. However, there are some limitations regarding 
algorithm transparency, biases in screening via AI, and the 
cost of training large NLP models. 
In the work [12], recruitment and organizational resources are 
investigated in relation to the role of AI in the adoption of AI 
for competitive advantage. It is a quantitative study relying 
on a cross sectional approach with partial least squares 
structural equation modelling (PLS -SEM) on data from 290 
human resource professionals. Using the resource based view 
and dynamic capability framework, it applies AI to 
recruitment process. Strengths of the study include the 
strategic insights for the HR professiona l provided by the 
study as to the way AI integration increases recruitment 
efficiency, improves decision making and stimulates 
innovation. Furthermore, it emphasizes that HR 
competencies, open innovation, and financial support as well 
as IT infrastructure are critical for deploying AI. Limitation 
of the study includes the cross sectional nature which gives 
only a snapshot of the current AI adoption trends and the
