requirement of continuous human intervention in order to 
ensure ethical use of AI.  
In the study [13], artificial intelligence is applied in 
automating different stages in recruitment. The introduction 
of the study includes an AI based system in the integration of 
natural language processing, machine learning and cloud 
computing to simplify task like resume screening, candidate 
evaluation and personality assessments. For technical 
interviews and personality insights, the system uses IBM 
Watson services for chatbot based technical interviews and 
personality insights to improve on hiring eff iciency. Other 
merits of this approach include decreased reduction time, 
reduced human bias and better candidate job matching 
through automated screening. It also makes hiring decision 
more data driven and objective with the help of the AI system. 
However, limitations include the potential bias from 
algorithm, dependency on high quality training data, and 
difficulty in evaluating the soft skills and human interactions 
properly.  
Further, the work [14] examines the use of AI to improve the 
process of recruitment decision making. The study uses a 
mixed method approach consisting of a literature review and 
a small scale survey of HR professionals from IT and 
manufacturing sectors. To  analyze the AI adoption trends in 
recruitment, the research uses the Smart PLS tool to study 
assisted intelligence for routine tasks and automation 
intelligence for complete decision making. Among the merits 
of this study are insights on AI based recruitm ent strategies, 
and the ways in which AI makes job postings, applicant 
screening, and interview scheduling more efficient. The 
research also highlights that there should be an equilibrium 
between the automation of AI and human intervention. 
However, limits  of the study include the small number of 
respondents (50) and the focus within the industry.  
In the research lead [15], the use of AI powered chatbots in 
improving the recruitment process is explored. It is based on 
literature reviews, journal articles and industry reports to 
study in detail how chatbots help to engage candidates, 
streamline hiring and overcome recruitment issues. The paper 
presents the possible use of AI based chatbots to do resume 
screening and matching, and to automate the interaction with 
the candidate. The advantages of AI chatbots in recruitment 
include that they can simplify  initial screening, increase 
candidate experience through real time communication and 
reduce repetitive tasks that increase efficiency. In addition, 
they also help organizations have a strong employer brand 
and automate scheduling and onboarding. Nevertheless, these 
limitations restrict the ability to fully replace human 
judgment, the complexity of handling query candidate 
questions, and in automated decision making, there may be 
biases in chatbot driven decisions.  
III. METHODOLOGY 
 The AI -Powered Virtual Job Interview Simulator 
architecture is built upon the systematic design of a 
heterogeneous processes and technologies based interaction, 
efficiency, and personalized interview practice tool. It takes 
an approach of using Natural Lan guage Processing (NLP), 
Machine Learning and user experience based principles to 
simulate real interview environments and give useful 
feedback. The sections of the approach can be considered to 
progress through a series of phases, where at each phase the 
user experience will be improved, and the accuracy of the 
system will be increased. 
 
[1]Requirement Analysis and Planning 
First, it was about identifying the most important 
problems during an interview that the job applicants have to 
tackle, like the absence of personalized feedback and the 
absence of realistic practice scenario. Taking these 
observations as a base, the syste m was designed to address 
these limitations by the introduction of various features 
including real time response analysis, role based question 
generation and in depth feedback. Additionally, this phase 
established the technical requirements including the NLP for 
text analysis, sentiment analysis and adaptability over 
different job domains. On the scale, it had a modular 
architecture to support scalability, and could allow the 
integration of other features in the future like non -verbal cue 
analysis and multilingual support. 
[2] Data Collection and Preparation 
Maintenance of accuracy and relevance of the system 
was supported by data preparation. A robust question pool was 
created that included behavioral, situational and technical 
interview situations across different sectors. In order to train 
NLP models on testing relevance, coherence and tone, sample 
responses were developed. To ensure quality input, pre 
processing methods such as tokenization, stop word removal 
and lemmatization were applied on standard inputs. 
Moreover, a job industry specific word dictionary was created 
by the system for recognizing the most important words 
during the response analysis. Accurate question generation 
and response analysis is based on structured dataset.  
[3]Question Generation 
The question generation module of the simulator 
generates personalized interview questions according to 
different positions and industries. The system uses pre trained 
NLP models such as GPT or BERT to analyze job 
specification and industry expectation to generate questions to 
find out what skills and capabilities are required for a position. 
Technical, behavioral and situational questions are used to 
classify questions to test for skills in a thorough manner. AI 
text generation is more flexible than preconfigured templates, 
and they react with variation and realism in questions. The 
hybrid mechanism makes sure that users go through the 
challenges they will encounter in real interviews, and 
therefore, they are prepared. 
[4]User Input and Response Processing 
Text or voice inputs are collected allowing typing and 
oral interaction with the simulator. Speech to text software 
such as Google Speech to Text is used to translate voice inputs 
into text for text based analysis. They are normalized text 
inputs with grammar check, tokenization, ready for analysis. 
Later releases capture the nonverbal inputs such as gestures 
and facial expressions using OpenCV and MediaPipe and 
infuses reality to the simulation. This module ensures that the 
system can accept different kind s of inputs, yet remain user 
friendly.

[5]Response Analysis 
What makes the simulator powerful is that it processes 
user responses through highly complex NLP techniques. 
Semantic analysis verifies whether the answer makes sense as 
an answer to the given question and sentiment analysis 
determines the degree of confidence, enthusiasm or hesitance 
with which that answer was given. This matches keywords to 
ensure that occurrence of job related terminologies does exist, 
thus confirming the user’s domain knowledge. The responses 
are graded based on preset parameters like clarity, 
completeness measure and similarity to target respon se. The 
analysis of this type covers the entire information, from the 
technical to the soft. 
[6]Feedback Generation 
The feedback module provides users with concrete 
feedback on performance. Clear expression or domain 
knowledge is indicated as a strength while a weakness of lack 
of structure or confidence is flagged. In the case of the 
indicators of performance, feedback  is provided in both 
textual and graphical modes like scorecards, heatmaps and 
charts, to make the indicators of performance clear to users. It 
provides practical advice for users to amend the way they 
communicate content and style. Finally, this module 
guarantees that users get good feedback to improve their 
interview skills well.  
[7]Adaptive Learning Mechanism 
The system has a mechanism for adaptive learning, in 
order to facilitate an adaptive and tailored learning process. 
Various sessions are tracked and the weaker parts of the 
performance are focused in later simulations. Questions are 
dynamically altered to be of difficulty level with respect to 
user progress so that the challenge and achievability balance 
is preserved. The system keeps track of the improvement 
paths, motivating the users and making them feel satisfied 
through a sense of achievement and of de velopment. The 
simulator is highly beneficial as a practice aid in interviews 
due to the adaptive mechanism, which ensures that the 
simulator continues to improve. 
 
 
 
Figure 2: Platforms Referred 
 
IV. IMPLEMENTATION 
A. User Interface 
In the system, the site of interaction initiation is the user 
interface where the users interact with the software and initiate 
the simulation. It gives us a way to present the feedback to the 
user while entering responses within it. With careful 
consideration of the interface, the interface is  planned to be 
simple and usable to the users with different levels of technical 
know how. The functional concern of this system is to ease of 
use of underlying AI module interaction for seamless 
integration of all operations with the user in mind. 
B. Input Processor 
 The user input to system analysis is managed by the 
input processor. It applies Speech to Text (STT) technology to 
convert words to text for voice input. This makes it more 
convenient as the system can take text as well as voice input. 
In addition, the Nat ural Language Processing (NLP) 
algorithms used by it analyse the meaning and context of the 
user input to do accurate analysis. The module cleans user 
input, particularly voice data, of noise and makes them clearer 
before analysis. It acts as an input qua lity layer to the AI 
modules, used as the initial interaction layer with the AI 
modules. The input processor processes the data by pre 
conditioning them for processing, thus keeping the data 
integrity in the simulation pipeline. 
C. Question Generator 
 A question generator is an interactive tool which using 
ML and NLP gives custom interview questions. It decides the 
questions based on user’s profession, industry or skill set for 
a mock interview. This system gives the industry specific 
access to data and  matches trends that are appropriate 
preparation for actual situations.  The system helps to think 
critically through personalized questions. This matches the 
approach to interactive learning that aligns question difficulty 
to a user’s level of performance  to maximize learning and 
challenge. This feature helps in improvement and to build user 
confidence in answering questions that are diverse and 
relevant.  
D. Response Evaluator 
The response evaluator analyzes the user’s answers using 
methods like reinforcement learning to score how relevant, 
clear and of high quality it is. An evaluation of linguistic 
content and contextual relevance of answers is done in this 
module. The feedback from this module is to improve verbal 
communication skills such as coherence, vocabulary and tone, 
which are needed in real world interviews. 
E. Behavior Analyzer 
 In addition to analyzing the user’s body language, 
gestures, and expression of facial expressions, the behavior 
analyzer goes on to examine the user’s voice modulation. It 
uses such sophisticated software as MediaPipe and OpenCV 
to find patterns and sugges t what to focus on — in delivery 
and body language — to improve. This is the module that will 
help you with a full appraisal as non -verbal messages are 
usually the deciding factor as to whether you succeed or fail 
in an interview.

F. Feedback Generator 
The response evaluator and behavior analyzer are 
combined into the feedback generator which provides detailed 
performance feedback. This feedback is also very 
comprehensive covering fluency, job knowledge and body 
language and voice communication skills. This feedback can 
be taken action on by users, who can use the information to 
make improvements. 
It also provides suggestions for improvements as 
feedback generator based on the performance of the user. For 
instance, it advocates for some areas of practice and body and 
tone correction. This user specific advice helps the users get 
better in certain areas and increase the level of confidence and 
readiness for real interviews. 
G. Text-to-Speech Module 
Text to Speech (TTS) module interprets feedback and 
plays it back to the user as audio, making this feedback 
interactive and immersive way for the user to see their 
performance. It is helpful to users who learn better with sound, 
and it mimics the conversa tional nature of real interviews; 
offering listening feedback. This method allows learning to be 
fun and your information to be absorbed better. 
Beside providing feedback, the TTS module has also an 
accessibility support. This feature gives accessibility to the 
simulator for visually challenged or dyslexic readers. Text 
based and audio feedback integration ensures a fair experience 
for the user with no interruptions in learning or practicing. 
 
H. Feedback Loop 
All this is held together by the feedback loop, the core of the 
system which ensures that the assessment happens iteratively 
with respect to the user performance and the quality of the 
overall experience is tweaked accordingly. Parts of the loop 
can look like this, so they link all of the modules in order to 
allow for immediate adjustments to question difficulty as well 
as evaluation criteria. To create an adaptive learning 
environment, feedback loop is important, as it provides steady 
progress and skill development of the users over time.

V. RESULT AND DISCUSSION 
The User Login/Sign Up method for the AI -Based 
Virtual Job Interviewer begins with the User being given the 
option to sign up for a new account or to log in to their existing 
account. This is the first step to secure access and creating 
one’s own account t hat will monitor the user progress and 
performance in the long run. Then, in Profile Setup users feed 
them in their educational background, work experience, skills 
and aims in career. This aggregation of this information helps 
personalize the interview process, making it as specific to the 
individual user’s specific goals and requirements. If this 
information is gathered, the system would personalize the 
experience and prepare for job oriented interview simulations. 
  
Then, the Users are given the means to define the 
boundaries of their practice session in the Interview Setup 
stage by choosing among the job position, the industry, and 
the type of interview (behavioral, technical, or situational). At 
this level of personalization, the simulation is highly relevant 
to the individual users because it is a replica of real job 
requirements. After which, the information provided is used 
to generate personal questions for the AI Interview 
Preparation module through advanced Nat ural Language 
Processing (NLP) methodologies. It enables the system to be 
used to generate dynamic and specific to industry question 
sets, providing for a highly realistic and interactive interview. 
Preparing questions is followed by going through the 
Interview Simulation in which the participants respond to the 
prepared questions by both textual or audio responses. Each 
one of such responses is checked by the AI Evaluation of 
Responses component that  is checking based on semantic 
appropriateness, grammatical correctness, emotional nuance 
and presence of some keywords relative to the domain. Once 
the system is done, it will then allow Feedback Generation to 
offer elaborate feedback about the performanc e of users by 
focusing on the good parts and pointing out the bad parts. Such 
feedback, along with practical suggestions, allows users to 
tune themselves and get ready to give a good performance in 
the real world interviews.

Figure 8: Chat with AI For Interview 
 
As seen in Figure 3, the process goes smoothly from profile 
creation to AI-based interview interactions, with performance 
measurement and feedback at the end. The system utilizes 
Natural Language Processing (NLP) to understand user 
responses and provide real -time, impartial evaluations based 
on set criteria. Figure 4 illustrat es a comparison between the 
traditional manual systems and the suggested AI -based 
solution. The findings reveal that the AI system outperforms 
in terms of response precision, processing performance, user 
satisfaction, and the quality of assessment. This improvement 
highlights the ability of the system to improve decision -
making in hiring while minimizing human intervention. 
 
User interface elements are inherent to this system's 
functionality and user experience. Figure 5 illustrates the login 
page, permitting secure entry using user authentication so as 
to secure data privacy and managed entry. Upon login, users 
are navigated to an individualized dashboard, as represented 
in Figure 6, whereby they can monitor interview progress, 
access performance indicators, and view feedback reports. 
In addition to improving the user experience, the AI 
provides an introduction to the interview in the form of an 
orientation, which is clear and brief and clearly sets 
expectations and directs the candidate through the process. 
It is illustrated in Figure 7. Throughout the interview 
session, the system uses a conversational interface, as 
illustrated in Figure 8, by requesting pertinent questions 
and assessing answers in real time. This method not only 
imitates a human interviewer but also ensures justice an d 
impartiality across all the applicants, hence adding more 
weight and authenticity to the hiring process. 
 
VI. CONCLUSION 
 This AI-Based Virtual Job Interviewer is aimed at 
changing the way job interviews are conducted. It promotes 
the utilization of cutting -edge technologies such as Natural 
Language Processing (NLP) and speech-to-text analysis. The 
difference from conventiona l techniques that use generic 
questions is that this AI provides real -time feedback that is 
specific to the user, simulating an actual interview 
environment. Through this interaction process, it is prepared 
to better meet the recruitment opportunities of the 
contemporary era. One of the key aspects is in-depth feedback. 
It evaluates responses for content relevance, technical 
correctness, emotional tone, and language proficiency, 
pointing out both strengths and weaknesses. It provides 
actionable feedback on how to enhance communication skills, 
boost confidence, and improve performance in the future. The 
flexibility of the system, along with the incremental learning 
process, ensures continual improvement and long -term 
advantages for users. 
The platform is scalable and flexible, making it suitable for 
users from various industries. Given these career aspirations, 
it supports the simulation of different types of interviews (e.g., 
behavioral vs. technical). Additionally, more interactivity and 
immersion are provided through multimodal input 
technologies, such as text and voice. It also supports training 
in non -verbal analysis, including facial expressions and 
gestures, making it realistic for preparation purposes. The AI-
Based Virtual Job Interviewer is a learning partner that helps 
you develop the skills and confidence needed in today’s 
competitive marke t. It combines the need for modern 
interviews with traditional preparation techniques through the 
integration of advanced AI technologies. It is versatile, 
customizable, and easy to use, making it a pathway to 
achieving dream careers. This platform has the  potential to 
become the norm for using AI to solve career problems, with 
continuous updates and innovations.

## [8] Automate Coach to Practice Conversations
- **Authors:** Hojae Lee, Sullam Jeoung, Yiting Shen, et al.
- **Year:** 2023
- **Filename:** `Automated_Coach_to_Practice_Conversations.pdf`

Automated Coach to Practice Conversations 
 
Mohammed (Ehsan) Hoque 
MIT Media Lab 
75 Amherst Street, 
Cambridge, MA, USA 
mehoque@media.mit.edu 
Rosalind W. Picard 
MIT Media Lab 
75 Amherst Street, 
Cambridge, MA, USA 
picard@media.mit.edu
 
 
Abstract— We present a real-time system including a 3D 
character that can converse, capture, analyze and interpret 
subtle and multidimensional human nonverbal behaviors for 
possible applications such as job interviews, public speaking, 
or even automated speech therapy. The system works in a 
personal computer and senses nonverbal data from video (i.e., 
facial expressions) and audio (i.e., speech recognition and 
prosody analysis) using a standard webcam. We contextualized 
the development and evaluation of our system as a training 
scenario for job interviews. Using user-centered design and 
iterations, we determine how the nonverbal data could be 
presented to the user in an intuitive and educational manner. 
We tested efficacy of the system in the context of job interviews 
with 90 MIT undergraduate students. Our results suggest that 
the participants who used our system to improve their 
interview skills were perceived to be better candidates by 
human judges. Participants reported that the most useful 
feature was being given feedback on their speaking rate, and 
overall they reported strong agreement that they would 
consider using this system again for self-reflection.  
 
Virtual agent; nonverbal behaviors; feedback; prosody; 
facial expressions.  
I.  INTRODUCTION 
We describe an automated system — My Automated 
Conversation coacH (MACH) — that interacts, captures, 
analyzes, and interprets human nonverbal behaviors in real-
time to help people explore and understand their subtle 
communicative behaviors system (Figure 1). MACH is 
designed to be standardized, repeatable, and to work 
autonomously on a standard personal computer. The 
evaluation of MACH is contextualized in a "job-interview" 
training scenario. In this context, we followed a user-
centered iterative design approach to define the interaction, 
and determine how to present the nonverbal parameters to 
the users using information visualization techniques. 
II. TECHNICAL SYSTEM  
The system consists of the following sensing modules, as 
shown in Figure 1.  
Nonverbal behavior synthesis: MACH has been developed 
on an existing life-like 3D character platform called 
Multimodal Affective Reactive Characters (MARC) [1].  It 
was designed to appear and behave human-like by adjusting  
Figure 1. The MACH system works in a regular laptop, which 
processes the audio and video inputs in real time. The processed data is 
used to generate the behaviors of the 3D character that interacts with 
and provides feedback to participants. 
its behaviors based on the interaction. This realism was 
achieved by integrating the following four components into 
the animation of the virtual coach: arm and posture 
movements, facial expressions, gaze behavior, and lip 
synchronization [2].     
Nonverbal Behavior Sensing: The sensing module consists 
of smiles, head gestures, speech recognition, pitch, loudness 
and pauses. Our decision of which nonverbal features we 
sense was determined by our ability to adequately recognize 
them using real-time automated computing. 
Facial Expression Processing:   From the face, we track 
smiles and head gestures (e.g., nods, shakes, tilts) in every 
frame. We used the Shore Framework  [3] to detect faces
2013 Humaine Association Conference on Affective Computing and Intelligent Interaction
978-0-7695-5048-0/13 $26.00 © 2013 IEEE
DOI 10.1109/ACII.2013.123
703
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:46 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

and facial features for recognizing smiles.  
 Head Nod and Shake Detection:  Detecting natural head 
nods and shakes in real-time is challenging because head 
movements can be subtle, small, or asymmetric. Our 
implementation is motivated by tracking the “between 
eyes” region, as described by Kawato and Ohya [4].  
Prosody Analysis: For prosody analysis, we automatically 
recognize pauses, loudness and pitch variation (e.g., how 
well one is modulating his/her voice), since these are 
useful for nonverbal expressivity assessment. We 
developed an API using low level signal processing 
algorithms from Praat [5], an open source speech 
processing toolkit, towards extracting prosodic features.    
Speech Recognition and forced alignment:  In order to 
perform word level prosody analysis, we use the Nuance 
SDK to first recognize the text, and then the forced 
aligner [6] to recognize the beginning and end of each 
word. 
Speaking rate: Speaking rate is calculated by calculating 
total number of syllables per minute during the entire 
interaction. 
Weak language: We want the interface to give feedback 
on the number of filler words (e.g., “like”, “basically”, 
“umm”, “totally” etc.) that the user produces. We worked 
with the OwnTheRoom Public Speaking Company [7]  to 
define the weak language dictionary. 
III. APPLICATION 
Understanding, analysis and interpretation of nonverbal 
behaviors are dependent on conversational context. To 
validate the system, we defined the interaction context as 
job interviews. We recruited 90 MIT undergraduate 
students for our study. Students who used MACH were 
perceived to be stronger candidates by human judges 
compared to the students in the control group. The judges 
scored videos from the interviews, blinded to the 
condition that the students were in.  More details on the 
experimental set up and results are available in [2]. A 
video demonstration of the system is available at: 
http://tinyurl.com/MIT-MACH  
ACKNOWLEDGMENT 
We would like to acknowledge Jean-Claude Martin 
and Matthieu Courgeon for allowing us to build our 
system on their Multimodal Affective Reactive Characters 
(MARC) [1] Platform. Special acknowledgements to 
Sumit Gogia and Alex Sugurel for helping with the UI 
design, and Eunice Lin, Nicole Pan, Kristi Tausk, Abra 
Shen, Tamanna Urmi for helping with the study. Bilge 
Mutlu provided valuable suggestions on the data analysis 
and experimental set up of our job interview experiment. 
We are grateful to Nuance communications, Samsung, and 
the Media Lab consortium for supporting our research.  
REFERENCES 
[1] M. Courgeon, S. Buisine, and J. Martin, “Impact of Expressive 
Wrinkles on Perception of a Virtual Character’s Facial Expressions 
of Emotions,” in Proceedings of the 9th International Conference on 
Intelligent Virtual Agents, 2009, pp. 201–214. 
[2] M. E. Hoque, M. Courgeon, J. Martin, B. Mutlu, and R. W. Picard, 
“MACH: My Automated Conversation coacH,” in International 
Joint Conference on Pervasive and Ubiquitous Computing 
(UbiComp 2013), 2013. 
[3] B. Froba and A. Ernst, “Face detection with the modified census 
transform,” in Sixth IEEE International Conference on Automatic 
Face and Gesture Recognition, 2004, pp. 91–96. 
[4] S. Kawato and J. Ohya, “Real-time detection of nodding and head-
shaking by directly detecting and tracking the ‘between-eyes’,” in 
Fourth IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and 
Gesture Recognition, 2000, pp. 40–45. 
[5] P. Boersma and D. Weenink, “Praat: doing phonetics by computer 
[Computer program].” [Online]. Available: www.praat.org. 
[Accessed: 21-Jun-2013]. 
[6] J. Yuan and M. Liberman, “Speaker identification on the SCOTUS 
corpus,” in Proceedings of Acoustics , 2008, vol. 123, no. 5, pp. 
5687–5690. 
[7] “OwnTheRoom.” [Online]. Available: owntheroom.com. [Accessed: 
21-Jun-2013].  
 
Figure 2.  The two forms of feedback provided by MACH. The summary feedback (left) captures the overall interaction. Participants can practice multiple 
rounds of interviews and compare their performance across sessions. The focused feedback (right) enables participants to watch their own video. As they watch 
the video, they also can see how their nonverbal behaviors, such as smiles, head movements, and intonation change over time.

## [9] Automated Interview Evaluation System Using RoBERTa Technology
- **Authors:** Deepali Vora, Vianna Dabre, Manav Shah, Dhwanil Shah, Tanay Nistane
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `Automated_Interview_Evaluation_System_Using_RoBERTa_Technology.pdf`

XXX-X-XXXX-XXXX-X/XX/$XX.00 ©20XX IEEE 
Automated Interview Evaluation System 
Using RoBERTa Technology 
 
1G. Sri Harsh                                   
Department of Information 
Technology, Vardhaman College of 
Engineering (Autonomous), 
Hyderabad, India. 
sriharshgampa5@gmail.com 
 
4* Saroja Kumar Rout 
Department of Information 
Technology, Vardhaman College of 
Engineering (Autonomous), 
Hyderabad, India.

2Y. Sai Sri Vivek 
Department of Information  
Technology, Vardhaman College of 
Engineering (Autonomous),  
Hyderabad, India.

5 S. Ranjith Reddy                                  
Department of Information   
Technology, Vardhaman College of 
Engineering (Autonomous), 
Hyderabad, India.

ranjithreddy1086@vardhaman.org 
3 Maneesha. P 
Department of Information

Hyderabad, India. 
maneeshapolasa55@gmail.com 
 
6 Bijaya Kumar Sethi  
Department of Computer Science & 
Engineering (Data Science), 
Vardhaman College of Engineering 
(Autonomous), Hyderabad, India.                        
ORCID- [0000-0002-59041-085X] 
sbijayakumar@gmail.com 
 
 
Abstract — This research paper delves into the realm of 
automated interview evaluation, employing state-of- the-art 
natural language processing models. The primary foc us lies in 
comparing the effectiveness of TF-IDF, BERT, RoBERT a, 
Jaccard similarity, and Word2Vec models in assessing interview 
responses. The paper commences by providing a comprehensive 
definition of automated interview evaluation, highl ighting the 
significance of efficient and unbiased candidate assessment. The 
proposed methodology involves the utilization of Ro BERTa, a 
robust transformer-based model, to analyze and score interview 
responses. Through meticulous experimentation and evaluation, 
the research scrutinizes the performance of each mo del, 
examining its ability to capture contextual nuances and semantic 
understanding. The final results reveal the superio r efficacy of 
RoBERTa over the other models, demonstrating its pr oficiency 
in evaluating interview responses and emphasizing i ts potential 
for enhancing automated hiring processes. This stud y 
contributes valuable insights into the evolving fie ld of natural 
language processing and automated recruitment systems. 
 
Keywords—Interview Automation, Smart Interviewing System, 
Multimodal Signal Analysis, Natural Language Proces sing, Deep 
Learning Applications.  
I.  INTRODUCTION  
The growing number of youth who are not in Eemploym ent, 
Eeducation, or Training (NEETs) is a significant pr oblem for 
Europe. Underdeveloped socio-emotional and interper sonal 
skills, including a lack of self-assurance and awareness of their 
abilities, are common among NEETs [1,2]. This affec ts how 
well they function in a variety of crucial scenarios, such as job 
interviews when they must persuade the interviewer that they 
would be a good match for the organization. Several European 
nations have created specialist inclusion centers to help young 
people find work by providing them with coaching fr om 
professionals to address this issue. One issue with this method 
is how time and money-consuming it is. In light of this, 
technologically advanced solutions offer themselves  as 
competitive and beneficial substitutes for the current methods 
of one-on-one tutoring. 
The purpose of the job interview is for the prospec tive 
employer to ascertain whether the candidate is qualified for the 
position. In a social context such as a job intervi ew, 
interviewers mostly base their assessments on social cues—the 
conscious or unconscious behaviors of the interview ee that 
carry particular meaning.  
The challenge facing HR managers today is to assemb le and 
choose a pool of exceptional candidates for the org anization. 
For a very long time, HR managers have chosen their  staff 
through interviews. The hiring of potential employe es is 
strengthened by the time and financial investment i n 
recruitment and training. A reliable and valid inte rview 
progression necessitates a performance analysis [ 3]. 
The field that these young people are most interest ed in is 
information technology. They will be searching for 
employment possibilities that align with their prof essional 
abilities and educational background after they gra duate. 
Future job markets would be more competitive as a r esult. 
Companies will be looking to hire the best candidates available 
in the labor market as the demand for positions ris es. 
Companies need to elevate their recruitment procedu res to a 
new level to achieve that. The majority of business es still 
conduct interviews using more human interaction traditionally. 
The issue is that because human interaction is involved, these 
conventional interviewing techniques frequently hav e issues. 
Additionally, because everything in a traditional interviewing 
process is managed and coordinated by a chosen grou p of 
individuals, traditional interviewing procedures typically take 
longer [4]. 
A behavioral event interview (BEI) is one method th at 
businesses can utilize to conduct competency assessments. BEI 
is a systematic interviewing technique that consist s of five 
steps: outlining the process, gathering details about job duties, 
gathering details about behavioral occurrences, inv estigating 
qualities needed for the position, and, at the end,  providing 
conclusions and summaries. Ordinary interviews are seen to be 
less helpful in forecasting candidates who would perform well 
than BEIs, which can provide extensive behavioral 
descriptions of a candidate's work, making them mor e 
2024 1st International Conference on Cognitive, Green and Ubiquitous Computing (IC-CGU) | 979-8-3503-6178-0/24/$31.00 ©2024 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/IC-CGU58078.2024.10530789
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:52 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

effective. BEI has certain drawbacks despite being a useful tool 
for assessing competencies. For instance, the inter view 
procedure is lengthier, necessitates the use of mor e licensed 
interviewers (trained specialists are currently in short supply), 
is expensive to arrange, and raises the possibility of interviewer 
bias in the evaluation. Concurrently, as firms and organizations 
become more aware of the benefits of conducting competency 
assessments, the necessity for doing so grows [5]. 
Developing AI and machine learning for HRM is a dif ficulty 
because, by data science standards, there are typic ally not 
many data sets. NLP, a subfield of AI, has been used in systems 
for conducting human interviews. To conduct intervi ews and 
provide results automatically, an interactive natur al language 
processing (NLP) interview bot system was created. NLP's 
capacity to automatically interpret and comprehend 
unstructured text data is one of its most important advantages 
[6]. 
There are numerous benefits to employing interview bots to 
conduct competency evaluations. During the intervie w 
process, potential workers or job seekers will have their skills 
evaluated by artificial intelligence (AI) bots that  are 
programmed to ask adaptive multilayer interview que stions 
and can determine the interviewee's initial compete ncy level 
based on their responses. Interview bots could potentially offer 
a fitting interface for millennials, who have a pre ference for 
interacting through sophisticated computer programs . 
Furthermore, interview bots will significantly help  stop the 
transmission of viruses through in-person interview processes 
during the pandemic, when in-person interviews shou ld be 
avoided or at least minimized. Furthermore, interview bots will 
enable businesses to lower interview expenses and e nhance 
their capacity for assessment [16]. 
II.  LITERATURE SURVEY  
The paper addresses the pressing and underexplored issue of 
automating the analysis and performance evaluation of 
interviewees during job interviews, offering a nove l 
computational framework. This framework leverages 
multimodal signals, including facial images and voi ce, to 
provide comprehensive feedback on interviewee performance. 
The interview footage is divided into audio and visual frames. 
Faces are then identified within the visual frames,  and 
emotions are assessed using methods like support ve ctor 
machine-based classification and histogram of orien ted 
gradients. The identified facial expressions encompass a range 
of emotions, including happiness, fear, sadness, ne utrality, 
surprise, disgust, and anger. Mel Frequency Cepstra l 
Coefficient feature extraction is used in parallel to process 
auditory cues, classifying fluency into three categories: fluent, 
non-fluent-pause, and non-fluent-stammer. When emotion and 
fluency assessments are combined, the result is a performance 
score that provides automatic ratings for interview behaviors, 
such as classifying interviewees as performing poor ly, 
moderately, or well. This approach shows promise in  
revolutionizing the evaluation of interviewees, enh ancing 
objectivity and efficiency in the recruitment process [7]. 
 
The Smart Interviewing System (SIS) described in th is paper 
is a testament to the transformative power of techn ology in 
streamlining the interview process for the benefit of 
interviewers and HR professionals, particularly those involved 
in technology-related recruitment. By harnessing Na tural 
Language Processing (NLP) and deep learning techniques, SIS 
offers a comprehensive solution that works with bot h spoken 
and written language patterns. It converts human language into 
machine-readable text inputs, which serve as the fo undation 
for automated interviews and evaluations. SIS goes a step 
further by rigorously assessing the accuracy of can didates' 
responses in both oral and written assessments, pre dicting 
scores for each answer using deep learning concepts . This 
automation significantly reduces the effort require d by 
interviewers to identify the most qualified candidates, leading 
to more accurate evaluations at every stage of the interview 
process. Developed with Python and ReactJS, SIS int roduces 
advanced evaluation parameters, uncovering potential human 
errors that could have far-reaching consequences fo r 
organizations. Consequently, this system enables 
organizations to identify top-tier talent more effi ciently than 
traditional methods, all while saving valuable time  and effort 
in the hiring process. SIS represents a compelling example of 
how technology-driven innovations are reshaping the  
landscape of talent acquisition and recruitment [8]. 
This paper delves into the assessment of non-native  speakers' 
speaking proficiency in an online dialogue task, em phasizing 
the importance of incorporating different modalitie s for a 
comprehensive evaluation. The study acknowledges th at 
language learners' conversational competence extends beyond 
speech content and prosody, encompassing vital visu al cues 
like facial expressions and eye gaze. While lexical and acoustic 
features have received substantial attention, the r esearch 
highlights a gap in the exploration of visual featu res. The 
authors develop an automated speaking proficiency s coring 
method that makes use of multi-modal elements to cl ose this 
gap. They gathered data from 210 Japanese English l anguage 
learners who participated in online video interview s and had 
their speaking abilities annotated. The research un dertakes a 
comparative examination of modality efficacy and pr oposes 
two ways to incorporate visual characteristics. The  results 
highlight how much better the end-to-end method usi ng deep 
neural networks is than hand-crafted features in te rms of 
obtaining a higher correlation with human rating. Furthermore, 
the study reveals that the order of effectiveness for modalities 
is lexical, acoustic, and then visual features, shedding light on 
valuable insights for enhancing automated speaking 
proficiency assessments [9]. 
The paper highlights the critical role of interview s in talent 
recruitment for assessing candidates' alignment wit h a 
company's corporate culture. While interviews typic ally 
involve the participation of third-party psychology experts and 
professionals, incurring substantial costs for organizations, the 
paper advocates for the adoption of automation thro ugh 
machine learning technology to streamline this proc ess. The 
focus of the study is on leveraging machine learnin g methods 
to evaluate job applicants' compatibility with corporate culture 
by analyzing interview transcripts. Three classific ation 
algorithms— naive Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and 
Support Vector Machines (SVM) —are compared for thei r 
effectiveness in this context. The findings demonst rate that 
SVM consistently outperforms the other two algorith ms, 
achieving an average accuracy rate of 86%, surpassi ng naive 
Bayes at 81% and KNN at 79%. This research underscores the 
potential of machine learning techniques in reducin g manual 
tasks and human involvement in talent recruitment w hile 
enhancing the accuracy of corporate culture fit ass essments, 
ultimately benefiting companies in their quest to find the most 
suitable candidates [10]. 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:52 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

 
The paper underscores the significance of Inference Engines as 
software tools capable of drawing conclusions from available 
data and initiating actions when certain activity patterns align 
with existing knowledge. These engines exhibit deci sion-
making capabilities rooted in their heuristics and learning 
mechanisms. The study emphasizes the potential of harnessing 
Inference Engines to create intelligent software sy stems that 
not only improve accuracy over time but also excel in handling 
intricate situations. Specifically, the paper focus es on the 
design and development of an application geared tow ards 
automating the HR interview process. Leveraging JES S, a 
popular Inference Engine, the application demonstra tes the 
formidable capacity of Inference Engines to meet the demands 
of complex logical requirements. This research underscores the 
transformative role of Inference Engines in streaml ining and 
enhancing various processes, highlighting their ada ptability 
and potential for creating intelligent systems that continuously 
evolve to address intricate challenges [11]. 
 
In addition to highlighting the importance of the hiring process 
for HRM, the paper lists several firms that still u se labor-
intensive, error-prone manual hiring practices. Acknowledging 
the possible limitations of this methodology, the r esearch 
suggests creating an automated hiring system. The goal of this 
system is to give job searchers a comprehensive pla tform to 
evaluate their skills, uncover skill trends that firms are looking 
for, and even automatically generate resumes. As su ch, its 
reach goes beyond standard recruitment responsibili ties. 
Because the technology automates processes like ski ll 
matching, resume shortlisting, and interview schedu ling, 
organizations can benefit from significant time and  cost 
savings. Pre-interview tests with background monito ring, 
automated scheduling, and online mock interviews ar e 
noteworthy features. The study describes the use of  machine 
learning algorithms and other technologies, such as  web 
scraping, to achieve these goals. This innovative a pproach 
promises to revolutionize the recruitment process, enhancing 
efficiency, accuracy, and the overall recruitment e xperience 
for both job seekers and employers [12]. 
 
This research presents a novel method for creating an 
immersive job interview simulation environment by u tilizing 
social signal processing techniques and virtual cha racter 
technologies. In this simulated environment, a virtual character 
takes on the role of a recruiter and demonstrates t he capacity 
to react to and modify its interactions in response to the user's 
activities. This adaptability relies on an automatic recognition 
component designed to detect social cues, encompassing both 
conscious and unconscious behavioral patterns relevant to job 
interviews. These pertinent social cues have been i dentified 
through a comprehensive knowledge elicitation study  
involving real job seekers. To assess the viability  of this 
approach and its effects on users, the paper reports the findings 
of two user studies. These studies provide valuable  insights 
into the feasibility of the proposed system and its  potential 
impact on users, shedding light on the potential be nefits of 
leveraging virtual character technology in job inte rview 
simulations [13]. 
 
This paper delves into a critical and evolving area of research, 
investigating the impact of non-verbal behavior on hiring 
decisions, a topic of great importance in the realm  of job 
interviews and recruitment. The study acknowledges that there 
may be benefits to learning how to prepare applican ts for job 
interviews and educating recruiters on the importan ce of 
nonverbal cues in these procedures. Recent advancem ents in 
tools for automatic social signal analysis, such as  facial 
expression detection and speech processing, along w ith 
machine learning methods, have accelerated research  in this 
domain. Nevertheless, the majority of this research has mostly 
relied on traits that have been hand-engineered, wh ich has 
limited the identification of significant social si gnals. On the 
other hand, deep learning techniques present a viab le way to 
find intricate patterns without requiring a lot of feature 
engineering. This article employs deep learning approaches to 
explore important nonverbal social signals in asynchronous job 
video interviews. The authors leverage a previously developed 
deep learning system designed to assess a candidate's liability 
based on responses to a sequence of interview quest ions, 
incorporating attention mechanisms to identify rele vant 
segments of an answer. The paper introduces a metho dology 
for automatically extracting attention slices, mome nts when 
attention significantly increases. This research co ntributes 
valuable insights into the role of non-verbal behavior in hiring 
decisions, offering potential avenues for improving  
recruitment processes and candidate training throug h deep 
learning methods and attention mechanisms [14]. 
 
This paper addresses the growing demand for automat ic 
semantic information indexing and retrieval systems, driven by 
the exponential growth of video content. In particu lar, the 
study focuses on the critical role of textual information within 
video sources for achieving high-level semantics. To leverage 
overlay text as a crucial source of information, th e research 
presents an approach for the automatic indexing and  retrieval 
of individuals in television news interview video s equences. 
The proposed methods are rooted in established prod uction 
rules of television news and take into account the temporal 
aspects of video sequences. The results demonstrate how well 
the suggested method works to realize an effective automatic 
indexing and retrieval system through tests done on  Korean 
television news footage. The significance of using overlay text 
to extract valuable information from video content is 
highlighted by this study, which will ultimately im prove the 
usability and accessibility of video archives, particularly when 
it comes to television news interviews [15]. 
 
This study addresses the crucial role of competency assessment 
interviews in Human Resource Management, highlighting the 
challenges posed by traditional interview processes , such as 
time and cost constraints and the need for face-to- face 
interactions, particularly in the context of the on going 
pandemic. To tackle these issues, the research pres ents the 
development of an interview bot employing artificia l 
intelligence technology, specifically designed to a ssess 
competencies using the Behavioral Event Interview ( BEI) 
method. This novel method examines a candidate's 
competency levels based on prior behavioral experiences while 
automating the interview process. There are two main stages in 
the development of the interview bot: data training  to create 
learning models for competency level determination,  and 
testing and evaluation to assess competency levels.  The 
findings show that the approach can reasonably accu rately 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:52 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

forecast an individual's competency levels. When co nducting 
online interviews and assisting with the assessment  center 
process, this interview bot proves to be a useful a nd 
trustworthy resource, especially when there is soci al and 
physical distance. It is given in Indonesian and gi ves 
participants freedom concerning scheduling and loca tion. 
Furthermore, it proves to be a cost-efficient alter native to 
traditional interviews employing the same BEI metho d, 
making it an attractive option, especially for millennials. This 
research exemplifies the potential of AI-driven interview bots 
in revolutionizing competency assessment processes,  making 
them more accessible, efficient, and adaptable to c hanging 
circumstances[16]. 
 
Depression is a pervasive and debilitating mental h ealth 
condition that is increasingly impacting individual s 
worldwide, leading to significant disruptions in we ll-being, 
and daily functioning, and profound personal, famil ial, and 
societal consequences. This pervasive condition not  only 
affects one's emotional state but also takes a toll  on physical 
health. The absence of objective clinical assessmen t methods 
for depression is a major obstacle in providing eff ective 
treatment to many individuals suffering from this condition. A 
rapidly growing area of research focuses on automat ic 
depression assessment using visual signals. Artific ially 
intelligent systems are being employed to simulate the 
cognitive processes of human beings, and recent stu dies have 
unveiled a strong link between mood fluctuations an d 
alterations in emotional attention. Analyzing audio  features 
holds promise as a valuable tool for identifying and monitoring 
depression throughout the disorder and recovery. Du ring low 
moods, human voices exhibit distinct characteristics compared 
to normal states. In response to this challenge, th e paper 
proposes the use of an artificially intelligent sys tem for the 
identification of depression based on audio cues, p roviding a 
potential breakthrough in the assessment and manage ment of 
this pervasive mental health condition [17]. 
 
This paper investigates the use of deep learning to  automate 
the scoring of candidates' open-ended answers to pr e-hire job 
selection questionnaires. Three algorithmic approac hes, a 
traditional bag of words (BoW), long short-term mem ory 
(LSTM) models, and robustly optimized bidirectional encoder 
representations from the transformers approach (RoB ERTa), 
were compared using text data from pre-employment v irtual 
assessment center exercises. The candidate assessme nt 
questions and human labels (subject matter experts (SME) 
assessments on job-relevant competencies) were deve loped 
using best practices in measurement and assessment, resulting 
in a rich set of data on which the algorithms could  be trained. 
The candidate textual responses were scored using the trained 
models according to the specified competencies, and  the 
degree of agreement with knowledgeable human raters  was 
evaluated [18]. 
 
The methodologies and uses of competency-based inte rviews 
are discussed in this thesis, along with an introdu ction to 
interview procedures. Then, a description of the technologies, 
libraries, and machine learning methods employed in  the 
Interviewer chatbot's architecture is given. Lastly , a detailed 
discussion of the outcomes of an evaluation study t hat used 
the transformer-based models BERT, DeBERTa, and 
ELECTRA models to the competency-based interview results 
of actual candidates is presented [19]. 
III.  PROPOSED METHOD  
This research proposes an automated interview evalu ation 
system that leverages state-of-the-art techniques, including 
Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) and speech-to-tex t (STT) 
capabilities, to provide a comprehensive evaluation  of 
interview responses. The system also incorporates u ser data 
storage functionalities to enable personalized feed back and 
analysis. 
 
The core of the system's evaluation functionality l ies in the 
application of an STS algorithm. STS algorithms measure the 
semantic similarity between pairs of text documents, allowing 
for the comparison of interview responses to pre-de fined 
criteria or benchmark responses. For our system, we  propose 
using STSB-RoBERTa-base, a variant of the RoBERTa model 
specifically designed for semantic textual similari ty tasks. 
STSB-RoBERTa-base has been fine-tuned on STS 
benchmarks, enabling a nuanced assessment of semant ic 
meanings between sentences. 
 
To address the challenge of handling audio recordin gs from 
interviews, the system integrates STT technology. S TT 
systems convert spoken language into machine-readab le text, 
allowing the system to process audio recordings and extract the 
spoken content of interviews. We propose employing a cloud-
based STT service, such as Google Cloud Speech-to-T ext, to 
handle the transcription process efficiently and accurately. 
 
To facilitate personalized feedback and analysis, t he system 
incorporates a user data storage mechanism. This me chanism 
allows the system to store information about individual users, 
such as their interview responses, evaluation score s, and 
feedback from interviewers. This data can be used t o provide 
tailored feedback to users, identify patterns in their responses, 
and track their performance over time. 
 
 
 
 
 
Fig.1.Block Diagram of the evaluation system 
 
Figure 1 illustrates the process of an automated in terview 
evaluation system that transcribes questions and an swers in 
real time, storing them directly in the database. If the interview 
questions are not completed, the loop repeats. 
 
A. Data Preprocessing 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:52 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

a) Text Normalization:  Involves converting text to a standard 
format, including tasks like lowercasing, removing 
punctuation, and handling abbreviations or acronyms to ensure 
consistency in the textual data. 
b) Data Cleaning:  Addressing issues like removing irrelevant 
information, handling misspellings, and eliminating  noise 
from the text data, ensuring a clean and standardiz ed dataset 
for further analysis. 
c) Tokenization:  Breaking down textual data into smaller 
components such as words or phrases, creating token s for 
analysis and processing. 
 
B. Speech-to-Text Conversion 
The Speech Recognition library in Python is a versa tile tool 
that simplifies speech-to-text conversion. It acts as a bridge to 
various speech recognition engines and APIs, enabli ng the 
conversion of spoken language into written text. Th is library 
offers flexible options for capturing audio from di fferent 
sources, supports multiple recognition engines like  Google 
Speech Recognition, Sphinx, and others, and provide s error 
handling for better reliability. It's cross-platfor m, well-
documented, and facilitates the integration of spee ch 
recognition capabilities into Python applications. 
 
C. Processing the Converted Text   
 Identifying Frequently Occurring Words (Bag of Words) 
Bag of Words Model: This entails numbering the frequency of 
each word in the corpus to create a dictionary and translate text 
into a numerical representation. Word occurrence an alysis in 
the interview responses is made easier by this model. 
a) Text Vectorization:  Converting the textual data into a 
numerical form that may be used with machine learni ng 
algorithms. Text data is formatted for analysis usi ng methods 
like Count Vectorization and TF-IDF (Term Frequency -
Inverse Document Frequency). 
 
D. Accuracy Calculation   
a) Cosine Similarity : A formula for calculating how similar 
two non-zero vectors are to each other. In the cont ext of an 
Automated Interview System, this method evaluates t he 
similarity between the candidate's response and an ideal 
answer, providing a quantitative measure of how clo sely the 
responses align. 
b) Threshold Setting:  Determining an appropriate threshold 
for acceptable similarity scores to judge the accur acy of a 
candidate's response against the model answer. 
 
E. Storing User Data 
Implementing a suitable database system such as SQL  
(Structured Query Language) databases to securely s tore and 
manage user information, ensuring scalability, reliability, and 
data integrity. 
 
IV.  RESULTS AND DISCUSSIONS  
A. TF-IDF (Term Frequency 
 
a) Inverse Document Frequency: To assess a term's 
significance within a document concerning a group o f 
documents, one important technique is to utilize TF -IDF 
(Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency). 
 It comprises two components: 
 
b) Term Frequency (TF) determines how frequently a term 
appears in a document: 
 
 , 
= Total terms in document 
Number of times term  appears in document 
       1 
 
Equation (1) represents the Term Frequency (TF) cal culation 
for a term t in a document d. It measures the frequency of term 
t within document d relative to the total number of terms in d. 
   
c) Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) determines the rarity 
of a term across a corpus: 
 
 = logNumber of documents containing term 
Total documents in the corpus !
 2  
 
Equation (2) represents the Inverse Document Freque ncy 
(IDF) calculation for a term t. It measures the rarity of term t 
across the entire corpus of documents. 
 
d) Application in Interview Response Evaluation 
Utilizing TF-IDF allowed the system to identify and prioritize 
terms or phrases critical in discerning the relevan ce and 
accuracy of interview responses. Terms with higher TF-IDF 
scores indicated uniqueness to a response, potentia lly 
signifying a more pertinent answer to the interview question. 
 
B. BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from  
Transformers) 
BERT operates on a complex architecture based on a 
bidirectional transformer model, its core strength lies in 
understanding the context and semantics of language. 
BERT's pre-training objective involves two main steps: 
a)Masked Language Model (MLM): BERT learns to predict 
intentionally masked words within a sentence. The g oal is to 
increase the likelihood that the masked word will be correctly 
predicted given its context. 
b) Sentence Prediction (NSP) 
Also learns to predict whether one sentence follows another in 
a given text. 
c) Application in Interview Evaluation: 
Semantic Understanding:  A more accurate assessment of 
interviewee responses is ensured by BERT's bidirect ional 
approach, which enables it to understand the meanin g and 
context of words in sentences. 
d) Contextual Awareness:  By capturing nuances and context, 
BERT facilitates a more refined evaluation, recognizing varied 
responses that align with the question's intent. 
 
C.Word2Vec  
Word2Vec is a technique in NLP for effectively capt uring 
semantic meanings and relationships between words i n a 
continuous vector space. It operates on the principle that words 
with similar meanings are contextually similar and therefore 
should be closer together in the vector space. 
 
 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:52 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

a) Word2Vec Fundamentals: 
Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW) and Skip-gram: 
 
b) CBOW Model 
Based on the context words in a window, it predicts  a target 
word. It learns by predicting the target word using the context 
words as input. 
c) Skip-gram Model  
Works inversely by using a word to predict the context words 
within a given window. 
d) Key Formulas 
Word2Vec models, particularly the Skip-gram model, utilize 
the concept of negative sampling to optimize the tr aining of 
word embeddings. The Skip-gram negative sampling objective 
function can be represented as in (3): 
#$%&'&()*∑, ∈ . log/0,1 ⋅ 0,3  4  ∑,
∈ .
5log/60,1 ⋅ 0,57                    3 
Where: 
•  vwo  is the vector for the observed (context) word. 
•  vwc  is the vector for the target word. 
•  ′W′ represents negative samples (random words not 
in the context). 
e) Application in Interview Evaluation: 
a) Semantic Understanding : Word2Vec effectively maps 
words to high-dimensional vectors that capture their meanings. 
This capability assists in comprehending semantic 
relationships in interview responses. 
 
b) Contextual Analysis:  By learning word embeddings from a 
large corpus, Word2Vec aids in understanding the context and 
meaning of words, ensuring a more nuanced evaluatio n of 
interviewee responses. 
 
 
D. Jaccard Similarity: 
A metric called Jaccard similarity is used to compa re how 
similar and different two sets are from one another. By dividing 
the intersection of two sets by their union, the si milarity 
between them is computed. 
 
a) Jaccard Similarity Formula: 
The formula for Jaccard similarity, as represented by (4), is: 
 
9$33$: ;&'&<$:&= >, ? = |A ∪ B||A ∩ B|       4 
Where: 
•  |
A ∩ B|  represents the number of elements in the 
intersection of sets A and B. 
•  |A ∪ B|  represents the number of elements in the 
union of sets A and B. 
b) Application in Interview Evaluation: 
In the context of interview evaluation systems, Jac card 
similarity can be employed to compare the similarity between 
the ideal or expected answers and the responses pro vided by 
interviewees, treating the answers as sets of words or phrases. 
 
c) Assessing Response Similarity:  
By converting responses into sets of words or phrases, Jaccard 
similarity measures the overlap between the expecte d and 
provided answers. Higher similarity scores indicate  a greater 
resemblance between the two sets. 
 
d) Quantifying Relevance:  
Jaccard similarity provides a quantitative measure of how 
closely an interviewee's response aligns with the ideal answer, 
enabling the system to evaluate response relevance. 
 
E. RoBERTa  
 
Building on BERT's language masking approach, RoBER Ta 
adjusts several critical hyperparameters in BERT, s uch as 
training with significantly bigger mini-batches and  learning 
rates, and eliminating BERT's next-sentence pretraining target. 
Additionally, RoBERTa was trained for a longer peri od than 
BERT using an order of magnitude more data. Compare d to 
BERT, this enables RoBERTa representations to gener alize 
even more effectively to downstream tasks. 
 
 
Fig.2.Architecture Diagram for RoBERTa model 
 
In Figure 2, a comprehensive and in-depth scenario is 
presented for tokenizing a complex sentence into di screte 
tokens. These tokens are then fed into the RoBERTa model for 
classification, producing informative class labels that capture 
the contextual understanding and semantic richness present in 
the description, demonstrating the model's capacity to identify 
and classify intricate linguistic subtleties. 
 
a) Application in Interview Evaluation: 
1) Understanding of meanings:  RoBERTa's twin network 
approach allows it to comprehend the meaning and context of 
words in both sentences at the same time, ensuring a more 
accurate evaluation of interviewee responses. 
 
 
Fig.3.Graph depicting model accuracies 
 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:52 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

This graph compares the accuracy of different text similarity 
models. The models were assessed using a dataset of text pairs, 
and the proportion of pairings for which the model accurately 
predicted whether the texts were similar or not was  used to 
calculate the models' accuracy described in Figure 3. 
 
 
TABLE.I. COMPARES THE ACCURACY OF DIFFERENT 
TEXT SIMILARITY MODELS 
 
Model  Accuracy 
TF-IDF 56.87 
BERT 40.42 
RoBERTa 93.17 
Jaccard 46.15 
Word2Vec 83.0 
 
Table I compares the accuracy of different text sim ilarity 
models. The models were evaluated on a dataset of text pairs, 
and their accuracy was measured as the percentage of pairs for 
which the model correctly predicted whether the tex ts were 
similar or not.As you can see, ROBERTA outperformed all of 
the other models, with an accuracy of 93.17%. This suggests 
that ROBERTA is a more effective model for measurin g text 
similarity than the other models tested. 
 
V.  CONCLUSION  
The proposed automated interview evaluation system 
represents a transformative innovation in recruitment, utilizing 
advanced technologies like Speech-to-Text (STT), Speech-to-
Speech (STS), and sophisticated user data storage. It 
introduces a comprehensive, objective, and personal ized 
approach to assessing candidates for job opportunities. A key 
advantage is its ability to streamline the evaluati on process, 
reducing time and effort for human evaluators and e nhancing 
the interview experience with insightful feedback. Moreover, 
it holds the potential to revolutionize recruitment practices by 
fostering fairer and more equitable hiring decisions, removing 
unconscious biases, and focusing solely on response  merit. 
This data-driven system ensures objective metrics guide hiring 
decisions, minimizing discrimination or favoritism. Embracing 
this technology promises a more efficient, data-dri ven, and 
equitable recruitment process, enabling organizations to make 
informed and fair hiring decisions while benefiting  both job 
seekers and employers. Overall, the proposed system  has the 
potential to be a game-changer in recruitment. 
VI.  FUTURE WORKS  
In future iterations, the automated interview evaluation system 
can undergo enhancements in several key areas to optimize its 
functionality. Firstly, prioritizing ongoing user f eedback 
collection will facilitate continuous refinement of  the user 
interface, ensuring alignment with user needs and preferences. 
Secondly, refining the Speech-to-Text (STT) and Spe ech-to-
Speech (STS) technologies is imperative for enhanci ng 
accuracy, particularly in accommodating diverse acc ents and 
speech patterns. Additionally, focusing on user int erface 
enhancements, such as streamlining navigation and 
incorporating user-friendly features, will augment overall 
usability. Furthermore, integrating emotion cues and sentiment 
analysis into the system can provide deeper insight s into 
candidates' communication skills and emotional inte lligence, 
enriching the evaluation process. By addressing the se areas, 
the system can evolve into a more intuitive, accura te, and 
comprehensive tool for interview assessment.

## [10] Automate Traditional Interviewing Process Using Natural Language Processing and Machine Learning
- **Authors:** Preethi J, Swetha V, Kavinaeshwar D.S., Praneen V.S.
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `Automate_Traditional_Interviewing_Process_Using_Natural_Language_Processing_and_Machine_Learning.pdf`

Automate Traditional Interviewing Process Using 
Natural Language Processing and Machine 
Learning 
Pasindu Senarathne 
Sri Lanka Institute of Information 
Technology-Undergraduate 
Department of Sftware Engieering 
Colombo, Sri Lanka 
IT17360142@my.sliit.lk  
Dulaj Kavinda
Sri Lanka Institute of Information 
Technology-Undergraduate 
Department of Sftware Engieering 
Colombo, Sri Lanka

Malaka Silva 
Sri Lanka Institute of Information 
Technology-Undergraduate 
Department of Sftware Engieering 
Colombo, Sri Lanka 
IT17 101134 @my.sliit.lk 
Prof.Samantha Thelijjagoda  
Dean  
SLIIT Business School 
Sri Lanka Institute of Information
Colombo, Sri Lanka 
samantha.t@sliit.lk 
Ama Methmini 
Sri Lanka Institute of Information 
Technology-Undergraduate 
Department of Sftware Engieering
Colombo, Sri Lanka 
IT17004596@my.sliit.lk 
Abstract—Nowadays, almost everything is equipped with 
technology. People can save time by using modern day 
technological applications in the most convenient way. Smart 
Interviewing System is one such software/tool which automates 
the traditional interviewing process using modern Natural 
Language Processing techniques and deep learning 
applications. The system will be mainly beneficial for 
interviewers and HR management employees working for 
different organizations who conduct technology related 
interviews. The system works with human voice and writing 
patterns. The system converts  human language into system 
understandable text-based inputs, and these are used as inputs 
in the automated interviewing process. The system then checks 
the accuracy of the answers which candidates provided on the 
both oral interviews/ technical interviews and written tests. 
Later, the system automatically predicts scores for each answer 
using concepts of the deep learning. Interviewers can reduce 
the effort that they have to put in for selecting the most 
suitable candidates who are qu alified enough to work with 
their organization. SIS is developed based on modern DL and 
NLP concepts using Python programming language alongside 
with ReactJS Framework. This system checking and 
evaluating candidate more accu rately in every stage of the 
interview using advance evaluation parameters than human 
oriented evaluations. Above process lead system to find more 
human errors which critically ca n be affected to future of the 
organizations. Because of that, it can be led organizations to 
find best human resources comparing to the traditional 
interviewing process by sacrificing less time and effort
.
Keywords—Human Resources Management, Smart 
Interviewing System, Deep  
I. INTRODUCTION
Information technology is one of the fastest areas that are 
growing around the world. Sri Lanka has a good reputation 
when it comes to the IT industry compared to other countries 
around the globe. According to a survey which was 
conducted by Department of Census & Statistics in Sri 
Lanka reveled that country already have 54,278 workforces 
on Information and Communication industry, and each year, 
the work force is increasing. Simultaneously, with the 
development of the education system in Sri Lanka, many 
youngsters are involved in degree programs or higher 
education programs. Information Technology is the most 
selected domain among these youngsters. On 2017, Over 
10,000 students enrolled for Computer Science Degree 
Programs and Technology related degree programs as 
undergraduates [1]. Upon their graduation, they will be 
looking for job opportunities that are suitable for their 
educational qualifications and professional skills. Because of 
that, job market would be more competitive in future. 
According to the Labor Demand Survey which was 
conducted by Department of Census & Statistics in Sri 
Lanka, proved that demand of the jobs will be increased  
[2].In a such environment, companies will be trying to recruit 
best people who are available within the workforce. In order 
to do that, companies must improve their recruitment 
processes to a higher level. Currently most companies follow 
traditional interviewing processes with more human 
involvement. Problem is, these traditional interviewing
processes have some common flaws due to human 
involvement. Also, traditional interviewing processes usually 
take more time, because everything within the traditional 
interviewing process is operated and organized by a selected 
group of humans. The proposed system in this paper 
automates the traditional interview process using Natural 
Language Processing and modern Machine Learning 
approaches. Within the system, interview process is divided 
into four main phases: Oral test, Written test, Expert area 
detection and Future HRM related problem detection 
II. RELATED WORKS
Using Text-to-speech (TTS) for Audio Computer-
assisted Self-interviewing (ACASI) is a research that has 
been done during the 2006–2010 National Survey on Family 
Development, which was perfo rmed in Turkey [3].There 
were no strong patterns in the empirical distributions that 
move from recorded human voices to text-to-speech that 
impact the absence of data rates. Researchers identified much 
higher records of audio usage, but no evidence to show that 
the duration of the interview improves. It is much simpler to 
replace or edit requests and make other changes to audio files 
in questions without causing unexpected disjoints such as 
pitch shifts, sound or even speech changes, etc. Although 
producing audio files in major surveys using audio computer 
assisted self-interviewing is not a significant cost in 
comparison to the field costs, the text-to-speech use is easier.
