gradually improves with increasing training steps. During
inference, our model was tested on multiple checkpoints
taken at intervals of 50k iterations. A few of these iterations
are mentioned in Figure 9. Even though the alignment at
some of these steps looks the same, an improvement was
noted over time with the synthesized child voices. This was
determined subjectively during training by listening to the
synthetic child speech generated. The training was halted
at 750k steps as improvements in the alignment graph had
become imperceptible after 700k steps. The output waveform
did not show any improvements beyond this step. The model
trained up to 750k iterations is used to provide audio samples
in this paper.
The V ocoder was trained at a batch size of 128 and learning
rate of 0.0001 and took 4 days of training to reach 250k
iterations. Most of the parameters for the vocoder were kept
the same as the original code. 7
The synthetic child voices during inference were natural
sounding and the trained model demonstrated an ability to
synthesize quite challenging phrases that were unseen in the
TinyMyST dataset. This was tested by using ‘tongue twisters’
as a reference text for synthesizing speech. However, it was
also noted that some phonemes were not synthesized cor-
rectly and lost their meaning during synthesis. These ﬁndings
are discussed in more detail in Section IV.
Code-related material and synthesized speech from
these experiments will be made available in our GitHub
Repository.8
IV. RESULTS AND EVALUATION
The evaluation in TTS is usually done by taking a Mean
Opinion Score (MOS) [59] on the synthetic speech for Speech
Similarity and Speech Naturalness.
7V ocoder Hyperparameters: https://github.com/CorentinJ/Real-Time-
V oice-Cloning/blob/master/vocoder/hparams.py
8GitHub for this paper: https://github.com/C3Imaging/ChildTTS
There are many objective and subjective evaluation meth-
ods proposed by researchers [60]–[66]. These traditional
speech evaluation methods work well for evaluating adult
speech but are not so suitable for child speech. A perfect adult
speech will contain ﬂuent pronunciation of a word/phoneme
however this is not the case for most child speech. Natural-
ness in child speech includes pauses, breaks, and pronunci-
ation difﬁculties in the speech. Other challenges were noted
with the start and end of phrases where children tend to be
somewhat hesitant when starting a phrase and may wander
towards the end of one. Children can also mispronounce
words, or struggle with the phonetics of a particular phrase.
These characteristics tend to manifest in the speech model
and a range of artifacts were noted that affect the quality of
the phrases synthesized by our pipeline. It was noted that
the ﬁrst or last words in many phrases were either missed
entirely or subject to various distortions or artifacts. In the
middle of a phrase, there could occasionally be slurring or
arbitrary elongating of one or more words. Another artifact
observed was that the pace or tone of voice could change
abruptly in the middle of a phrase. Despite these artifacts,
the majority of phrases were quite intelligible, and a large
proportion was also very natural sounding. Therefore, there
is a need for a better subjective evaluation method for child
speech synthesis.
In the following, we present the results obtained using
the proposed subjective evaluation method and the various
listening tests performed (subsection 4.A), two objective
evaluation methods, based on MOSNet (subsection 4.B) and
an ASR system (subsection 4.D) and we evaluate the simi-
larity of the synthesized speech and natural child speech (in
subsection 4.C).
A. PROPOSED SUBJECTIVE EVALUATION METHOD
To check the phonetic coverage of our child speech TTS,
Harvard sentences [60] were used, which are a set of 720
phonetically balanced sentences. These sentences cover most
of the phoneme range and were designed to be implemented
with V oice over Internet Protocol (V oIP) technology. These
texts were used to generate synthetic child speech. This was
done to check the subjective quality of synthesized audio with
respect to phoneme coverage.
Our evaluation method uses a MOS-like evaluation with
different categories for scoring. When generating synthetic
voices using Harvard sentences, it was observed that some
sets of phonemes were not pronounced correctly even when
synthesized using different reference child speakers (more
detail in a later section). After our initial subjective study of
these 720 synthesized audio samples, it was decided that a
more detailed evaluation protocol was required to address the
various artifacts observed and identify what additional data
samples might be needed to further improve our model. For
this reason, our evaluation was performed in two phases. For
each of the two phases, different evaluators were gathered to
perform the speech evaluation. Each evaluator was asked to
listen to synthetic audio ﬁles using Headphones/Earphones
VOLUME 10, 2022 47635

R. Jainet al.: TTS Pipeline, Evaluation Methodology, and Initial Fine-Tuning Results
in a noise-free environment. They were asked to rate each
of the synthetic voices assigned to them from a range of
1 to 5, for each of the different categories in two phases. The
categories included Speech Intelligibility, V oice Naturalness,
and V oice Consistency. V oice Consistency contained three
sub-categories of its own namely, Start of Phrase quality,
Middle of Phrase quality, and End of Phrase Quality.
Evaluation data was provided in a OneDrive Environment.
All the synthetic voices were shared in a common OneDrive
folder to the evaluators and a common spreadsheet was circu-
lated containing the utterance ID of Harvard sentences used
for synthesizing a child’s voice. While listening to many
different natural child voices, it was also noticed that recorded
child audio can be a difﬁcult task to understand if not pro-
vided with a suitable transcript. Some of the child’s speech
can be non-meaningful as mentioned in problems with the
MyST section. After performing many different tests and
trials using child speech, the use of transcripts as a part of
MOS-based evaluation is considered to be a more natural
way of evaluating child speech. Therefore, corresponding
transcript information is also provided in the spreadsheet to
each evaluator to base their conclusion on ‘what they hear
in child audio’ and ‘what they read in child transcripts’.
This way more coherent patterns can be observed among the
phonemes and graphemes in a child’s voice for each of the
mentioned categories. An example of this spreadsheet can be
seen here.9
By performing the evaluation using OneDrive environ-
ment, it was easy to distribute the synthetic speech ﬁles to dif-
ferent evaluators without having to spend time and resources
on expensive Mushra-based evaluations [67] or crowdsourc-
ing the evaluation task on platforms like Amazon Mechani-
cal Turk (AMT) [59], [68]. Mushra-based evaluations were
also avoided due to potential biases that can occur in these
tests and how these biases can impact synthetic child voice
evaluation for MOS [69]. Most of these TTS evaluations
have been conducted before with synthetic adult speech, this
novel synthetic evaluation is implemented for ﬁrst-time with
synthetic child speech. Using a common spreadsheet made it
effective to perform analysis of spreadsheet for MOS using
pandas and other python-based tools.
1) PHASE-I EVALUATION
For the phase-I evaluation, all 720 Harvard sentences were
generated using our proposed TTS method. Two random ref-
erence utterances were selected from the TinyMyST dataset
and were used to generate all the Harvard sentences. These
720 sentences were shared among 5 evaluators in a spread-
sheet document, who rated the voices from 1 to 5 based
on Speech Intelligibilityand Voice Naturalness. The MOS
ratings from 1 to 5 were further explained in the spreadsheet
ﬁle as can be seen in Table 5.
9Example Spreadsheet: https://www.github.com/C3Imaging/ChildTTS/
blob/main/synthetic%20evaluation%20example/Example.xlsx
TABLE 5. MOS (from 1 to 5) explained for speech intelligibility and voice
naturalness.
TABLE 6. MOS from phase-I evaluation with 95% confidence interval
The spreadsheet was later analyzed to get the ﬁnal mean
opinion score in each category. MOS of 3.88 for voice nat-
uralness and 4.13 for speech intelligibility was observed as
seen in Table 6.
An average score for each of the 720 sentences was cal-
culated for the combined value of speech intelligibility and
voice naturalness. All the 720 sentences were sorted into
difﬁcult and easy sentences with respect to the children’s
linguistic capabilities. This was done to keep track of Harvard
sentences where synthesized speech becomes unintelligible
and inarticulate.
2) PHASE-II EVALUATION
After our phase-I evaluation, a common set of sentences
were observed where pronunciation sounds unintelligible
at the start, middle, or end of sentences for speciﬁc
words/phonemes. There was an inconsistency in voice qual-
ity. These sets of sentences are the ones that were not learned
properly during training or were missing in the training
dataset for child audio. To make a note of these sentences,
extra categories of ‘V oice Consistency’ were added to the
phase-I evaluation. Therefore, all the 3 sub-categories under
Voice Consistencywere used in the second phase of the eval-
uation. These subcategories included ‘Start of Phrase Qual-
ity’, ‘Middle of phrase quality’ and ‘End of phrase quality’.
The MOS ratings from 1 to 5 for each of these categories were
also explained in the spreadsheet as mentioned in Table 7.
For the second phase of evaluation, the evaluation was
undertaken by 20 evaluators divided into 4 groups. This was
done as per the guidelines mentioned in [70] for performing
MOS evaluations. For each group, a speaker identity was
selected from the TinyMyST dataset. All the speaker iden-
tities were sorted, and the top 20 speaker identities were
47636 VOLUME 10, 2022

R. Jainet al.: TTS Pipeline, Evaluation Methodology, and Initial Fine-Tuning Results
TABLE 7. MOS (from 1 to 5) explained for voice consistency and its three
sub-categories.
TABLE 8. Selected speaker identity information in TinyMyST VS TTS
utterances for the same speakers.
selected, having the most minutes. Among these 20 identities,
4 speaker identities were randomly selected. All the 4 groups
are named as ‘013020’, ‘008045’, ‘002113’, and ‘995737’,
corresponding to each identity label. This approach was taken
to select speakers with the most data and also to keep the
process randomized. More information on these selected
speakers can be seen in Table 8. This table is also used for
speaker similarity and objective intelligibility experiments in
the future sections.
A reference child utterance was selected randomly from
each of these groups, and 50 Harvard sentences were selected
randomly for each of the groups. Therefore, 50 Synthetic
utterances were generated, and all the evaluators were asked
to rate the utterances assigned to them.
MOS results from the phase-II evaluation are presented
in Table 9. MOS of 3.95 was observed for Speech Intel-
ligibility, 3.89 for Voice Naturalness, and 3.96 for over-
all Voice Consistency(including the three sub-categories).
MOS of 4.07 was observed for ‘Start of phrase quality’,
4.18 for ‘Middle of phrase quality’, and 3.62 for ‘End of
phrase quality’. The MOS score implies that the quality of
synthesized child speech is quite good. However, there is still
TABLE 9. MOS from phase-II evaluation with 95% confidence interval.
room for improvement in the ‘End of phrase quality’ of Har-
vard sentences. There is information loss observed at the end
of most sentences containing inarticulate and unintelligent
information or noise. The reason for this information loss can
be redirected back to the child dataset used for training. Even
though TinyMyST is much cleaner than the MyST dataset,
it still contains some of the problems seen in Section II.A.1.
The information obtained from voice consistency will be
discussed more in future work.
A similar experiment was also performed using the real
utterances from Table 8 to obtain a baseline MOS on natural
child speech. 15 random real utterances were selected from
the real speakers mentioned in Table 8. Evaluators were asked
to perform a similar evaluation as done in phase-II evaluation
for all the selected 60 utterances. A comparison between the
baseline MOS on Natural MyST and synthetically generated
utterances is mentioned in Table 10.
Synthetic Speech MOS for three categories is very close to
Natural Speech MOS. There is a MOS differenceof ‘0.26’
for Speech Intelligibility, ‘0.16’ for V oice Naturalness, and
‘0.12’ for V oice Consistency between natural and synthetic
speech. From Table 10, it can be concluded that the MOS
for Natural and Synthetic child speech are quite close to
each other. This subjective evaluation approach is proposed
as a part of this paper. Due to very limited work done on
child speech synthesis, we did not ﬁnd any reliable way of
performing subjective evaluation over the synthesized child
speech. From our experience with the evaluation of synthetic
child speech, this new metric of evaluation can help evalu-
ate synthetic child speech and can help further this area of
research. It is also intended to use this proposed approach for
our future work with child speech synthesis.
B. OBJECTIVE NATURALNESS EVALUATION USING A
PRETRAINED MOSNET
For this objective evaluation, a pretrained MOSNet was used,
which is trained on VCC 2018 dataset from Blizzard Chal-
lenge [66] comprising of adult speech. According to their
paper, MOSNet predictions yield a high correlation to human
ratings. As MOSNet was trained on adult speech, it is unlikely
that it will generalize well for child speech. It won’t be
possible to train a MOSNet with child voices as there is not
VOLUME 10, 2022 47637

R. Jainet al.: TTS Pipeline, Evaluation Methodology, and Initial Fine-Tuning Results
TABLE 10. MOS natural speech VS MOS synthetic speech with 95%
confidence interval.
FIGURE 10. Spectrogram comparison between reference and synthesized
child audio for 5 audio samples used with MOSNet.
enough data to perform a large-scale evaluation such as a
blizzard challenge. This objective evaluation was performed
to see the correlation between reference child audio and
synthetic child audio. A random set of 50 utterances were
selected from the TinyMyST dataset as a part of this inside
test. These utterances were used as reference utterances and
the corresponding transcripts were used to generate synthetic
speech for each of these utterances. This gave us 50 reference
and 50 synthetic utterances which were used to calculate
MOS using MOSNet. MOS score for 5 samples can be seen
in Table 11. The spectrograms for these 5 samples can be seen
in Figure 10.
Table 12 shows the overall MOS output for MOSNet. MOS
of 2.96 was observed for reference child audioand 2.66
for synthetic child audio. There is only a 0.3 difference in
MOS between reference and synthetic child voices. MOSNet
trained on adult speech data is not expected to give MOS
ratings correlated with human MOS ratings for child speech
data. MOSNet was only used to get a correlation between the
TABLE 11. MOSNet output for 5 samples.
TABLE 12. MOSNet output for 50 samples with 95% confidence interval.
reference child audio and synthetic child audio. This gave us
a comparison between reference and synthetic child voices
as to how close they are to each other in terms of audio
features calculated using MOSNet. The results conﬁrmed that
MOSNet output for reference child speech and synthetic child
speech are very close to each other with a comparative MOS
difference of 0.3.
C. SPEAKER SIMILARITY EVALUATION USING A SPEAKER
VERIFICATION SYSTEM
Speaker similarity between a synthesized speech and a real
speech can be calculated using a Speaker Veriﬁcation (SV)
system. The pretrained speaker encoder from section 2.B.1.
was used with a third-party tool 10 to extract and visualize
the speaker embeddings. This tool uses cosine distance to
calculate the similarity between the two embeddings. The
same speakers mentioned in our subjective evaluation (see
Table 8) were used for this evaluation. 10 utterances were
randomly selected for both real and synthetic speech for each
of the 4 speakers mentioned in Table 8. 1 male and 1 female
speaker from the LibriSpeech dataset were also added with
10 utterances each to show the speaker similarity comparison
between an adult and child speaker. A visualization of this
similarity in a 2D projection can be seen in Figure 11, ‘gt’ is
used as a label for the ground truth of the speaker and ‘ss’ is
used as a label for the synthetic speech of the same speaker.
‘Adult_Male’ and ‘Adult_Female’ are two randomly selected
male and female speakers from the LibriSpeech Dataset.
From Figure 11, it can be inferred that Male, Female, and
Child speech have a difference in similarity from each other.
Male and Female adult speakers are far apart from each other
and from child speakers in this 2D projection of speaker
embeddings.
To further comment on the similarity between real child
speech and synthetic child speech, the ‘child speech’ contour
from Figure 11 is extended to get a more visual representation
of embeddings. This can be seen in Figure 12. The ‘gt’ labels
and very close to ‘ss’ labels in this 2D projection space.
These embeddings are 256-dimensional feature vectors
trained by our speaker encoder. Therefore, cosine similarity
was used to further calculate the cross-similarity between

FIGURE 11. Projections of embeddings between different real and

This line was drawn from the centroid of each cluster to show the visual
representation of similarity between real and synthetic speech.
each speaker. Each of the 10 Speakers with 10 utterances
each (1 Adult Male, 1 Adult Female, 4 Ground Truth Child,
and 4 Synthetic Speech Child) were divided into 2 sets A and
B. Embeddings are extracted for each of the utterances for
each of the sets and averaged together for each speaker. This
gave us 10 unique speaker embeddings in sets A and B each
for 10 speakers. Cosine similarity is ﬁnally used to measure
the similarity between each of the 10 speaker embeddings in
sets A and B. A plot for the cross similarity between speakers
can be seen in Figure 12.
In Figure 13, speaker similarity between synthetic speech
and ground truth for speaker ‘995737’ is 0.91, whereas for
speakers ‘013020’ and ‘008045’ is approximately around
0.82 and ﬁnally for the speaker ‘002113’ is approximately
0.7. This cross-similarity matrix gives us an idea of how
close synthetic child voices are in comparison to the real
child voice. It also shows us how different an Adult Male
and Female Speech is in comparison to a child’s speech.
Overall, the similarity between most of the child and adult
speech is between 0.3-0.4 whereas the similarity between
most of the synthetic child speech and ground truth child
speech is between a range of 0.65-0.85. Cross-similarity
across the diagonal signiﬁes that an utterance in set-A is 95%
similar to utterances in set-B having the same speakers. More
conclusions can be drawn from Figure 13, however, for the
scope of this research, it is only used to show the different
FIGURE 13. Cross-similarity between 10 speakers in Set A and Set B. The
rectangular black box represents the similarity between real and
synthetic child speech for respective speakers in set-A and set-B. Set-A is
along the x-axis and Set-B is along the y-axis. ‘ss’ represents the synthetic
speech and ‘gt’ represents the ground truth (real) speech.
speaker similarities between real child speech and synthetic
child speech and to draw a conclusion that our synthetically
generated child speech is very close to real speech in terms
of speaker similarity with an average similarity of 81%.
D. OBJECTIVE INTELLIGIBILITY EVALUATION USING A
PRETRAINED ASR SYSTEM
A pretrained wav2vec2 model is used to provide veriﬁcation
on synthetic utterances. A comparison of the speech tran-
scription between real and synthetic child voices is presented.
Child speech recognition is a challenging task of its own. The
ASR on child speech is a part of our future work. Our intent
to use this model for this paper is based on the popularity of
the model, being SOTA on adult speech. A wav2vec2 model
trained on adult speech data is used to provide that compari-
son. This speech transcription was obtained for the synthetic
and real utterances mentioned in Table 8. A random set of 30
utterances for each speaker for both real and synthetic voices
are selected. The instruction for using this model is mentioned
in their Github. 11
A comparison of this model is also provided using adult
speech by selecting the equal number of adult voices from the
LibriSpeech dataset. Word Error Rate (WER) is calculated
from the output of wav2vec2 and is mentioned in Table 13.
The Flashlight 12 library is used to calculate the WER using
Viterbi decoding. No external language model (LM) was
used.
From Table 13, it can be inferred that the WER for Adult
Speech (Librispeech_test_clean) is 3.43, evidently, due to the
model being trained on adult speech data, the WER for real
child speech is 15.27 and in comparison, WER for synthetic
utterances is 25.63. An ASR model was able to recognize

12Flashlight: https://github.com/ﬂashlight/ﬂashlight

TABLE 13. WER on adult speech, real child speech and synthetic child
speech.
75% of the synthetic speech with a relative difference of
10 WER when compared with real child speech recognized
by the same model for the same speakers.
V. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK
In this paper, a pipeline for generating synthetic child speech
in a limited training data scenario is proposed. A small
set of child speech data is created by cleaning an existing
child speech dataset and making it suitable for TTS training.
A transfer learning approach is used to train our model with
adult speech data in a pretraining setting and child speech data
as low as 19 hours for ﬁne-tuning. MOSNet based objective
evaluation shows a high correlation between real and synthe-
sized child voices. A subjective evaluation method suitable
for synthesized child speech is also proposed and demon-
strated. Subjective MOS of synthesized voices is observed
as 3.95 for speech intelligibility, 3.89 for voice naturalness,
and 3.96 for overall voice consistency which is very close
to Natural speech MOS. These MOS values tell us about
how good the synthesized child voices are. However, voice
inconsistency for ‘End of phrase quality’ containing noise and
unintelligible information was also observed. There is scope
for improvement for these phrases. WER for synthetic child
voices using a pretrained adult speech wav2vec2 ASR model
came to be 25.63 as compared to WER of real child voices
of 15.27. Synthetic child speech samples can be viewed in
our GitHub repository. 13 Multi-speaker TTS can be the key
to child speech synthesis with limited training data. Child
speakers with speech duration between 5-7 minutes in TTS
training gave 81% average cosine similarity with a synthetic
speech from the same speakers. This choice of the model
allows the TTS to learn useful speaker information which can
be leveraged to produce better quality synthetic voices even
with limited child speech.
For future work, our aim is to improve this method by
incorporating more information to our multi-speaker TTS
model such as duration predictor and energy as implemented
in FastSpeech2 [12]. The trained vocoder was also ﬁnetuned
on the TinyMyST dataset. However, there was no signiﬁcant
improvement in the quality of the generated audio waveforms
and an additional noise was observed in some of the synthesis.
More child speech data would be required to achieve any
signiﬁcant improvement over the quality of the vocoder. It is
also intended to implement GAN-based SOTA V ocoders such
as HiFi-GAN [19] for future experiments. More experiments
such as training a forced aligner using children’s voices is
also part of our future work. It will help to generate more
meaningful alignments for splitting the longer audio ﬁles
13https://c3imaging.github.io/ChildTTS/
to increase the training dataset. The information collected
from our subjective evaluation such as voice consistency in
Harvard sentences will be used to improve child speech.
This information will be used to collect better TTS-based
child speech data based on Harvard sentences to accord with
‘end of the phrase’ information loss and voice inconsistency
observed with our current results. The use of synthetically
generated child speech to improve other areas of child speech
research such as ASR and speaker recognition will also be
investigated in future work. TTS-generated child voices can
be used as a data augmentation technique for training these
models with additional data. It is also intended to use the
subjective evaluation method proposed in this paper for per-
forming all future subjective evaluations with TTS generated
child speech.

throughout.

RISHABH JAIN (Graduate Student Member,
IEEE) received the B.Tech. degree in computer
science and engineering from the Vellore Insti-
tute of Technology (VIT), in 2019, and the M.S.
degree in data analytics from the National Univer-
sity of Ireland Galway (NUIG), in 2020, where
he is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree. He is
also working as a Research Assistant at NUIG
under DA VID (Data-Center Audio/Visual Intelli-
gence on-Device) Project. His research interests
include machine learning and artiﬁcial intelligence speciﬁcally in domain
of speech understanding, text-to-speech, speaker recognition, and automatic
speech recognition.
MARIAM YAHAYAH YIWERE received the
Bachelor of Science degree from the Department
of Computer Science, Kwame Nkrumah Univer-
sity of Science and Technology, Kumasi, Ghana,
in 2012, and the Master of Engineering and Ph.D.
degrees from the Department of Computer Engi-
neering, Hanbat National University, South Korea,
in August 2015 and February 2020, respectively.
Since October 2020, she has been working on the
DTIF/DA VID Project as a Postdoctoral Researcher
with the College of Science and Engineering, National University of Ireland
Galway, Galway. Her research interests include text-to-speech synthesis,
speaker recognition and veriﬁcation, sound source localization, deep learn-
ing, and computer vision.
DAN BIGIOI (Graduate Student Member, IEEE)
received the bachelor’s degree in electronic and
computer engineering from the National Univer-
sity of Ireland Galway, in 2020. Upon graduat-
ing, he worked as a Research Assistant at NUIG
studying the text-to-speech and speaker recog-
nition methods under the DA VID (Data-Center
Audio/Visual Intelligence on-Device) Project.
Currently, he is working on his Ph.D. at NUIG,
sponsored by D-REAL and the SFI Centre for
Research Training in Digitally Enhanced Reality. His research interests
include novel deep learning-based techniques for automatic speech dubbing
and discovering new ways to process multimodal audio/visual data.
PETER CORCORAN (Fellow, IEEE) is currently
holding the Personal Chair of Electronic Engineer-
ing with the College of Science and Engineering,
National University of Ireland Galway (NUIG).
He was the Co-Founder of several start-up com-
panies, notably FotoNation (currently the Imaging
Division, Xperi Corporation). He has more than
600 cited technical publications and patents, more
than 120 peer-reviewed journal articles, 160 inter-
national conference papers, and a co-inventor on
more than 300 granted U.S. patents. He is an IEEE Fellow recognized for
his contributions to digital camera technologies, notably in-camera red-eye
correction and facial detection. He is also a member of the IEEE Consumer
Technology Society for more than 25 years and the Founding Editor of IEEE
Consumer Electronics Magazine.
HORIA CUCU(Member, IEEE) received the B.S.
and M.S. degrees in applied electronics and the
Ph.D. degree in electronics and telecommunica-
tion engineering from the University Politehnica
of Bucharest (UPB), Romania, in 2008 and 2011,
respectively.
From 2010 to 2017, he was a Teaching Assistant
and then a Lecturer at UPB, where he is cur-
rently working as an Associate Professor. In this
position, he authored over 75 scientiﬁc papers
in international conferences and journals, served as the project director
for seven research projects, and contributed as a researcher to ten other
research grants. He holds two patents. In addition, he founded and leads
Zevo Technology, a speech start-up dedicated to integrating state-of-the-
art speech technologies in various commercial applications. His research
interests include machine/deep learning and artiﬁcial intelligence, with a
special focus on automatic speech and speaker recognition, text-to-speech
synthesis, and speech emotion recognition.
Dr. Cucu was awarded the Romanian Academy Prize ‘‘Mihail Drăgă-
nescu’’ (2016) for Outstanding Research Contributions in Spoken Language
Technology, after developing the ﬁrst large-vocabulary automatic speech
recognition system for the Romanian language.

## [15] Generative AI-Powered Mock Interview System with Real-Time Multimodal Feedback & Skill Matching
- **Authors:** Likhitha A, L M Pavan Kumar
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `Generative_AI-Powered_Mock_Interview_System_with_Real-Time_Multimodal_Feedback_amp_Skill_Matching.pdf`

979-8-3315-5418-7/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE 
Generative AI-Powered Mock Interview System 
with Real-Time Multimodal Feedback & Skill 
Matching 
 
1st Simmran Agarwal  
Jaypee Institute Of Information 
Technology 
Noida, India 
simmran2003@gmail.com 
4th Dr. Alka Singhal 
Jaypee Institute Of Information 
Technology 
Noida, India 
alka.singhal@jiit.ac.in 
2nd Jatin Gupta 
Jaypee Institute Of Information 
Technology 
Noida, India 
jatin321gupta@gmail.com 
 
3rd Aman Kulshrestha 
Jaypee Institute Of Information 
Technology 
Noida, India 
kulaman20903@gmail.com 
Abstract— Preparing for technical interviews is a critical yet 
challenging task for job aspirants, especially in the software 
industry. This paper introduces an AI-powered mock interview 
system that offers an interactive, personalized, and real-time 
evaluation experience. The system dynamically generates 
domain-specific questions by analysing a candidate's resume 
and job description using the Gemini API. It incorporates 
speech and video emotion recognition models to evaluate a 
candidate's confidence and communication skills. Speech input 
is transcribed using Wav2Vec2 and evaluated for emotional 
tone using a pre-trained emotion classification model. Video 
responses are analysed using Ollama's Gemma3 for facial 
emotion detection and expression analysis. Candidate 
performance is scored based on technical correctness and 
emotional expressiveness, with comprehensive feedback 
provided after each session. Empirical studies show an average 
accuracy of 89% for technical evaluation and 84% for emotion 
detection, validating the system's reliability. This work 
demonstrates the potential of integrating multimodal AI 
technologies to improve technical interview readiness and 
reduce candidate anxiety. 
I. INTRODUCTION 
Securing a position in the technology sector requires 
candidates to excel not only in their technical knowledge but 
also in communication skills and confidence during 
interviews. While traditional mock interviews provide 
limited exposure, they often fall short of adapting to a 
candidate's unique skill set or delivering real-time feedback. 
With the evolving landscape of artificial intelligence (AI), 
there is a growing demand for intelligent, automated systems 
that can mimic real-world interview scenarios while offering 
actionable insights. 
Our work presents an AI-powered mock interview platform 
designed to address the limitations of conventional methods. 
The system parses a candidate’s resume and provided job 
description to identify relevant technical skills using natural 
language processing. Based on the overlap or approximated 
match between the two, it generates custom questions 
dynamically through the Gemini API. It follows a progressive 
questioning strategy- starting with basic questions and 
adapting to candidate’s performance level by generating 
more complex follow-ups. 
Beyond technical evaluation, this platform integrates 
multimodal emotion recognition capabilities. Using speech 
transcription models such as Wav2Vec2 and facial analysis 
via Ollama’s Gemma3, it evaluates a candidate’s emotional 
state to determine confidence levels. These insights are used 
to produce a composite performance score and detailed 
feedback, helping candidates identify their strengths and 
areas for improvement. By bridging the gap between 
technical preparation and behavioural assessment, this 
system aims to equip users with a holistic mock interview 
experience. 
II. RELATED WORK 
A. Review Of Existing AI-Based Interview Systems 
Several systems have been developed to aid interview 
preparation using artificial intelligence, each adopting 
different methodologies and architectures. One notable 
system is InterviewX, which utilizes Retrieval-Augmented 
Generation (RAG) and QLoRA for context-aware question 
generation tailored to specific roles [3]. InterviewX 
emphasizes the generation of personalized questions, though 
it lacks the integrated multimodal emotion analysis offered 
by our system. 
MIP (Mock Interview Platform) is another significant 
example, leveraging both visual and audio modalities to 
deliver performance feedback [2], [4]. It employs traditional 
speech emotion recognition tools and facial recognition APIs 
to gauge candidate responses. However, these platforms often 
do not support real-time feedback or dynamic adaptation 
based on a candidate’s previous answers. 
Speech processing advancements, such as those brought by 
Wav2Vec2, have significantly improved automatic speech 
recognition (ASR) accuracy, enabling real-time transcription 
of interview responses [6]. Similarly, vision-based emotion 
2025 Seventeenth International Conference on Contemporary Computing (IC3) | 979-8-3315-5418-7/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/IC366947.2025.11290351
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:47 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

detection models like MobileNetV2 have shown promis e in 
lightweight, low-latency emotion classification on edge 
devices [5]. 
B.  Addressing Gaps and Enhancing System Capabilites 
Despite the capabilities of these systems, many fal l short 
in maintaining the structural integrity needed for 
comprehensive performance analysis. They either do not 
retain historical user data for iterative improvement or lack a 
robust backend architecture for storing composite s cores 
derived from both technical and emotional metrics. Our 
system, by contrast, integrates these components in to a 
unified feedback engine that enables candidates to track 
progress over time. 
By ensuring real-time evaluation, progressive diffi culty 
scaling, and emotion analysis across modalities, ou r AI-
powered mock interview system stands out by deliver ing a 
holistic, adaptive, and data-driven interview prepa ration 
experience [10]–[12]. 
III.  P
ROPOSED WORK  
A.  Overview 
This proposed work introduces an intelligent, multimodal 
mock interview platform that uses artificial intell igence to 
simulate technical interviews in an adaptive, inter active, and 
personalized manner. Unlike traditional mock interview tools 
that rely heavily on static questions or fixed form ats, our 
system tailors each interview session to the candid ate’s 
background and real-time performance. It not only t ests a 
candidate’s technical knowledge but also evaluates 
communication style and emotional confidence using both 
audio and video analysis. 
The core idea is to bridge the gap between technica l 
evaluation and soft-skill assessment, which are bot h vital 
components in real-world interviews. Candidates oft en 
underperform not due to lack of knowledge but due t o 
nervousness, poor articulation, or low confidence. This 
system proposes a unique blend of dynamic question 
generation, speech and facial emotion recognition, and 
holistic feedback generation to address those issues. 
By leveraging advanced models such as Wav2Vec2 for 
speech-to-text transcription and RAVDESS-trained mo dels 
for speech emotion detection, the system can effect ively 
evaluate the tone and emotional state of a candidat e’s verbal 
responses. For facial emotion analysis, while Mobil eNetV2 
was initially explored using the FER dataset, the f inal 
implementation employs Ollama's Gemma3 to interpret  
visual emotional cues from candidate responses more  
contextually. This dual evaluation—combining audio and 
video emotion recognition—enables more insightful 
feedback and better simulates real-world interview 
conditions. 
B.  System Architecture 
The system architecture comprises a Next.js-based 
frontend and a Python-powered backend. The backend is 
responsible for key functionalities such as skill e xtraction, 
intelligent question generation, response evaluatio n, and 
personalized feedback generation. To support these 
capabilities, it leverages state-of-the-art machine  learning 
models—Wav2Vec2 is employed for accurate speech-to-text 
transcription, while a custom emotion recognition m odel 
initially trained on the RAVDESS dataset was used t o 
classify emotional tone in audio responses. 
Although early prototypes utilized MobileNetV2 trai ned on 
the FER dataset for facial emotion detection, the f inal 
deployment integrates Ollama’s Gemma3 model. This choice 
allows for a more nuanced and context-aware interpr etation 
of visual emotions, improving the system’s ability to assess 
non-verbal cues. 
For persistent session management and long-term 
performance tracking, MongoDB serves as the primary  data 
store. Its flexibility supports storing detailed re cords of user 
interactions, including question-answer pairs, emot ion 
metrics, and evaluation outcomes. 
The system's modular and extensible design ensures that it 
can readily accommodate future enhancements. Planne d 
upgrades include support for multiple languages and  real-
time coaching features, making the platform adaptab le to a 
wide range of user needs and use cases. 
        Fig. 1. System architecture of the AI-powered mock interview platform  
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:47 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

C.  Objective 
The objective of this project is to develop a robus t and 
scalable mock interview system that can: 
• Use Automatically tailor interviews to individual skill 
sets based on resumes and job descriptions. 
• Dynamically adjust the difficulty of questions in real-
time. 
• Evaluate emotional confidence through speech tone 
and facial expressions. 
• Provide comprehensive feedback for continuous 
improvement. 
 
D.  Key Features 
The proposed system incorporates several advanced 
features to deliver an effective and intelligent mock interview 
experience. First, it enables automatic parsing of resumes and 
job descriptions using fuzzy string matching to ext ract key 
technical skills, which are then mapped to relevant  and 
personalized interview questions. The platform uses  the 
Gemini API to generate questions of increasing diff iculty 
levels, beginning with a basic conceptual question and 
proceeding to more complex follow-ups based on cand idate 
responses. 
Candidate responses can be submitted via audio or v ideo. 
Audio responses are transcribed using the Wav2Vec2 model 
and further analysed for emotional tone. While an i n-house 
model trained on the RAVDESS dataset was initially 
developed for speech emotion recognition, the syste m later 
transitioned to a more robust pre-trained model to enhance 
reliability and performance. For video responses, e arly 
iterations employed MobileNetV2 trained on the FER 
dataset; however, the final deployment uses Ollama’ s 
Gemma3 to interpret facial expressions in a more co ntext-
aware and semantically rich manner. 
Emotional confidence is assessed using both speech and 
visual emotion recognition to classify states such as calm, 
happy, fearful, neutral, or surprised. The system t hen 
evaluates the technical accuracy of responses by co mparing 
them with Gemini-generated reference answers and 
integrates emotion-based confidence metrics to comp ute a 
composite score. All performance data is stored and  
visualized over time, allowing candidates to monito r their 
progress and receive personalized feedback for cont inuous 
improvement. 
 
 
 
 
 
               Fig. 2. Sequential workflow of the candidate interview process.  
 
E.  Expected Outcomes 
The proposed system is expected to achieve the following key 
outcomes: 
• Enhanced Interview Preparation : By simulating 
real-world technical interviews with adaptive 
questioning and emotional assessment, candidates are 
better equipped for actual interview scenarios. 
• Comprehensive Skill Evaluation : The integration of 
both technical answer analysis and emotion-based 
confidence scoring offers a holistic evaluation 
approach that goes beyond conventional mock 
interview systems. 
• Personalized Feedback and Growth Tracking : 
Continuous feedback on technical accuracy, 
confidence levels, and performance trends enables 
candidates to pinpoint weaknesses and monitor 
improvements over time. 
• Increased Confidence and Communication Skills : 
Exposure to timed, AI-evaluated sessions helps 
candidates build articulation skills, reduce anxiet y, 
and improve non-verbal communication. 
• Data-Driven Insights for Users and Institutions : 
The system’s ability to collect and analyze 
performance data over time offers valuable insights  
not only for individuals but also for training 
institutions or placement cells. 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:47 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

F.  Results 
Initially, the system employed custom-trained model s for 
both audio and video emotion recognition. For audio , a 
sequential model using YAMNet embeddings was trained on 
the RAVDESS dataset, achieving a test accuracy of 6 0.04%. 
However, the model suffered from overfitting and po or 
generalization in real-world settings due to ambient noise and 
subtle emotional variations. For video, a MobileNetV2 model 
trained on the FER2013 dataset achieved 77% accurac y on 
test data but faced challenges like facial occlusio ns and 
lighting inconsistencies during live use. 
Due to limitations encountered with the initial custom-trained 
models, the system pivoted to using robust pre-trai ned 
alternatives for improved real-world performance. F or audio 
emotion recognition, a pre-trained model from Hugging Face 
trained on the RAVDESS dataset was adopted, offerin g 
82.23% accuracy, faster inference, and better gener alization 
across varied audio inputs. For video emotion recognition, the 
system integrated Ollama’s Gemma3 model, which 
outperformed MobileNetV2 under diverse conditions 
including occlusions and variable lighting, and pro vided 
improved accuracy and real-time responsiveness. 
The system’s effectiveness, internal testing and li mited-user 
trials were conducted. In internal testing, the system achieved 
an average accuracy of 89% for technical answer eva luation 
and 84% accuracy in emotion detection. Speech emoti on 
recognition was particularly effective under contro lled noise 
conditions, and facial expression recognition achie ved high 
reliability under well-lit environments. 
A pilot test involving 20 participants was conducted to gather 
user feedback and validate real-world performance. Most 
participants reported an increase in their confiden ce after 
interacting with the system. Among them, 17 partici pants 
improved their performance scores by an average of 14% 
between their first and third mock interviews. The confidence 
scoring component, based on multimodal emotion 
recognition, aligned well with human evaluators in 81% of 
the cases.  
Extended trials with a larger group of 60 users showed a 17% 
overall improvement in both technical and confidenc e 
metrics across three sessions. The platform’s feedb ack loop 
enabled users to identify weak technical areas and improve 
articulation and emotional control, contributing to  
measurable performance growth. 
 
 
 
 
 
                 Fig. 3. Confusion Matrix for Audio Emotion Recognition Model 
                  Fig. 4. Confusion Matrix for Video Emotion Recognition Model 
IV.  METHODOLOGY  
The methodology follows a structures pipeline that begins 
with extraction of technical skills from the candidate’s resume 
and the provided job description. These skills form  the basis 
for generating personalized interview questions usi ng the 
Gemini API. Candidates respond using either text, speech, or 
video, and their responses are evaluated not only for technical 
accuracy but also for emotional confidence using au dio and 
facial emotion recognition models. The processed re sults are 
fused into composite score, stored in database, and  used to 
generate detailed feedback that helps track perform ance and 
guide improvement across sessions. 
A.  Input Processing and Skills Extraction 
         The system begins with the user uploading a resume and 
a job description in PDF format. These files are parsed using 
Python-based PDF parsing libraries to extract raw t ext 
content. Fuzzy string matching techniques are then applied to 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:25:47 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

identify and extract up to five relevant technical skills that 
overlap between the resume and the job description.  If no 
matches are found, the system defaults to generating domain-
based questions based on the provided job role  
B.  Adaptive Question Generation 
Once relevant skills are identified, the Gemini API is used 
to dynamically generate skill-specific technical questions. For 
each skill, the system first generates a basic-level question. If 
the candidate answers it correctly, follow-up quest ions of 
medium and high difficulty are presented. This prog ressive 
difficulty system helps in evaluating the depth of the 
candidate’s technical knowledge. 
C.  Multimodal Response Collection 
Candidates can respond to questions using text, speech, 
or video input: 
• Speech Input is transcribed using the Wav2Vec2 
model. The text is evaluated for technical correctness, 
and the audio signal is analyzed for emotional 
features. 
• Video Input is processed frame-by-frame. Facial 
expressions are analyzed to detect emotions such as  
nervousness, confidence, or neutrality. 
• Text Input is directly evaluated for content accura cy 
using comparison with Gemini-generated model 
answers 
A.  Emotion Recognition Pipeline  
                     Emotion recognition is carried  out using       
two independent pipelines:       
• Audio Emotion Recognition  is performed using a 
pre-trained Hugging Face model fine-tuned on the 
RAVDESS dataset. It detects emotions like calm, 
happy, sad, or angry and maps them to confidence 
scores. Performance analysis is visualized using 
graphs plotted with the Matplotlib library, 
highlighting variations in emotional tone across 
different responses. 
• Facial Emotion Recognition  uses Ollama’s 
Gemma3 model to identify facial expressions in 
each video frame. To optimize input for analysis, 
each candidate's video is divided into 16 evenly 
spaced frames, which are then stitched together into 
a single grid image. This grid is then passed into the 
model for emotion prediction. The model performs 
reliably even under occlusions or poor lighting. Like 
the audio pipeline, visual analytics for the facial  
emotion results are also generated using Matplotlib. 
D.  Evaluation & Scoring 
The candidate’s response to each question is evaluated in 
two parts: 
1.  Technical Evaluation  compares the answer with 
reference solutions generated by the Gemini API. 
2.  Confidence Assessment  combines scores from 
both the audio and facial emotion pipelines. 
A weighted composite score is calculated for each q uestion. 
The system also stores session-wise scores to monit or 
progress across multiple attempts. 
 
E.  Feedback Generation & Analytics 
Upon completion of the interview, a detailed feedba ck 
report is generated. This includes: 
• Question-wise scores 
• Detected emotional states and corresponding 
confidence scores 
• Summary of strengths and improvement areas 
All data is stored in a MongoDB database & Firebase , 
allowing the system to build performance trends ove r time 
and provide data-driven suggestions for improvement . This 
continuous feedback loop is central to helping cand idates 
enhance both their technical and communication skills 
 
 
V.  CONCLUSION AND FUTURE SCOPE  
This paper presents an AI-driven mock interview 
system that combines personalized question generati on with 
emotion and confidence analysis, simulating real-wo rld 
interview conditions. By integrating technical eval uation 
with audio and visual emotion recognition, the syst em 
provides a holistic feedback mechanism to improve candidate 
performance. It has shown significant improvements in both 
technical articulation and confidence through multi ple trial 
sessions. 
While the initial development faced challenges with  custom 
models, switching to robust pre-trained solutions h as greatly 
enhanced the platform's reliability. The system’s r eal-time 
interaction, multi-session tracking, and adaptive q uestioning 
contribute to a user-friendly and effective intervi ew 
preparation experience. 
Future developments aim to include real-time coaching using 
reinforcement learning, multilingual support, live coding 
environments for technical roles, and deeper behavi oral 
analytics. The system also plans to scale for use i n 
institutional training, positioning it as a compreh ensive tool 
for interview preparation

## [16] Integration of Vector Databases with Large Language Models (LLMs)
- **Authors:** Palak Sharma, Ayushman Singh Pundir, Gagan Dev Singh
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `Integration_of_Vector_Databases_with_Large_Language_Models_LLMs.pdf`

Integration of Vector Databases with Large Language 
Models (LLMs) 
 
               Palak Sharma1                            Ayushman Singh Pundir2                     Gagan Dev Singh3                                                       
Department of Information Technology          Department of Information Technology     Department of Information Technology 
Ajay Kumar Garg Engineering College                  Ajay Kumar Garg Engineering College              Ajay Kumar Garg Engineering College 
Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh, India                                  Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh, India                             Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh, India 
Sharmapalak2307@gmail.com                                       ayushman.puru111@gmail.com                                    gagan2111015@akgec.ac.in 
 
                                                                           Ruchi Gupta4 
                                                                                               Department of Information Technology 
                                                                               Ajay Kumar Garg Engineering College 
                                                                                             Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh, India 
                                                                                                           80ruchi@gmail.com 
 
Abstract—The fast-accelerating growth of unstructured and 
semi-structured data leads traditional relational and NoSQL 
databases to unsatisfactory performance of today’s queries sys- 
tem.This paper explores the advantages of using vector databases 
rather than using traditional databases for storing and main- 
taining the databases with Large Language Models(LLMs) to 
improve and increase the efficiency of the query retrieval.Vector 
embeddings are generated using Hugging Face models, while 
Google PaLM further refines the search process. To efficiently 
match queries, Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) is used. The 
results significantly demonstrates the improvements in query ac- 
curacy and retrieval speed, underlining the collaboration between 
vector databases and LLMs for data-intensive applications. 
Index Terms—LLMs, Artificial intelligence, vector databases, 
Hugging Face, Locality Sensitive Hashing. 
 
I. INTRODUCTION 
With the rapidly changing landscape of technology, the quest 
for appealing yet an effective query retrieval system is a 
must in terms of user interaction and information return. 
However, little attention has been paid to the fact that 
though traditional query processing methods have been 
effective to some degree, they continue to struggle with the 
complexity, adaptability, and rapidity inherent in user-
generated queries within our real-time environments. Recent 
technical developments have been shaping human life by 
rendering it easier as well as efficient. This technology 
revolution can be epitomized by the birth of the AI-powered 
algorithms, notably the Large Language Models(LLMs). These 
models are great at finding solutions to a variety of 
questions and presenting comprehensive answers based on 
their training data. They understand natural human language 
through languages like Python and Javascript to pass 
commands. This design grants general user access to them, 
causing a further attraction and increase in adaptability with a 
wider reach. 
 
Effective data retrieval and management are at the core 
of good model achievements in large-language models 
(LLMs). As AI tools, these models must remain as neutral 
as possible while solving problems. When a command is 
issued, they interpret the input by processing data within the 
space of their memory and generate possible solutions to 
the analysis. Notably, these models respond to the individual 
queries of many users positioned in different scenarios through 
evaluating each particular context. But, the effective 
implementation of this feature must depend on their retrieval 
and management of data. An LLM trained appropriately 
becomes a trusted platform, where users would turn for conflict 
resolution in real situations. Efficiency in data retrieval and 
management stands vital to the success of such models. 
 
As far as computer science is concerned, the upsurge in 
the adoption of vector databases is owed to Large Language 
Models (LLMs). These databases provide an efficient storage 
mechanism for information, usually in a three-dimensional 
(3D) vector form, allowing efficient data management and 
retrieval through the leveraging of their various advanced 
mathematical functions. 
 
The process begins with embedding. Data of various 
formats-such as texts, images, and videos-are transformed into 
numerical vector representations. The vectors are then stored 
in databases, forming a structure readily optimized for 
advanced data retrieval techniques. One of the main advantages 
of vector DBs is efficiency; there is not a lot of time involved 
in retrieving the data, no major processing. The retrieval works 
on the basis of a distance measure between vectors making it 
able to quickly locate the most relevant information. 
 
Moreover, embedding and storing keywords and related data 
in vectors allows these databases to improve the overall 
performance of LLMs. The approach significantly advances the 
accuracy, reliability, and validity of such responses as 
offered by such models. Consequently, the output of LLMs 
is not only correct and free from errors but is also 
comprehensible and easy for human interpretation. This 
combination of speed and accuracy and the user-friendly nature 
of the vector database makes it an essential tool for maximizing 
the potential of LLMs and for their effectiveness in solving 
real-world problems. 
2025 International Conference on Next Generation Information System Engineering (NGISE) | 979-8-3315-2059-5/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/NGISE64126.2025.11085087

II.  HISTORY OF LANGUAGE MODELS 
 
In the pursuit of Natural Language Processing and A rtificial 
Intelligence, improvements in Large Language Models focused 
attention into the ways of query processing. Over t he past 
decades, we have seen quite a number of improvement s in this 
arena; we have renderediable our understanding of q ueries, 
empowered to receive appropriate responses. This se ction 
portrays the passage of those advances, culling out  a 
transformation of query processing and understandin g. 
 
Beginning from the history of early language models, involving 
n-gram models and statistical language models were crucial in 
the early phase of development of machine translati on and 
speech recognition systems that were mainly based o n the 
statistical probabilities of word sequences. The ea rly language 
models had an inherent limitation due to their inab ility to 
understand complex linguistic relations, remain tied down only 
on simple conditions, differentiated either at inte rmediate or 
end stages, in the context of very long contexts. 
 
For language processing, the inception of neural ne twork- 
based models turned a critical corner. Recurrent Ne ural 
Network (RNNs) or Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) 
networks were introduced to overcome the canvas nat ure 
and are far better than the traditional approaches with respect 
to being tools for understanding sequential data an d 
contextual relationships. However, struggling with long 
sequences and being computationally expensive has l imited 
their applicability across scenarios. 
 
In 2017, language modeling was completely transformed by the 
introduction of transformer architecture. Transform ers are 
different because they try to use self-attention me chanisms to 
jointly process the whole text sequence, which allo ws them 
to capture context effectively. Groundbreaking desi gns like 
OpenAI’s GPT (Generative Pretrained Transformer) series and 
Google’s BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representation s from 
Transformer) were born out of this revolutionary foundation. 
 
BERT combines bidirectional training methodology in  one 
of its crowning contributions by letting it compreh end the 
context in which a word was used relative to all ne arby words. 
Because of this stride, BERT was very effective for  question 
answering and language inference issues, setting a new 
benchmark in natural language processing. 
 
The GPT series showed remarkable abilities to produce human-
like text and to construct contextually correlative  responses to 
questions asked. Built upon a huge-scale transforme r model 
and trained with very big data sets, it  could result in extensive 
pieces of coherent and contextually appropriate text. 
 
In recent developments, such as Google’s PaLM and 
Gemini, these possibilities have only increased. Pa LM’s 
gigantic scale and great training processes allow i t to master 
not only language comprehension but also generate r ich and 
nuanced text, making it suited for challenging query processing 
tasks. In contrast, Gemini’s multimodal capabilities allow it to 
understand text, images, and code. Such versatility  assists, 
especially regarding web development challenges in which 
questions may include written descriptions, images,  and code 
samples. 
 
Thus the advances in the area have greatly altered the way 
in which query processing is conducted. The languag e models 
have transitioned from simply predicting the next w ord of a 
sequence into understanding and responding to compl ex 
queries like a human being. This put paths 
 for emerging 
AI-powered tools such as virtual assistants, smart chatbots, and 
advanced search engines that provide an unprecedented level of 
accuracy and relevance in meeting user needs. 
 
III.  VECTOR DATABASES : AN OVERVIEW 
The entry of vector databases into information retr ieval 
has contributed greatly to AI and web development. This 
section covers the development and usage of vector databases, 
focusing on their point of contribution towards inf ormation 
retrieval in AI systems. 
 
Vector databases are databases for storing the data embeddings, 
which facilitate retrieval and similarity searches, hence a long-
term memory for the AI systems [23]. The vector dat abases 
support similarity operations, where the users just  explore the 
data corresponding to their query vector through si milarity 
calculation. 
 
Vector databases are structured for the storage of vector 
representations in an organized manner , ensuring o rderly 
management and rapid data retrieval. They support c ritical 
operations such as vector searches and similarity r ankings, 
which are required for a number of AI applications.Also, these 
databases also play a pivotal role in vector data-driven analysis 
and decision-making. In essence, vector databases s erve as the 
foundation for AI systems to handle and analyse vec tor data 
quickly and effectively. 
A.  How vector databases differ from traditional databa ses 
Reflectively , the breadth of the data has changed,  going 
beyond typical organized data that is easily stored in traditional 
databases. Today, data comprises a vast array of di fferent and 
complex types of information, which requires creative methods 
for its collection, storage, and analysis. Vector d atabases are 
designed to manage unstructured data represented as  vectors, 
whereas traditional databases are primarily designed to han- dle 
structured data organized in tables with spatial sc hemas [24]. 
While vector databases designed for the execution of similarity 
search operations employ specific query languages o r APIs, 
traditional databases often use SQL for querying. V ector 
databases place more emphasis on similarity search than 
traditional databases on matching specific values a nd 
performing relational operations. 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:41 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

 
 
 
 
Fig. 1. Traditional Database  
 
 
B.  Key features of Vector Database 
• Vector Embeddings 
Vector embedding are all mathematical representations of 
generic objects-such as words, sentences or other forms of 
data-in such a way that in high dimensional space r elated 
objects are mapped to vectors closely spaced togeth er. In 
particular, it captures the deep semantic relations hips and 
patterns within the data. 
 
Fig. 2. Vector Embedding Model  
 
• Enhance data retrieval efficiency 
LLMs commonly train on highly voluminous data, such  
as text corpora, embeddings, and other kinds of 
linguistic resources, while vector databases are us ed as 
effective mechanisms for the storage and access of these 
high-dimensional vector representations. Vector databases 
empower LLMs to retrieve contextual information dur ing 
operations by wrapping data in vector format and 
applying some special-purpose index scheme for 
similarity search.Other traditional 
 data-handling 
databases have difficulty managing high- dimensiona l 
data competently due to the so-called dimensionalit y. 
Interestingly, vector databases come to help with t hat 
challenge by using some purposes-built indexing 
techniques along with similarity algorithms, thus boosting 
data retrieval for LLMs. [25]. 
 
• Real-time search capabilities 
In simple terms, the Large Language Models, also referred 
to as the LLMs, require speedy access to a massive variety 
of languages and embeddings when used and interlink ed 
online. The vector databases are assisting in quick  and 
exact retrieval of the related data 
points, based on similarity metrics, allowing real- time 
search operations. These databases are vital for va rious 
functions, such as exploring similar text passages,  getting 
related embeddings, or finding related concepts. That said, 
real-time search, via vector databases, especially benefits 
applications such as chatbots, recommendation syste ms, 
and information retrieval systems. In this case, ra pid 
access to the right information is of utmost importance for 
operational efficiency. [26]. 
 
• Reduced latency and computational overhead 
Vector databases are designed to reduce delays and 
computational strain by optimizing how queries are 
processed and minimizing the time needed for simila rity 
searches. These databases store precomputed and indexed 
vector representations, which indicates that they d on’t 
have to search broadly through huge datasets and re sults 
in faster responses to queries and decreases computational 
expenses. Additionally, the distributed nature of v ector 
databases enable queries to be processed in paralle l, 
further reducing the delays and boosting overall sy stem 
efficiency. This lessening in delays and computatio nal 
burdens results in faster response times, improved system 
performance, and more well-organized usage of the 
resource. As a result, Large Language Models (LLMs) can 
operate more smoothly and cost- effectively. 
 
Basically, vector databases pose relevance to the L arge 
Language Models (LLMs) in terms of systematic retrieval 
of data, scaling it, benefiting from real- time sea rches, 
and finishing on time and with lower computational 
requirements. Basis on large-scale vector data is b eing 
batched by these databases as they break LLMs apart  for 
complex language processing systems across quite a 
number of other applications. 
 
IV.  METHODOLOGY  
Vector databases integrated with language models or  archi- 
tectures must deliberate on data preparation for em bedding 
generation, indexing, storage, query processing, an d real-time 
search capabilities. Let us analyze each of them in  detail and 
illustrate the entire process step by step with an example, 
further consolidated by a diagram to depict the flow. 
 
    Fig. 3. A sample framework that uses Vector Dat abases 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:41 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

 
 
A.  Integration of vector databases with LLMs 
Vector databases are combined with Large Language M od- 
els(LLMs) to enable efficient storage and retrieval  of high- 
dimensional vector representations of textual data.  With this 
combination, LLMs could perform complex language tasks like 
similarity searches, semantic analysis, and informa tion 
retrieval by using the capabilities of the Vector Database. With 
these databases, LLMs now have rapid access to pert inent 
information, so also they can read and understand l anguage 
better. 
 
Fig. 4. LLM pipeline  
 
 
B.  Data preprocessing and embedding generation 
Raw textual data undergoes various preprocessing fo llow 
ups, like tokenization, stemming, and removing stop  words, to 
clear and structure the text. Text that is pre-proc essed is trans- 
formed into dense vector embeddings by exploiting techniques 
like Woed2Vec, GloVe, or BERT. These embeddings cap ture 
the semantic and syntactic relationships between wo rds and 
phrases , forming the foundation for events such as  similarity 
search and allowing systems to better understand the context of 
the text. 
 
C.  Query Processing 
• Data Manipulation 
Embedding model can be inside or outside 
VDBMS/internal or external. In-system manipulation 
enables users to directly change the feature vector s with 
the assistance of the model [7], [8]. The collection appears 
more as a family of entities, rather than vectors, and 
manipulations of harvest proceed in the form of 
entities [5], [6]. The vector database provides the  
embedding model. 
 
• Query Variants 
Most VDBMS practically support predicated or ”hybri d” 
queries, while other types support batched queries and 
multi-vector queries for various application scenar ios, 
including  e-commerce,  facial  recognition,  and  text 
retrieval [9]–[11] . Accordingly, a more specific definition 
for hybrid queries would be: The collection vector set is 
related to the structured attributes of the entity represented 
by them, and each vector in the answer set should a lso 
satisfy Boolean predicates on firmly defined attrib utes. 
Batched queries collect several queries and feed th em at 
once to the VDBMS for processing; hence, the entire batch 
must be executed under the same engine process. Som e 
techniques have been suggested with the potential t o 
monetize similarities between queries to expedite b atch 
processing [10], [12]. Essentially multi-vector queries use 
more than one feature vector for either the query o r 
each of the entities [10]. Further classification o f 
components of multi-vector queries contains details  on 
various optional aspects, as each one of these mult iple 
feature vectors may allow the aggregation of the 
information through scoring. 
 
• Basic search queries 
The (c, k)-search query is aimed at retrieving a se t of 
k vectors that are nearest to the query vector, and  no 
retrieved vector can be allowed to have a similarit y score 
that is worse by a factor c than that of the best n onzero 
score. A range query employs a pre-specified simila rity 
threshold. Thus one would like to return all vectors whose 
similarity scores do not exceed the specified thres hold. 
Particular cases of (c, k)-search query have, for i nstance, 
been studied for c=0,k >1for k-NN query, and c >0,k >1 
for ANN query. Such queries have continued to be re - 
searched independently and have served as the inspiration 
for a plethora of techniques and theoretical outcomes [13]–
[15]. 
 
Fig. 5. Vector Database with LLM  
 
 
D.  Locality Sensitive Hashing 
In the querying process, the query vector is serial ised into 
these tables: any vector hashing to the same bucket as the query 
vector is a possible candidate to match with the sa me query 
vector. The parameters L and K into hash division a llow the 
fine-tuning of the trade-off between accuracy and performance 
while providing guarantees and error bounds 
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:41 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

 
 
for the ANN search. Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH ) is a 
typology for managing similarities in vectors—althou gh each 
vector is treated differently according to a collection of random 
hash functions.Many hash families are known, with d ifferent 
performance and accuracy properties, such as random  
hyperplane in E2LSH [16], binary projection in IndexLSH [1], 
and overlapping spheres in FALCONN [17], [18]. 
 
V.  CHALLENGES AND FUTURE WORK  
A.  Are vector searches all you need? 
Vector databases offer a cost-effective and efficient means to 
enhance information retrieval in LLM-based systems.  How- 
ever, their adoption in traditional relational data base opera- 
tions remains limited due to the immaturity of vect or search 
methods for tasks like post-query filtering, full-text search, and 
exact keyword matching—core functions in conventiona l 
DBMSs. While vector search excels in semantic retri eval, it 
falls short in scenarios requiring precise transact ional or 
keyword-based queries. Conversely, traditional data bases are 
robust in managing structured data and transactiona l opera- 
tions. This gap highlights the need for hybrid search algorithms 
that combine the semantic strengths of VecDBs with the 
precision and reliability of traditional relational systems. 
 
B.  Data management systems for LLMs with multi-tenancy  
Under these conditions, both vector databases and L LMs 
seem to encounter issues in minimizing data breaches amongst 
tenants, in an effort to provide isolation and conf identiality of 
data of different tenants while still focusing on efficiency, care 
being taken to ensure that isolation serves privacy and security. 
Yet, one should be equally mindful of the need for dynamic 
sharing of resources so that assignment and de allo cation of 
resources could be done flexibly. 
 
C.  Cost-effective and scalable data storage and retrie val 
Although modern vector search algorithms are based on 
memory, the memory resources available in the cloud  are 
deemed expensive and volatile. Since there are billions of data 
points, relying solely upon memory resources may no t  be 
financially feasible. Disk-based search algorithms suffer 
performance degradation such that they quite often are no 
longer applicable in real-time scenarios with strin gent latency 
requirements. This requires the performance of LLM appli- 
cations across their life cycles to be realized in a more cost- 
effective manner. 
 
VI.  CONCLUSION  
In conclusion, the integration of Large Language Mo dels 
(LLMs) with Vector Databases (VecDBs) marks a signi ficant 
advancement in the field of intelligent data proces sing and 
retrieval. This study has offered a comprehensive o verview 
of recent innovations, highlighting the motivations  behind 
coupling LLMs with VecDBs and their relevance acros s 
various applications. By examining multiple prototy pes and 
classification frameworks, the research illustrates  how these 
hybrid systems are being adopted across disciplines  to en- 
hance performance, scalability, and semantic unders tanding. 
Looking ahead, the field presents promising prospects, but not 
without its challenges—ranging from data security an d model 
interpretability to system efficiency and cost. Con tinued 
multidisciplinary research and collaboration will b e vital to 
overcoming these hurdles and unlocking the full pot ential of 
LLM-VecDB synergy in next-generation intelligent systems.
