## [19] Reducing Stress in Annual Performance Reviews through AI-Enabled Avatar Interaction
- **Authors:** Yoto Mizuno, Richard Roth, Maxime Cordeil, Nilufar Baghaei
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `Reducing_Stress_in_Annual_Performance_Reviews_through_AI-Enabled_Avatar_Interaction.pdf`

Reducing Stress in Annual Performance Reviews through AI-Enabled
Avatar Interaction
Y oto Mizuno*
The University of Queensland, Australia
Richard Roth†
The University of Queensland, Australia
Maxime Cordeil‡
The University of Queensland, Australia
Nilufar Baghaei§
The University of Queensland, Australia
ABSTRACT
Virtual reality (VR) has gained significant attention in recent years
as a potential tool to improve interview skills for employment. In-
terviewees facing challenges during the job application process,
especially those with high anxiety, can benefit from VR-based train-
ing platforms that simulate interview scenarios. We investigated
the integration of VR avatar design and Generative Artificial Intelli-
gence (GAI) in the context of annual performance reviews (APR).
The study evaluated user experiences across three APR settings: a
face-to-face APR without VR, a realistic AI-enabled avatar in a VR
environment, and a stylised AI-enabled avatar in a VR environment.
The VR avatars were connected to a GAI system, which facilitated
audio-based interactions during the APR process.
A within-subject experiment involving 20 participants compared
these settings through questionnaires, assessing system usability,
sense of presence, ease of self-disclosure, intimacy, stress levels
and acceptance of opinions. The results showed that in-person
APR was preferred for self-disclosure, intimacy, and acceptance
of opinions during the performance evaluation. VR APRs with
realistic and stylised avatars received positive usability feedback
and were favoured for reducing stress and pressure. Participants
provided qualitative feedback emphasising the need to improve the
VR avatars’ responses, facial expressions, and eye contact.
The findings suggest that VR APR systems hold promise as an
alternative or complementary approach to traditional methods, offer-
ing potential benefits such as reducing stress for employees during
performance evaluations.
Keywords: Virtual Reality (VR), Generative Artificial Intelligence
(GAI), Generative AI, User Experience (UX), Avatar Interactions,
Annual Performance Reviews (APR), Stress Reduction
Index Terms: H.5.1 [Information Interfaces and Presentation]:
Multimedia Information Systems—Artificial, augmented, and vir-
tual realities; H.5.2 [Information Interfaces and Presentation]: User
Interfaces—User studies; I.2.7 [Artificial Intelligence]: Natural Lan-
guage Processing—Text analysis; J.4 [Social and Behavioral Sci-
ences]: Psychology—Cognitive modeling; K.4.3 [Computers and
Society]: Organizational Impacts—Computer-mediated communi-
cation; I.6.8 [Simulation and Modeling]: Types of Simulation—
Animation
1 I NTRODUCTION
Virtual Reality (VR) technology has made significant advancements
in recent years, revolutionising various sectors and industries [1, 5–
*e-mail: y.mizuno@uq.net.au
†e-mail:richard.roth@uq.edu.au
‡e-mail:m.cordeil@uq.edu.au
§e-mail:n.baghaei@uq.edu.au
7, 10, 14, 17, 27, 28]. Despite promising development, the integration
of VR into everyday life of people remains limited. One potential
application area with potential to impact productivity is human
resource management, particularly for Annual Performance Reviews
(APR).
An APR is a structured assessment conducted by an employer
to evaluate an employees job performance over the course of the
year, which can include looking at accomplishments, strengths and
areas of improvement. This process typically involves setting ob-
jectives, tracking progress, and providing constructive feedback to
facilitate professional growth and alignment toward organisational
goals. APR also gathers insights into an employee’s circumstances
and considers potential personnel changes and adjustments to the
working environment for the upcoming year [2]. Considering the
recent developments in generative artificial intelligence (GAI) tech-
nology, exploration of this new medium could lead to enhancements
to the APR process. This paper aims to assess the effectiveness
of APR within a VR environment using 3D human avatar models
connected to a GAI application programming interface (API), as a
replacement or complement for human-led APR.
Utilising VR and GAI for APR holds the potential to elevate the
quality of the performance review process and enhance standardisa-
tion and objectivity [9]. This could encourage employees to share
information that is difficult to discuss in person, such as preference
for job rotations, family-related situations, feelings of inequity in
workload, and dissatisfaction with colleagues. APR within a VR
environment with a GAI connected avatar can effectively collect
employee circumstances and information, potentially reducing per-
sonnel placement mismatches.
The future of APR may be virtual and this paper aims to ex-
plore how it could be conducted by a supervisor in three modalities
including; face-to-face as a control, a realistic avatar in a virtual
environment and a stylised avatar in a virtual environment.
2 R ELATED WORK
Virtual reality has gained significant attention in recent years as
a potential tool to improve interview skills for employment [18].
Interviewees facing challenges during the job application process,
especially those with high anxiety, benefit from VR-based training
platforms that simulate interview scenarios [22].
Hartholt et al. showed that introducing a VR-based training
system to support those suffering from high-anxiety using virtual
human characters in diverse interview scenarios led to positive out-
comes for participants [15]. This system’s design is promising, but
Hartholt et al. emphasises the need for further research to validate
its effectiveness in real-world scenarios.
Similar techniques were utilised by Adiani et al., developing a VR
employment interview training platform to address the employment
challenges for individuals on the autism spectrum with the aim of
creating less anxiety-inducing environments to practice interview
skills [3]. In contrast, Smith et al. assessed the feasibility and ef-
fectiveness of virtual reality employment interview training, finding
that role-play within the virtual environment lead to an increase in
545
2025 IEEE International Symposium on Mixed and Augmented Reality Adjunct (ISMAR-Adjunct)
2771-1110/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE
DOI 10.1109/ISMAR-Adjunct68609.2025.00110
2025 IEEE International Symposium on Mixed and Augmented Reality Adjunct (ISMAR-Adjunct) | 979-8-3315-9347-6/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/ISMAR-Adjunct68609.2025.00110
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:44 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

self-confidence among participants [25].
Self-disclosure plays a crucial role in APR [24]. VR environ-
ments provide a unique context for self-disclosure, allowing indi-
viduals to share personal information with virtual agents or avatars.
Maloney et al. explored user behaviour and perceptions related to
self-disclosure and privacy in social VR environments, proposing
design implications to address privacy concerns and to create safer
social VR environments [19].
Ichino et al. investigated the impact of avatars on self-disclosure
and reciprocity in virtual environments, finding that avatars with-
out similarity encouraged self-disclosure, but both types of avatars
facilitated reciprocity [16]. The study underscores the influence of
avatar design on self-disclosure and highlights the value of avatars
to understand the thoughts and emotions of users.
The personality of virtual agents plays a crucial role in eliciting
trust and self-disclosure. Zhou et al. developed AI interviewers with
distinct personalities to investigate their influence on trust in users,
showing that users were more willing to confide in AI interviewers
with specific personalities in high-risk contexts [29].
These studies provide valuable insights into the application of
VR technology for job interviews and self-disclosure in VR environ-
ments, however, there is a gap in the research, specifically focusing
on the utilisation of VR technology for annual performance reviews.
Our study aims to bridge this gap by exploring the design of effective
VR-based APR systems that encourage self-disclosure.
3 M ETHODOLOGY
The experimental design and methodology used to compare user
experiences of APR were split into three modalities; a face-to-face
APR conducted by an interviewer, a realistic avatar powered by
GAI conducting APR in a virtual environment and a stylised avatar
powered by GAI conducting APR in a virtual environment.
3.1 Experimental Design
The experiment was designed to assess the usability of the VR
system for APR and to compare factors including self-disclosure, in-
timacy, stress levels, and acceptance of participants’ opinions across
the three APR modalities. A within-subject design was employed,
where each participant experienced the three APR situations. Ethics
approval was obtained from the University of Queensland Human
Ethics Committee.
Figure 1: Image of the interviewers of the three APR modalities; face-
to-face (left), realistic GAI avatar (centre), and stylised GAI avatar
(right)
3.2 Participants
The experiment involved 20 participants (6 females) aged 18 and
over with some form of industry or work experience. A significant
number of participants were Masters and Doctoral students at the
University of Queensland, however there was diversity in level of
work, and VR experience.
3.3 Protocol
A simple protocol was used with participants to ensure conformity
between each test. The protocol was ordered into four steps; briefing,
face-to-face APR, VR avatar APR and questionnaires.
1. Briefing: Participants were informed about the purpose of the
study and the three APR interviews they would experience.
They were asked to provide informed consent.
2. Face-to-Face APR: Participants engaged in the APR with a
human interviewer without wearing a VR headset. They were
asked a set of questions and provided responses.
3. VR Avatar APR: Participants were randomly assigned to one of
two groups, first experiencing the realistic avatar APR (Figure
1: centre) or the stylised avatar APR (Figure 1: right), followed
by the other condition.
4. Questionnaires: After each APR interview, participants com-
pleted questionnaires assessing system usability, ease of self-
disclosure, intimacy, stress levels, acceptance of their opinions,
and presence within the VR environment.
After completing the protocol, participants were given the follow-
ing questionnaire with the prompt ’Answer the following questions
based on your experiences in your current or past workplace.’.
Table 1: Questionnaire after completing APRs
Q# Question
1 Please provide an overview of your performance
this year, such as any significant achievements and
challenges.
2 Compared to your colleagues, do you think the
amount of work you do is fair? If not, please de-
scribe how to make it fair.
3 What are your future career goals, and do you have
specific plans for career development?
4 Do you have any opinions or feedback about your
workplace or supervisor? Please share any points
that you think should be improved.
3.4 Measures
The following measures were used to evaluate the user experience
and compare the three APR interviews. Participants were requested
to complete questionnaires from Q1 to Q21, as described in the
tables below.
3.4.1 System Usability Scale (SUS):
The System Usability Scale (SUS) originally developed by Brooke
was used to assess the usability of the VR system for APR. The SUS
consists of 10 questions rated on a 5-point Likert scale, ranging from
”Strongly disagree” to ”Strongly agree” [8].
3.4.2 SUS Presence Questionnaire:
The Slater-Usoh-Steed (SUS) Presence Questionnaire was used to
evaluate participants’ sense of presence in the VR environment
as shown (Table 3). This questionnaire, developed by Usoh et al.
consists of six questions rated on a 7-point scale and assesses the
extent to which users experience the virtual environment as if it were
real [26].
546
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:44 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

3.4.3 Preference Measurement:
To measure ease of self-disclosure, intimacy, stress levels, and ac-
ceptance of participants’ opinions; participants were asked to rank
the three APR interviews (face-to-face, realistic avatar, and stylised
avatar) from the most preferred to the least preferred based on the
criteria as shown in Table 4 below. This measurement was adopted
from Norouzi et al. and modified for this experiment by asking
participants to rank the three APR situations for various criteria [21].
Table 2: Questionnaire for Preference Measurement
Q# Question
Q1 Please rank the APR in terms of Ease of Self-
Disclosure (how easy it is to open and share your-
self).
Most preferred = 1, Least preferred = 3
Q2 Please rank the APR in terms of Intimacy (degree of
closeness you feel to the APR conductors).
Most preferred = 1, Least preferred = 3
Q3 Please rank the APR in terms of Stress (degree of
stress and pressure in the APR).
Most preferred = 1, Least preferred = 3
Q4 Please rank the APR in terms of Acceptance of Your
Opinions.
Most preferred = 1, Least preferred = 3
Preference Measurement:Participants provided optional feed-
back about their impressions of the experiment and areas for im-
provement by Q21.
3.5 Implementation of VR Environment and Avatars
The VR environment and APR interviewers using 3D human models
were implemented using Unreal Engine 5.3 and Meta Quest2 [13,20].
The realistic avatar was modeled after the researcher using Polycam,
Blender, and MetaHuman, while the stylised avatar was provided
by a GAI system called Convai [4, 11, 12, 23]. The VR avatars were
connected to the Convai GAI system via an API to enable audio
input and output.
4 R ESULTS
This section presents the results of the experiment conducted to
compare the user experience of an Annual Performance Review
(APR) with and without a Virtual Reality (VR) environment.
4.1 System Usability Scale (SUS) Results
The average SUS score for the realistic avatar (Figure 1: centre) was
76.0, while the average SUS score for the stylised avatar (Figure 1:
right) was 77.25, indicating good usability for both VR avatars, with
the stylised avatar slightly outperforming the realistic avatar.
4.2 Slater-Usoh-Steed (SUS Presence) Results
The average count of SUS presence score 6 or above for the realistic
avatar (Figure 1: centre) was 4.83, while the average count for
the stylised avatar (Figure 1: right) was 3.33. This indicates that
participants experienced a higher sense of presence with the realistic
avatar compared to the stylised avatar.
4.3 Preference Rankings
The face-to-face APR was most preferred for ease of self-disclosure,
intimacy, and acceptance of opinions, while the VR avatars were
preferred for reducing stress and pressure.
Figure 2: SUS Score of Each Participant for Realistic Avatar (Figure
1: centre)
Figure 3: SUS Score of Each Participant for Stylised Avatar (Figure 1:
right)
4.4 Qualitative Feedback
Participants provided feedback on their experiences, highlighting
areas for improvement in five of the VR avatars’ responses includ-
ing, facial expressions, and eye contact. They also found the VR
avatars more comfortable to talk to compared to the face-to-face
APR. Participants noted the similarity between the realistic and
stylised avatars.
Figure 4: Count of SUS Presence score 6 or above
5 D ISCUSSION
The results of the experiment demonstrate that the VR system for
APR received positive feedback in terms of usability, acceptance,
547
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:44 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

and sense of presence. The realistic and stylised avatars performed
well, with slight variations in specific aspects.
The preference rankings revealed that the face-to-face APR was
most preferred for ease of self-disclosure, intimacy, and acceptance
of opinions, while the VR avatars were preferred for reducing stress
and pressure. The qualitative feedback provided valuable insights
into participants’ experiences and highlighted areas for improvement,
such as enhancing the VR avatars’ responses, facial expressions, and
eye contact.
These findings suggest that the VR system for APR has the poten-
tial to be a valuable tool in the human resources domain, offering an
alternative or complementary approach to traditional APR methods.
There are, however, some aspects that may improve the experiment
and the VR system for APR which are listed below.
5.1 Influence of the APR Order
One possible aspect that may have influenced the responses of the
participants is the order in which the three different APRs were
taken. A t-test: Two-Sample Assuming Unequal Variances was
conducted to investigate the influence of the APR order on partic-
ipants’ responses. The results suggest that the experimental order
may have influenced the responses for some questions, particularly
in the SUS Presence questionnaire. One possible reason for the
influence of the experimental order is that the participants may have
become accustomed to the same VR environment and identical APR
questions.
5.2 Similarity of Realistic and Stylised Avatars
A noteworthy finding is the similarity in participants’ responses
to the realistic and stylised avatars. Both the System Usability
Scale and Slater-Usoh-Steed (SUS Presence) scores showed similar
trends for the realistic avatar and stylised avatar, both in terms of
overall scores and individual question scores. In the preference
rankings, multiple respondents gave the same rank to these two
avatars. This may be attributed to the fact that factors other than
the visual differences between the avatars were kept as identical as
possible to examine the impact of the visual differences.
5.3 Limitations
This study had two main limitations. First, the implementation of
VR avatars requires further refinement, particularly in relation to
achieving more natural eye contact and facial expressions. Second,
the sample was relatively small, which may limit the generalizability
of the findings.
5.4 Future Directions
Future research could explore different APR orders and participant
groupings, implement various VR avatars and environments such
as changing the sex of the avatar, introducing animal companions,
changing the avatar’s voice, facial expressions, personality, and
altering avatar’s responses. Conducting text analysis of the APR
content is also suggested to examine how accurately participants
responded to the APR with VR avatars. This can be recorded with
audio and converted to text to record how participants change their
responses in APR with VR avatars compared to the face-to-face
APR.
6 C ONCLUSION
This study investigated the use of VR avatars connected to GAI in
order to conduct Annual Performance Reviews. The experiment
compared user experiences in three different APR settings: face-to-
face APR without a VR environment, realistic avatar conducting an
APR in a VR environment, and a stylised avatar conducting an APR
in a VR environment.
The results demonstrated that the VR system for APR received
positive feedback in terms of usability, with both realistic and
stylised avatars performing well. The preference rankings revealed
that the face-to-face APR was most preferred for ease of self-
disclosure, intimacy, and acceptance of opinions, while the VR
avatars were preferred for reducing stress and pressure. Qualitative
feedback provided valuable insights into participants’ experiences
and highlighted areas for improvement.
The study contributes to the domain and research on the applica-
tion of VR technology in human resource management. The findings
suggest that VR-based APR systems have the potential to be a valu-
able tool, offering an alternative or complementary approach to
traditional APR methods. Further research and development are nec-
essary to fully harness the benefits of VR technology in this context
and create more effective and engaging VR-based APR experiences.

## [20] Structured Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multi-Entity Question Answering over Heterogeneous Sources
- **Authors:** Teng LIN
- **Year:** 2024
- **Filename:** `Structured_Retrieval-Augmented_Generation_for_Multi-Entity_Question_Answering_over_Heterogeneous_Sources.pdf`

Structured Retrieval-Augmented Generation for
Multi-Entity Question Answering over
Heterogeneous Sources
1st Teng LIN
DSA Thrust
HKUST(GZ)
Guangzhou, China
tlin280@connect.hkust-gz.edu.cn
Abstract—Conventional RAG approaches, though effective for
general tasks, often fail to retrieve precise information for
complex questions involving multiple entities, which is called
Multi-Entity question answering (QA), such as “How are all
IEEE Fellows distributed across distinct research domains by
2024?”. To bridge this gap, we propose an innovative struc-
tured retrieval-augmented generation framework that includes
semantic KG retrieval driven by large language model (LLM)
and a table generation module, which transforms unstructured
data into relational table structures. This structured format
enables efficient querying via advanced table-based methods,
ensuring high precision for intricate, entity-dense questions.
Complementing our approach, we develop a tailored benchmark
to rigorously assess performance of RAG systems for Multi-
Entity QA. Experimental evaluations reveal that our system
outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 11.6% in accuracy,
demonstrating the critical role of structured database technology
in improving RAG systems for knowledge-intensive applications.
This work paves the way for integrating database principles
with generative AI to tackle real-world QA challenges requiring
precise, multifaceted reasoning.
Index Terms—LLM-Driven Database Retrieval, Structured
RAG, Table Generation, Multi-Entity Question Answering
I. I NTRODUCTION
Recent advancements in Retrieving-Augmented Generation
(RAG) have significantly transformed the manner in which
language models access and utilize external knowledge, show-
casing remarkable capabilities across a diverse array of appli-
cations, including question answering and document synthe-
sis [1]. These systems have achieved superior performance
by integrating sophisticated retrieval mechanisms with robust
language models, demonstrating notable efficacy over the
past few years. Nonetheless, a persistent challenge remains
in accurately obtaining batch of entities information from
external knowledge bases, particularly when dealing with
multiple documents and diverse data sources. This issue is
especially pronounced in the context of what is termed Multi-
Entity Question Answering (MEQA), where the complexity of
the task is exacerbated by the need to integrate and synthe-
size information from multiple documents and sources, with
inquiries like “What are the capitals of countries bordering
France?” or “How many Turing Award Winners are Canadian”
( the query Q in Figure 1-a). This question requires extracting
information of multiple documents to answer unless there is
special statistical work that has been done on a webpage,
which is not a common situation. In MEQA, the challenge
lies not only in recognizing and extracting relevant entities
precisely from data but also in understanding the properties of
these entities within the context of the query.
Existing RAG methods struggle with MEQA tasks, because
useful information required to these tasks is badly scattered.
This characteristic makes it difficult for existing RAG methods
to accurately identify key information and perform global
reasoning.
To address the challenge of aggregating multi-entity infor-
mation from heterogeneous sources, we propose an innovative
Structured Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework
which includes two main parts: (1) LLM-Driven Knowledge
Graph Retrieval System as illustrated in Figure 1-a, and
(2) Structured Question Answer (SQA) as illustrated in
Figure 1-c, to effectively address multi-entity QA. The data
we used for experiments are from Wikipedia.
a) Contributions: Our notable contributions are summa-
rized as follows.
• MEBench: Specialized Benchmark for MEQA. We
curate MEBench, a standardized benchmark based on
Wikipedia to evaluate the effectiveness of various ap-
proaches to address the complexities of information ex-
traction and reasoning involving multiple entities.
• LLM-Driven Knowledge Graph Retrieval. Multi-entity
Semantic Retrieval innovatively enhances semantic re-
trieval by integrating language models with structured
Graph. This integration enables the creation of precise
SPARQL queries to effectively retrieve relevant entities
and web pages from very large data sources such as Wiki
data and Internet. The method’s key contribution is its
ability to improve retrieval accuracy by leveraging the
strengths of both language models and structured data
verification.
• SQA: Module of Structuring Entity Information and
Reasoning. We propose the SQA, a module for managing
vast and unstructured data by extracting attributions of
253
2025 IEEE 41st International Conference on Data Engineering Workshops (ICDEW)
2473-3490/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE
DOI 10.1109/ICDEW67478.2025.00036
2025 IEEE 41st International Conference on Data Engineering Workshops (ICDEW) | 979-8-3315-9959-1/25/$31.00 ©2025 IEEE | DOI: 10.1109/ICDEW67478.2025.00036
Authorized licensed use limited to: NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY TIRUCHIRAPALLI. Downloaded on February 28,2026 at 15:26:42 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 

entities and organizing information into structured tables
with a rigorous and accurate schema, which facilitates
analysis. Our experiments demonstrate its remarkable
performance, achieving SOTA results and outperforming
the strongest baselines by 11.6% in overall accuracy,
while leading across all eight tasks of Multi-Entity QA.
II. R ELATED WORK
A. Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Advanced RAG systems have evolved to incorporate pre-
retrieval, retrieval, and post-retrieval strategies aimed at miti-
gating the limitations of basic RAG methods. In parallel, Mod-
ular RAG systems have introduced frameworks for iterative
and dynamic cycles of intertwined retrieval and generation
processes [2]. Community summaries serve as a form of
self-memory [3], enhancing generation-augmented retrieval to
support future cycles of information generation. Additionally,
the parallel generation of community answers from these
summaries reflects an iterative [4] or federated [5] retrieval-
generation approach. Similar concepts have been amalgamated
in other systems for tasks such as multi-document summariza-
tion [6] and multi-hop question answering [7]. Furthermore,
using a hierarchical index and summarization aligns with
alternative methods, such as generating a hierarchical index
of text chunks through vector clustering [8] or constructing
a “tree of clarifications” to address multiple interpretations of
ambiguous questions [9]. These methods concentrate on se-
mantic aggregation and overlook structured views of multiple
entities.
B. Graph RAG
Recently, several studies have explored the incorporation
of graph structures into RAG systems to enhance LLMs in
addressing complex question-answering tasks [10] [11]. One
approach involves utilizing pre-existing knowledge graphs,
from which subgraphs are extracted based on specific queries.
These subgraphs are then encoded as soft prompts or converted
into plain text for the generation module GraphGPT [12]
and other methods [13] [14]. Another method focuses on
extracting entity-relation triples from relevant text documents
according to query needs, allowing for the construction of
graph structures that facilitate knowledge augmentation, like
GraphRAG [10] and RQ-RAG [15]. These models are inade-
quate for addressing numerous issues in graph-based applica-
tions, such as structured views of multiple entities. In response,
we introduce a comprehensive Multi-Entities QA benchmark
MEBench based on Wikipedia Graph and provide a first-of-
its-kind original system Structured RAG.
III. P ROBLEM STATEMENT
A. Wikipedia Graph
Wikipedia graph is represented as G=(V , E, P), where
V = {v1, v2, ..., vn} is the set of nodes in the graph, with
each node (vi ∈ V ) representing an entity; E is the set
of direct edges in the graph, with each edge (ej(vi, vk) ∈
E) representing a connection (or relationships) between two
nodes. An edge is a tuple (va, vb, t), where (va, vb ∈
V ) and t is the type of relationship. For example, E =
{(v1, v2, t1), (v2, v3, t2), ...,(vm, vn, tk)}. P represents the set
of properties associated with both nodes and edges. Figure 2
is a sample of a Wikipedia graph snippet.
B. Multi-Entities Question
A Multi-Entities Question can be formally defined as q =
(y, Vq, Pq), where y is the type of query. Y = {y1, y2, ..., y8}
is the set of types. Details can be seen in Table I. Vq is
the entity set in which the user is interested. Pq is the set
of properties relevant to the query, potentially involving both
entity and relationship properties. Q is set of queries, where
q ∈ Q.
IV. S YSTEM DESIGN
In our system, the LLM-Driven Knowledge Graph Retrieval
involves conducting a SPARQL synthesis retrieval across the
Wikipedia graph to obtain relevant knowledge. Secondly, in
SQA module, Table generation begins with “guessing” a table
schema based on the given query, followed by the extraction
of information from the identified entities to populate the
table. Finally, we implement an executor that processes the
generated table to respond to the specified query. Next, we
will elaborate on the details of each step.
A. SPARQL Synthesis Retrieval
The SPARQL query generated by LLM (we use GPT-
4) contains entity and property spans. To make the query
executable, we deploy LLM as the Semantic Analysis Model
to identify entities and properties within user queries, replacing
the spans with entity and property, as illustrated in Figure 1-
a2. Our system retrieves entities by identifying the unique
entity and property in Wiki Graph iteratively. Consequently,
our system is capable of performing entity and property
identification without ambiguities. Next, the query is executed
on Wikidata to retrieve the results, which serve as the final
supporting knowledge. For multi-hop queries, the Semantic
Analysis process initially deconstructs the queries into sub-
queries, allowing SPARQL synthesis step to be applied se-
quentially to each sub-query to identify entities and properties
until all sub-queries have been processed.
B. Table Generation
SPARQL provides aggregation functions such as SUM,
A VG, COUNT, etc. But they are insufficient for complex
statistical problems. Therefore, we build tables instead of
extended graphs to support more complex algorithms and
analysis. The table generation consists of two steps: (1)
generate the schema of table; (2) extract entity to fill the table.
Schema Generation. We use GPT-4 to generate attributions
for the entities which can be interpreted as the table schema to
construct the table,which is based on the query. These gener-
ated properties may necessitate adjustments or refinements to
align more closely with the intent of the input query. For ex-
ample, for the query “How many Nobel Prizes in Physics lau-
reates have been awarded for discoveries in Particle Physics?”,

Fig. 1. An Overview of Multi-entity QA Solutions over Wikipedia. (a) LLM-Driven Knowledge Graph Retrieval: In a1, a rough SPARQL query is generated
using a LLM (we use GPT-4). In a2, we integrate the LLM’s semantic parsing with Wikipedia’s API and utilize verifiable query accuracy on structured
Wikidata to accurately identify entity (Turing Award) and property (Award with). In step a3, we synthesize an exact SPARQL query. Finally, in a4, the refined
SPARQL query is used to retrieve the relevant entities (e.g., Yoshua Bengio) and their web pages. (b)Existing Solutions: b1 represents direct responses from
LLMs, while b2 combines LLMs with RAG. (c) Our proposal: SQA. Initially, in step c1, a LLM (GPT-4) is employed to analyze the question and determine
the table schema. In c3, we utilize the structured information extraction module to populate the table. Finally, in step c4, A TableQA module is used to derive
the final answer.

Fig. 2. Illustration of a Wikipedia graph snippet.
the LLM produces a schema (name, YearAwarded, field) ,
there are two issues , first is that (field) is oversimplified, it
should be (field in Physics) . The second issue is that columns
YearAwarded are redundant. Therefore, We prompt GPT-4
to critically review content to minimize over-simplification,
omission of essential elements, and redundancy.
Entity Information Extraction. In our data processing
workflow, we use open source LLM Mistral-7B to extract
information from retrieved data, populating a table where
each row represents a unique entity, as shown in Figure 1-c3.
This transforms entities into structured tables, enabling easier
access, manipulation, and analysis.
C. Executor
We use GPT-4 to create SQL query, as it was exposed to
SQL during pre-training, enhancing its ability to generate SQL
queries. To improve accuracy, we include relevant information
in the prompt, such as the table schema and data samples. The
generated SQL queries are executed directly on the generated
table to obtain results, which could be a single value or a
subset of table.

TABLE I
EXAMPLES OF MULTI -ENTITIES QUERIES .
categories Types Examples
Comparison Intercomparison Which has more ACM fellow, UK or USA?
Superlative Which city has the highest population?
Statistics
Aggregation How many ACM fellow are from MIT?
Distribution Compliance Does the nationality of ACM fellows follow a normal distribution?
Correlation Analysis Is there a linear relationship between number of events and records
broken in Olympic Games?
Variance Analysis Do the variances in the number of participating countries and total
events in the Summer Olympics differ significantly?
Relationship
Descriptive Relationship Is there a relationship between the year of ACM fellowship
induction and the fellows’ areas of expertise?
Hypothetical Scenarios If China wins one more gold medal, will it overtake the US in
the gold medal tally at the 2024 Olympics?

#-Queries 3406 1374 4780

V. E XPERIMENT
A. Dataset
MEBench Benchmark. Multi-entity questions [16] extend
the focus to consider the interactions and relationships between
multiple entities simultaneously. Table I shows examples of
multi-entity queries in three categories with eight types. A
total of 4,780 questions are divided between two datasets: the
test set, with about 30% or 1,374 questions, and the training set
with 3,406 questions. Table II provides MEBench benchmark
statistics. We utilize this benchmark as our experimental
dataset. And We use the test set of MEBench for evaluation.
B. Experiment Setup
Baselines. For base LLM, we select the widely acknowl-
edged GPT model GPT-4 [17], which have been extensively
adopted in various applications. Additionally, we incorporate
RAG across the vanilla baseline model for comparative anal-
ysis and evaluation of the model’s capacity to integrate and
leverage external data sources. We also select advance RAG
methods for MEQA tasks as baselines. RQ-RAG [15] employs
a trained LLM to refine the original question for a more
accurate augmentation of the chunk. GraphRAG [10] extracts
triples from documents to build graphs, using this kind of
structured information to aid the LLM in answering questions.
Evaluation Metrics. We adopt Accuracy (Acc) as the pri-
mary metric to assess the performance of LLMs on MEBench
O u r s
Fig. 3. Experimental results for eight tasks of each method.
tasks. For the subcategories of Variance Analysis, Correlation
Analysis, and Distribution Compliance within the Statistics
tasks shown in Table I, we focus solely on prompting LLMs to
identify relevant columns and applicable methods, evaluating
the accuracy of their selections instead of the computational
results, as LLMs’ abilities in precise calculations are not the
central focus of this study.
C. Results and Analysis
Different methods display significant performance differ-
ences on MEBench. Table III presents experimental results
256

TABLE III
EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS FOR MEB ENCH .
Models Accuracy
Comparison Statistics Relationship Overall
GPT-4o 0.199 0.289 0.507 0.316
GPT-4o + RAG 0.763 0.410 0.687 0.593
RQ-RAG 0.733 0.566 0.807 0.685
GraphRAG 0.77 0.624 0.829 0.773
Structured RAG 0.934 0.908 0.803 0.889
alongside the overall accuracy in MEBench, and Figure 3
shows the accuracy in eight types further divided.
Performance of Structured RAG and SOTAs. Compared
to other existing methods, our Structured RAG approach
significantly enhances overall accuracy, reaching 88.9% and
raising the SOTA(GraphRAG) by 11.6%. Our approach out-
performs accuracy in the comparison query types by 20.1%,
respectively, while achieving a remarkable improvement of
28.4% for statistical query types. In relationship queries, our
model performs at a level comparable to the state-of-the-art
(SOTA). The slightly lower performance can be attributed to
the fact that text and graph representations are often more
effective for addressing these types of questions than tabular
data. We plan to explore the incorporation of additional data
structures during the inference phase of the system.
Errors Analysis for Structured RAG We sample and
analyze the output of the Structured RAG system. It faces
two challenges which are listed below.
• Relation semantic parsing. The Structured RAG Multi-
entity Semantic Retrieval module can effectively rec-
ognizes entities but struggles with relationship identi-
fication, leading to challenges in graph retrieval and
negatively affecting the performance of RAG-based ap-
proaches, including Structured RAG. For example, in
the query “How many US presidents have served more
than one term in office?” The model incorrectly identifies
the relationship as “instance of” rather than the correct
“position held”, leading to erroneous results.
• Insufficient information extraction. We also identified
errors in Structured RAG’s information extraction during
the table-filling phase. An analysis of more than 2,000
table filling instances reveals that these errors occur
primarily as omissions (albeit with a low probability of
approximately 0.1%). A new challenge is the appearance
of multi-word synonyms within the same column, such
as “US” and “America”, which negatively affects the
accuracy of SQL execution such as “SELECT”.
VI. C ONCLUSION
The study addresses the limitations of conventional RAG
systems in handling complex, multi-entity questions by intro-
ducing a novel structured framework. By integrating semantic
knowledge graph retrieval guided by large language models
and a table generation module, the proposed approach trans-
forms unstructured data into relational tables, enabling precise
querying through advanced table-based methods. This struc-
tured framework significantly enhances accuracy for entity-
dense queries. Experimental results validate the framework’s
superiority, achieving an 11.6% improvement in accuracy
over state-of-the-art baselines on a purpose-built benchmark
designed to rigorously evaluate Multi-Entity QA performance.
The success underscores the critical role of structured database
principles—such as relational modeling and efficient index-
ing—in augmenting generative AI systems for knowledge-
intensive tasks. By bridging database technologies with gen-
erative AI, this research advances solutions for applications
demanding multifaceted reasoning over heterogeneous sources
while setting a foundation for future innovations at the in-
tersection of structured data management and LLM-driven
intelligence.
