## [21] The Evolution of AI: From Classical Machine Learning to Modern Large Language Models
- **Authors:** Rahul Mundlamuri, Ganesh Reddy Gunnam, Nikhil Kumar Mysari, Jayakanth Pujuri
- **Year:** 2025
- **Filename:** `The_Evolution_of_AI_From_Classical_Machine_Learning_to_Modern_Large_Language_Models.pdf`

Received 14 September 2025, accepted 8 October 2025, date of publication 14 October 2025, date of current version 20 October 2025.
Digital Object Identifier 10.1 109/ACCESS.2025.3621344
The Evolution of AI: From Classical Machine
Learning to Modern Large Language Models
RAHUL MUNDLAMURI
 1,2, (Senior Member, IEEE),
GANESH REDDY GUNNAM
 2, (Senior Member, IEEE),
NIKHIL KUMAR MYSARI
 1, (Member, IEEE),
AND JAYAKANTH PUJURI
 1, (Member, IEEE)
1Sira Consulting Inc., Houston, TX 77057, USA
2The University of Texas at San Antonio, San Antonio, TX 78249, USA
Corresponding author: Rahul Mundlamuri (rahul.mundlamuri473@gmail.com)
ABSTRACT This paper provides a comprehensive review of the evolution of artificial intelligence from
early symbolic, rule-based systems to modern large language models (LLMs) and retrieval-augmented
generation (RAG) architectures. Early AI was dominated by symbolic reasoning and expert systems using
hand-crafted rules, before a paradigm shift toward data-driven learning occurred with the advent of machine
learning, notably the backpropagation algorithm that enabled training of multi-layer neural networks to learn
complex tasks. This breakthrough ushered in the deep learning era: convolutional neural networks (CNNs)
achieved landmark results in computer vision, recurrent neural networks (RNNs such as LSTM) excelled at
sequential data processing, and deep reinforcement learning (RL) systems mastered challenging decision-
making tasks. In natural language processing (NLP), distributed word embedding models like Word2Vec
and GloVe replaced sparse representations, capturing semantic relationships in vector space. To handle
rare and out-of-vocabulary words, subword tokenization techniques such as Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) and
WordPiece were introduced. These advances culminated in the Transformer architecture, based on self-
attention mechanisms, which now underpins most state-of-the-art LLMs. Transformer-based LLMs, pre-
trained on large text corpora, demonstrate advanced capabilities in language generation and understanding.
Beyond model architectures, the review highlights how LLMs are augmented with external knowledge
via RAG. Retrieval-augmented generation combines an LLM with a vector database of text embeddings,
allowing the model to retrieve relevant external information to ground its outputs. This approach significantly
improves factual accuracy and relevance, mitigating issues like hallucination, though ensuring complete
truthfulness remains challenging. Additional obstacles include the limited context windows of LLMs (on
the order of a few thousand tokens) and the computational complexity and latency of inference. The review
also examines societal implications of advanced AI, such as bias amplification from training data and
other ethical considerations in deployment. Finally, it outlines future directions, including extended context
lengths, improved interpretability and alignment, greater efficiency, and neuro-symbolic hybrid approaches,
aiming to develop more robust and trustworthy AI systems.
INDEX TERMS Artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, reinforcement learning, large
language models, neural networks, transformers.
The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and
approving it for publication was Larbi Boubchir
 .
I. INTRODUCTION
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has undergone profound transfor-
mations over the past several decades, progressing through
distinct paradigms. Early AI research in the mid-20th
century was dominated by symbolic AI and rule-based
178302

 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License.
For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
expert systems, which attempted to encode human knowledge
and reasoning as explicit logic rules [1]. In subsequent
decades, limitations of these hand-crafted approaches became
apparent, leading to a shift toward data-driven methods.
The field embraced statistical learning and classical machine
learning (ML) algorithms that could automatically infer
patterns from data as a major departure from manually
encoding knowledge [2]. This transition set the stage for
the deep learning revolution in the 2000s-2010s, where
multilayer neural network models trained on large datasets
dramatically advanced the state of the art. Most recently, the
advent of transformer-based large language models (LLMs)
has pushed AI capabilities even further, enabling machines to
generate human-like text and engage in complex reasoning
tasks [3].
This Introduction situates these developments in historical
and technical context, tracing the evolution from symbolic
AI to modern LLMs. We highlight key transitions and
foundational algorithms from early expert systems and
decision tree learners to statistical learning techniques, neural
network breakthroughs, and the transformer architectures
underpinning today’s generative models. In doing so, we also
discuss the technical innovations (e.g. backpropagation, con-
volutional networks, attention mechanisms) and engineering
advances (e.g. tokenization schemes, word embeddings,
vector databases) that have enabled each era. Finally,
we provide a high-level overview of the remainder of this
review, including the challenges and societal considerations
accompanying these advances, and outlooks for future
research.
From Symbolic AI to Machine Learning: Early Paradigms
and Shifting Approaches The birth of AI as an academic field
in the 1950s-1960s was rooted in the belief that intelligent
behavior could be achieved by explicitly encoding knowledge
and logical rules [4]. This approach, later termed Good
Old-Fashioned AI (GOFAI), treated intelligence as a problem
of symbol manipulation [5]. Symbolic AI systems represent
knowledge in formal languages (such as logical proposi-
tions, production rules, or semantic networks) and derive
conclusions via logical inference [6]. Early examples include
Newell and Simon’s General Problem Solver and McCarthy’s
development of the Lisp programming language for symbolic
computation [7]. A notable success of the symbolic paradigm
was the rise of expert systems in the 1970s and 1980s.
Expert systems, such as MYCIN for medical diagnosis and
DENDRAL for chemical analysis, were rule-based programs
designed to mimic the decision-making of human specialists
[8]. They consisted of a knowledge base of if-then rules and
an inference engine that applied these rules to facts provided
in a query. These systems demonstrated that, in narrow
domains, computers could emulate expert-level reasoning
by following handcrafted rules. For example, the MYCIN
system contained hundreds of rules elicited from physicians
and could perform antibiotic recommendations for blood
infections with accuracy comparable to medical residents.
FIGURE 1. Timeline of major milestones in AI.
The emergence of such knowledge-based systems validated
the power of symbolic representations and led to widespread
deployment in industries like finance and engineering in the
1980s [9]. However, the limitations of symbolic AI also
became increasingly evident. Encoding expert knowledge
by hand was labor-intensive (the ‘‘knowledge acquisition
bottleneck’’), and the resulting systems were brittle they
struggled to handle situations that fell outside their encoded
rules or expertise [10].
Expert systems had no capacity to learn or generalize
beyond their initial rule base. As the complexity of tar-
get problems grew, purely rule-based approaches became
unwieldy. Maintenance was difficult and subtle bugs or
exceptions in rule sets could lead to erroneous conclusions.
Moreover, many tasks (like vision or natural language
understanding) proved hard to define with exact rules. By the
late 1980s, these difficulties, coupled with unmet high
expectations, led to reduced funding and interest a period
known as the ‘‘AI winter.’’ Indeed, a second AI winter in the
late 1980s was precipitated in part by the failure of expert
systems to deliver a general breakthrough in intelligence
[11]. Initially celebrated, these systems ultimately proved
costly to maintain and unable to generalize knowledge
beyond narrow domains. The disappointment with rule-based
AI prompted researchers to seek more robust, automated
learning techniques. The transition to machine learning began
as researchers explored algorithms that allow computers to
learn from data rather than rely on manually coded rules.
Pioneering work in the late 1970s and 1980s on decision tree
learning, Bayesian inference, and neural networks laid the
groundwork for data-driven AI [12].
An early milestone was Quinlan’s development of the ID3
algorithm for inducing decision trees from examples [13].
ID3 demonstrated that a system could automatically generate
a logical rule structure (in the form of a decision tree) by
training on labeled data rather than having rules explicitly
programmed. This approach inverted the knowledge acqui-
sition problem-instead of experts writing down rules, they
would provide training examples and let the algorithm infer
the rules. Quinlan’s ID3 and its successors (C4.5, CART)
became core tools of symbolic machine learning, extracting
human-readable if-then rules or decision trees from data.
In parallel, other forms of statistical learning gained
traction. Researchers developed powerful classification algo-
rithms like the support vector machine (SVM), introduced
by Cortes and Vapnik [14]. These algorithms emerged from
statistical theory and optimization and often outperformed
expert systems on pattern recognition tasks. For example,
VOLUME 13, 2025 178303

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 1. The evolution of AI: from classical machine learning to modern large language models.
by the 1990s, SVMs were achieving high accuracy in
domains like handwriting recognition, medical diagnosis,
and financial prediction areas where manual rule-writing
had stalled. As data availability grew and computing power
increased, data-driven models showed increasing returns:
more data resulted in better performance, a trend that would
only amplify in the coming decades.
While symbolic AI and classical machine learning were
developing, another lineage of research-connectionist AI-was
exploring computation via networks of neuron-like units.
Inspired by neuroscience, artificial neural networks (ANNs)
attempt to model the brain’s distributed information process-
ing. Early forms of neural networks (e.g., Rosenblatt’s per-
ceptron, 1958) could learn simple decision boundaries, but for
decades, training multi-layer networks to solve complex tasks
was an unsolved problem. A critical breakthrough came in
1986 with the re-discovery of the backpropagation algorithm
for training multi-layer neural networks [15]. Rumelhart,
Hinton, and Williams demonstrated that back-propagating
error gradients through the network could effectively adjust
the weights in each layer, enabling networks with one
or more hidden layers to learn internal representations.
This development meant that neural networks were no
longer limited to a single layer; for the first time, deep
networks could be trained to hierarchically learn features
from data. Backpropagation unlocked much richer learning
behaviors-the hidden units of a neural network trained via
backpropagation could automatically discover useful features
and encodings of the input domain, rather than relying on
human-designed features.
Throughout the late 1980s, researchers applied backprop-
agation to problems like character recognition and sensor
fusion, demonstrating that neural nets could achieve good
performance given enough training data [16]. For instance,
multi-layer perceptrons trained with backpropagation sur-
passed earlier rule-based systems on tasks such as zip
code digit recognition [16]. Despite these successes, neural
networks of that era often remained limited by computational
power and the size of available datasets. Training was
slow, and overfitting was a concern for networks with
tens of thousands of parameters trained on relatively small
datasets of the time. Nevertheless, the connectionist approach
introduced key advantages. Unlike symbolic systems, neural
networks could generalize from examples and were robust to
noise. They offered a path to handle perceptual tasks such as
vision and speech that eluded neat symbolic rules [16].
Research in the 1990s led to improved architectures
specialized for certain data types. A prime example is
the convolutional neural network (CNN), pioneered by
LeCun and colleagues for image recognition [16]. CNNs
introduced structured arrangements of neurons inspired
by the visual cortex, using local receptive fields, weight
sharing, and spatial subsampling to exploit the structure
of images. LeCun’s LeNet-5 CNN, developed in the mid-
1990s, achieved excellent accuracy in classifying handwritten
digits (such as postal codes) by automatically learning visual
178304 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
FIGURE 2. Conceptual evolution of AI: From symbolic systems to deep
learning and large language models.
features like strokes and loops [16]. By constraining the
network architecture to be partially translation-invariant (via
convolution and pooling layers), LeNet-5 could efficiently
learn from relatively small image datasets. In a 1998 paper,
LeCun et al. showed that a CNN trained with backpropa-
gation significantly outperformed other techniques on digit
recognition, with minimal preprocessing of images [16].
This was a compelling demonstration that learned features
could surpass hand-crafted ones; the network’s convolutional
layers effectively learned to extract edges, curves, and shapes
necessary for recognition. CNNs thus became the state-of-
the-art method for vision tasks by the late 1990s, finding
commercial use in recognizing handwritten checks and
documents.
During the 1990s, neural networks and other ML
approaches coexisted, each dominating different applica-
tions. Many practitioners remained skeptical of neural
nets due to their ‘‘black box’’ nature and heavy compute
requirements. It wasn’t until the 2000s that several factors
converged to spark the deep learning revolution. First, the
amount of available training data grew exponentially with the
internet, digital sensors, and cheap data storage [17]. Datasets
with millions of examples (orders of magnitude larger than
in the 1980s) became available in areas like images, speech,
and text. Second, hardware advancements, especially the
repurposing of graphics processing units (GPUs) for gen-
eral computation, dramatically accelerated neural network
training in the late 2000s [18]. Researchers discovered that
GPUs originally designed for fast image rendering were
well-suited to the linear algebra operations in neural network
training, often delivering tenfold or more speedups over
CPUs. This made it feasible to train much larger networks
on much more data. Third, various algorithmic improvements
emerged (such as better weight initializations, activation
functions like ReLUs, and regularization techniques like
dropout) that helped train deeper networks without diverging
or overfitting [19], [20].
The watershed moment came in 2012 with the success of
a deep CNN in the high-profile ImageNet competition [21].
ImageNet was a massive visual database of over a million
real-world images across 1000 categories, far bigger and
more complex than prior image datasets. In 2012, Krizhevsky,
Sutskever, and Hinton introduced a deep convolutional
network (later known as AlexNet) and achieved a stunning
win in the ImageNet Large-Scale Visual Recognition Chal-
lenge. Their CNN, with 8 learned layers and 60 million
weights, far outperformed all traditional computer vision
methods, halving the top-5 error rate relative to the next
best entry. AlexNet’s success was due to both its depth and
the effective use of GPU acceleration and techniques like
ReLU activations and dropout regularization. On ImageNet
test data, the deep CNN achieved a top-5 error of 15.3%
vs. 26.2% for the best non-neural approach, an astonishing
improvement. This result convincingly demonstrated that
scaling up neural networks (in depth, width, and data) could
yield breakthroughs in performance.
In the aftermath, the research community rapidly embraced
deep learning not only for vision, but in speech recognition,
where deep neural networks (DNNs) introduced by Hinton
et al. in 2012 achieved major accuracy gains in transcribing
speech [22]. By the mid-2010s, deep learning became the
dominant approach in AI research. Deep neural networks
trained on large datasets were setting new records in
diverse tasks: image classification, object detection, machine
translation, language modeling, and more [23]. This era saw
the development of many specialized architectures (e.g., deep
recurrent networks for sequence data, autoencoders for
unsupervised learning, generative adversarial networks for
image generation [24]), but all shared the core paradigm of
multi-layer representation learning via end-to-end training.
As an authoritative 2016 review by LeCun, Bengio, and
Hinton proclaimed, deep learning had become the ‘‘fifth
generation’’ of computing, enabling computers to discover
intricate patterns in data with minimal human feature
engineering.
One pivotal family of neural networks in this period was
the recurrent neural network (RNN), designed for sequence
and time-series data. RNNs ‘‘recycle’’ or feedback their
outputs from one time step as inputs to the next, giving
them a form of memory. This makes RNNs natural for
modeling sequences like text, speech, or time series, where
the context from previous inputs is relevant to future ones.
However, a known challenge with standard RNNs is the
vanishing gradient problem: as gradients are backpropagated
through many time steps, they can shrink exponentially,
preventing the network from learning long-range depen-
dencies. A major advance came with the Long Short-Term
Memory (LSTM) architecture proposed by Hochreiter and
Schmidhuber (1997). LSTM introduced gating mechanisms
that allow gradients and information to flow more effectively
over long sequence intervals. In effect, LSTM networks
VOLUME 13, 2025 178305

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 2. Mapping AI paradigms to key application domains.
mitigate vanishing gradients and can preserve information for
hundreds of time-steps, whereas traditional RNNs struggled
with even tens of steps. The LSTM’s design featuring an
explicit memory cell and gating units (input, output, forget
gates) enabled it to learn long-term sequences of information
by deciding which information to keep or forget at each
step [25].
One especially impactful trend enabled by Transformers
has been the rise of large pre-trained language models.
Instead of training a model from scratch for each NLP
task, researchers discovered that training a very large
neural network on a generic language modeling objective
(predicting the next word or a masked word in text) using a
massive text corpus can yield a versatile model that contains
extensive linguistic and factual knowledge [26], [27]. Early
successes of pre-training included ELMo (Peters et al., 2018)
with LSTMs and the use of unsupervised pre-training in the
GPT model by OpenAI (Radford et al., 2018). However, the
approach reached new heights with BERT and subsequent
Transformer-based large language models (LLMs). BERT
(Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers),
introduced by Devlin et al. in 2018, exemplified the power of
this paradigm. BERT is a Transformer encoder trained on a
massive corpus (Wikipedia + BooksCorpus) with a masked
language modeling objective and a next-sentence prediction
objective. By jointly conditioning on left and right context,
BERT learns deep bidirectional representations of language.
The pre-trained BERT model can then be fine-tuned on
various NLP tasks by adding a small output layer, instead
of training a new model from scratch. BERT achieved
state-of-the-art results on numerous NLP tasks including
question answering and natural language inference, often
with significant improvements. For instance, BERT notably
improved the GLUE benchmark and set new records on
SQuAD and MultiNLI.
The GPT series from OpenAI further scaled this paradigm
with a focus on generative capabilities using Transformer
decoders. GPT-2 (2019) demonstrated that a 1.5 billion-
parameter Transformer could generate coherent and diverse
text. The culmination came with GPT-3 [26], featuring
175 billion parameters and trained on a vast and diverse
corpus. GPT-3 showed exceptional few-shot learning capa-
bilities, performing impressively on tasks such as trans-
lation, arithmetic, and commonsense reasoning without
task-specific fine-tuning. The model’s scale allowed it to
internalize broad knowledge and reasoning abilities. The
scaling trend continued with even larger models such as
Google’s PaLM with 540 billion parameters [28], Deep-
Mind’s Gopher [29], and OpenAI’s GPT-4 [30], reinforcing
the scaling laws of neural language models [31]. These
LLMs now possess capabilities that extend beyond basic
NLP tasks, enabling activities such as code generation,
article composition, student tutoring, and fluent conversation,
significantly advancing towards the goal of general-purpose
AI systems.
Advances Enabling Modern NLP: Tokenization and Vector
Representations
Alongside the evolution of architectures, specific tech-
niques in NLP preprocessing and representation have been
crucial for the success of modern language models. Two such
innovations are subword tokenization and distributed vector
representations (embeddings).
Tokenization: In earlier NLP systems, words were typically
treated as atomic units (tokens), which led to the problem of
out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words-any word not seen during
training could not be handled. Moreover, languages with
complex morphology or tasks involving creative language
would require extremely large vocabularies. To address this,
AI researchers developed subword segmentation algorithms,
breaking text into smaller units so that any word can be
composed from these units. Byte Pair Encoding (BPE),
adapted by Sennrich et al. for machine translation, is a promi-
nent method [32]. BPE starts with characters as tokens and
merges the most frequent adjacent pairs iteratively. Variants
of BPE, such as WordPiece introduced by Schuster and
Nakajima for voice search, were adopted widely, notably by
BERT and GPT models [33]. More recently, SentencePiece
provided a unified, language-independent framework that
trains subword models directly from raw text, accommodat-
ing languages without explicit spacing [34]. Tokenizers like
T5’s span-masking [35], BART’s byte-level BPE [36], and
multilingual SentencePiece in mT5 [37] illustrate continued
advances. These tokenization techniques significantly reduce
vocabulary size and implicitly incorporate morphological
awareness, essential for efficient training and generalization
in large language models.
Distributed Representations (Embeddings): Another fun-
damental concept in AI’s evolution is vector embeddings
for discrete entities. Traditional one-hot representations fail
to capture semantic similarity. Distributed representations
encode entities in continuous vector spaces, enabling seman-
tic similarity by proximity. The Word2Vec toolkit, created
by Mikolov et al., popularized efficient learning of word
embeddings [38]. Subsequently, Pennington et al. introduced
178306 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
GloVe, combining global corpus statistics with local contexts,
achieving similar results and widespread adoption [39].
These embeddings became foundational in NLP systems,
enhancing performance and generalization across numerous
tasks.
The trend shifted to contextual embeddings with models
like ELMo, BERT, and their successors. Transformers trained
with masked or autoregressive objectives generate contex-
tualized token vectors, allowing richer semantics. Unified
representation learners like SimCSE [40], CoSENT [41], and
GTR [42] improved semantic retrieval and sentence embed-
dings, outperforming static embeddings in downstream tasks.
As models increased in complexity and scale, the need for
efficient knowledge retrieval grew, leading to the develop-
ment of vector databases and retrieval-augmented generation
(RAG) techniques. FAISS, introduced by Facebook AI,
enabled fast similarity search over millions of vectors [43].
Dense retrieval with models like DPR [44] and ColBERT [45]
brought significant gains in open-domain question answer-
ing. Lewis et al. demonstrated RAG, combining pre-trained
models with external knowledge retrieval from dense vector
indexes, significantly improving accuracy [46]. Hybrid
systems, integrating parametric models with explicit memory
or retrieval tools, have increasingly become standard practice,
exemplifying the maturation and effectiveness of modern
NLP methods.
The dramatic progress in AI capability has been accom-
panied by new challenges and concerns. Contemporary large
models are not without limitations. One major issue is the
tendency of generative models to produce hallucinations-
outputs that sound plausible but are factually incorrect or
nonsensical. An LLM like GPT-3 or GPT-4 might confidently
state a wrong date for a historical event or invent a reference
that doesn’t exist, due to the way they probabilistically
sample text without a guarantee of truthfulness. These
hallucinations occur when the model’s learned distribution
fills in information that wasn’t explicitly provided or
learned incorrectly. Mitigating hallucination remains an open
research problem; techniques like grounding the model
with retrieved documents (as in RAG) or using confidence
estimators and reinforcement learning from human feedback
(RLHF) are being investigated to reduce this issue [47].
Another challenge is scalability and latency. The largest
models today consist of hundreds of billions of parameters,
which require tremendous computational resources to train
and even to deploy. Serving a model like GPT-3 in real-time
applications can be difficult-inference may require multiple
GPUs and has non-trivial latency, which is problematic
for interactive systems. Researchers are exploring model
compression (distillation, pruning) and efficient architectures
to make large models more tractable for deployment.
Additionally, the cost of training and running these models
is extremely high not just financially but also in terms
of energy consumption, leading to concerns about the
environmental footprint of AI. Efforts in green AI are
pushing for more compute-efficient training techniques and
reuse of pre-trained models to avoid repeatedly expending
energy [48].
From a societal perspective, as AI systems permeate
various aspects of life, ethical and fairness concerns have
come to the forefront. One issue is bias in AI models.
Since modern models learn from vast amounts of human-
generated data, they inevitably absorb the biases present
in society and in those data. Researchers have found,
for example, that word embeddings captured gender and
ethnic stereotypes. Bias in LLMs remains persistent even
in newer models [49]. Mitigating these biases is a major
concern: techniques include dataset curation, bias detection
metrics, and model debiasing algorithms (such as removing
gender directions from embeddings or fine-tuning LLMs on
balanced data) [50].
Another concern is privacy. LLMs trained on scraped inter-
net data or user submissions could inadvertently memorize
sensitive information (like phone numbers, private messages,
or proprietary code) and potentially regurgitate it. There have
been demonstrations of training data extraction attacks on
foundation models [51]. This raises legal and ethical issues,
especially when models are trained on copyrighted material
or personal data. Techniques like differential privacy and
secure training pipelines are under development to address
this [52].
As AI systems become more powerful and widely
deployed, ensuring they behave in alignment with human
values becomes critical. Misuse risks (e.g., disinformation,
cyberattacks), economic impacts (e.g., job automation), and
concerns over governance and transparency continue to
grow [53], [54]. Despite the challenges, the future of AI
holds exciting possibilities. Ongoing research is tackling
fundamental limitations like explainability [55], modular AI,
neurosymbolic hybrids, and multimodal reasoning, pushing
us closer to truly general-purpose intelligent systems.
Section III Statistical Learning and Classical Machine
Learning: Describes the shift in the 1980s - 1990s toward
data-driven approaches. This section highlights algorithms
like decision tree learning (ID3/C4.5), Naive Bayes clas-
sifiers, support vector machines, and ensemble methods.
We examine how these techniques enabled learning from
data, outperforming manual rules in many domains, and we
illustrate the theoretical foundations they introduced (from
information gain in trees to margin maximization in SVMs).
Section IV The Deep Learning Revolution: Discusses
the re-emergence of neural networks and the breakthroughs
in representation learning. We detail the development of
multi-layer perceptrons trained by backpropagation, the
success of convolutional neural networks (LeNet, AlexNet)
in vision, and recurrent networks (LSTM) in sequence
modeling. We also cover the expansion of deep learn-
ing into diverse areas and the factors (big data, GPUs,
algorithms) that enabled networks to go deeper, achieving
superhuman performance in vision, speech, and games
VOLUME 13, 2025 178307

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
(notably highlighting DQN and AlphaGo in reinforcement
learning).
Section V Transformers and Large Language Models:
Explores the recent transformation in NLP driven by
the Transformer architecture and huge pre-trained models.
We explain the self-attention mechanism and why Trans-
formers replaced RNNs in many tasks. The section then
focuses on large language models (BERT, GPT-3, etc.),
including their training objectives, scale, capabilities (such
as few-shot learning), and architectural variants. We also
include technical discussions of tokenization methods (BPE,
WordPiece, SentencePiece) that enable LLMs to handle open
vocabularies, and distributed representations (Word2Vec,
GloVe) that underlie how language and knowledge are
encoded in vectors.
Section VI Challenges and Limitations of Modern AI:
Addresses the key technical challenges that arise with
advanced AI systems. We examine issues of hallucina-
tions and factuality in generative models, the difficulties
in interpreting and explaining deep model decisions, the
computational and energy demands of scaling models, and
engineering challenges like latency in real-time inference.
This section also surveys research efforts aimed at mitigating
these limitations (such as model compression, retrieval
augmentation, and better training protocols). Section VII
Societal Impacts and Ethical Considerations: Examines the
broader implications of AI’s evolution on society. We discuss
biases learned by AI systems and their potential harmful
effects, concerns about privacy and data misuse, and security
issues (e.g. adversarial examples). We also touch on AI gov-
ernance, including fairness, accountability, and transparency
principles, and the need for regulations or standards as AI is
deployed in sensitive domains.
Section VIII Future Directions: Outlines promising
avenues for future research and development in AI.
We highlight directions such as developing more general AI
systems that can learn and reason in more human-like ways,
efforts to combine symbolic reasoning with neural networks,
advancements in unsupervised and semi-supervised learning
to reduce reliance on large labeled datasets, and the push
towards more efficient and sustainable AI. We also consider
the potential of AI in new fields and the importance of
interdisciplinary approaches moving forward. Section IX
Conclusion: Summarizes the insights from this evolution,
reflecting on how far AI has come and the road ahead.
We conclude with final thoughts on the interplay between the
different paradigms of AI and how they might converge into
creating truly intelligent systems. Through this comprehen-
sive review, the Introduction has set the stage by charting the
historical and technical path from the rule-based origins of AI
to the era of large language models. In the sections that follow,
each phase of this evolution is examined in depth, drawing
on seminal works and breakthroughs. By understanding
this journey including the successes achieved and lessons
learned we gain perspective on current state-of-the-art AI
and how future innovations can be guided to address current
challenges, leverage the strengths of each paradigm, and
ultimately advance the field toward more robust, intelligent,
and human-aligned systems.
II. EARLY FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
The origins of Artificial Intelligence (AI) can be traced
back to foundational concepts that emerged long before
the advent of modern computing [56]. Early AI research
was heavily influenced by rule-based systems, symbolic
reasoning, and statistical methods [57], [58], [59], which
laid the groundwork for more sophisticated AI models that
followed. This section explores the historical evolution of AI
through three key aspects: rule-based systems and symbolic
AI, early statistical methods in AI, and the limitations of these
early approaches [60], [61].
A. RULE-BASED SYSTEMS AND SYMBOLIC AI
Rule-based systems, also known as expert systems, were
among the earliest attempts at creating artificial intelli-
gence [56], [58]. These systems were designed to emulate
human decision-making processes using a structured set of
predefined rules. A rule-based system operates on an if-
then principle, where predefined rules determine the system’s
response to various inputs [57], [62].
The first notable rule-based systems emerged in the 1950s
and 1960s, influenced by mathematical logic and symbolic
representation [57], [63]. The logic-based approach allowed
AI researchers to model reasoning and decision-making using
formal rules and structured knowledge representations [61].
Symbolic AI, also known as GOFAI (Good Old-Fashioned
AI), focused on representing knowledge using symbols and
manipulating them through logical inference [56], [61].
Researchers in the field of symbolic AI sought to develop
algorithms that could process human knowledge using
structured data representations, such as production rules (IF
condition THEN action), semantic networks, frames, and
logic-based approaches [63], [64].
One of the most significant achievements in symbolic
AI was the development of SHRDLU, a natural language
processing system created by Terry Winograd in the early
1970s [65]. SHRDLU was capable of understanding and
responding to human instructions in a constrained envi-
ronment called the ‘‘blocks world.’’ This demonstrated
the potential of symbolic AI for reasoning and language
understanding.
During the 1970s and 1980s, expert systems became
the dominant AI paradigm [58], [66]. These systems were
designed to mimic human experts in specialized domains
by using extensive knowledge bases and inference engines.
Some of the most well-known expert systems included:
• MYCIN (1970s): A medical diagnosis system that
assisted doctors in identifying bacterial infections and
recommending treatments [62].
• DENDRAL (1960s-1970s): A chemistry expert system
for analyzing molecular structures [67].
178308 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 3. Rule-based AI systems: key examples and core components.
• XCON (1980s): A system developed by Digital Equip-
ment Corporation (DEC) for configuring computer
hardware [68].
Expert systems were widely adopted in fields such
as medicine, engineering, and business decision-making.
However, they were limited by their reliance on manually
curated knowledge bases, which made them difficult to scale
and adapt to dynamic environments [56], [61].
B. EARLY STATISTICAL METHODS IN AI
While rule-based AI relied on logical rules, statistical
approaches emerged as an alternative to handle uncertainty
and probabilistic reasoning. Bayesian networks, introduced
by Judea Pearl in the 1980s, became a cornerstone of
probabilistic AI.
Bayesian networks use directed acyclic graphs to model
relationships between variables, allowing AI systems
to update probabilities dynamically as new information
becomes available. These networks were applied in various
fields, including:
1. Medical diagnosis (e.g., predicting the likelihood of
diseases based on symptoms)
2. Speech recognition (e.g., probabilistic modeling of
phonemes and words)
3. Robotics While rule-based AI relied on logical rules,
statistical approaches emerged as an alternative to handle
uncertainty and probabilistic reasoning [56]. Bayesian net-
works, introduced by Judea Pearl in the 1980s, became a
cornerstone of probabilistic AI [59].
Bayesian networks use directed acyclic graphs to model
relationships between variables, allowing AI systems
to update probabilities dynamically as new information
becomes available. These networks were applied in various
fields, including medical diagnosis (e.g., predicting the
likelihood of diseases based on symptoms) [69], speech
recognition (e.g., probabilistic modeling of phonemes and
words) [70], and robotics (e.g., probabilistic localization and
navigation) [71].
Markov models and Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)
played a crucial role in AI, particularly in sequential data
processing. HMMs, introduced in the 1960s and widely
adopted in the 1980s, became essential for applications such
as speech recognition (e.g., modeling phoneme sequences
in natural language) [70], optical character recognition
(OCR) (e.g., recognizing handwritten text) [72], and natural
language processing (NLP) (e.g., part-of-speech tagging,
machine translation) [73]. HMMs provided a statistical
approach to AI, contrasting with the deterministic nature of
rule-based systems. They allowed AI to handle ambiguous
and noisy data, making them invaluable for real-world
applications [70].
While modern deep learning dominates AI research today,
the origins of neural networks date back to the 1940s and
1950s. The Perceptron, developed by Frank Rosenblatt in
1958, was the first artificial neural network capable of
learning weights from input data [74]. Despite its limitations,
the perceptron laid the foundation for future advancements in
neural computation [75].
The limitations of early neural networks, particularly
their inability to solve non-linearly separable prob-
lems, led to the first ‘‘AI Winter’’ in the 1970s [75],
[76]. However, research in statistical learning contin-
ued, influencing later developments in deep learning
[23], [56].
Despite their promising potential, early AI methodologies
faced several challenges that hindered their widespread
adoption. Rule-based AI required extensive manual knowl-
edge engineering, making it difficult to scale; updating and
maintaining knowledge bases was labor-intensive and prone
to inconsistencies [56], [58]. Expert systems struggled with
generalization beyond predefined rules. Symbolic AI and
expert systems lacked the ability to learn from new data,
limiting their flexibility. Unlike modern machine learning
models, early AI relied on static rule sets rather than
adaptive learning [61]. Early AI systems required significant
computational resources, which were scarce in the 20th
century. Complex rule-based reasoning was computationally
expensive and inefficient compared to modern deep learn-
ing [77]. Early AI struggled with ambiguity, uncertainty,
and noisy real-world data. Statistical approaches provided
VOLUME 13, 2025 178309

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 4. Statistical methods in AI vs. rule-based approaches.
FIGURE 3. Concept map of major statistical and probabilistic methods
in AI.
some improvements, but robust AI required advancements in
machine learning and deep learning [23], [56].
The early foundations of AI, including rule-based sys-
tems, symbolic reasoning, and statistical methods, laid
the groundwork for the machine learning revolution that
followed [56], [61]. While these early approaches had
significant limitations, they provided critical insights into
knowledge representation, decision-making, and probabilis-
tic reasoning [58], [59]. The shift from manually curated rules
to data-driven learning marked a turning point in AI history,
leading to the development of deep learning, reinforcement
learning, and the emergence of large-scale AI models
[23], [78].
The next stage in AI’s evolution would involve leveraging
neural networks, deep learning, and scalable algorithms
that could learn from vast amounts of data. This transition
set the stage for modern artificial intelligence, shaping the
advancements that continue to drive AI innovation today
[23], [77].
III. CLASSICAL MACHINE LEARNING (ML)
The evolution of Artificial Intelligence (AI) from rule-based
systems to data-driven learning models marked a significant
paradigm shift in the field. Classical machine learning (ML)
emerged as a critical phase in this evolution, introducing
a structured methodology for learning patterns from data
and making data-driven decisions [56], [79]. In contrast to
the knowledge-engineered rules of symbolic AI, classical
ML models could learn from examples and generalize to
unseen scenarios [80], [81]. This section explores the major
categories of classical ML, the foundational algorithms and
models, key use cases, and the limitations that eventually led
to the rise of deep learning [23], [77].
Classical ML refers to a group of algorithms that learn
patterns and rules from data to perform tasks such as
classification, regression, clustering, and dimensionality
reduction. The primary objective is to build models that can
generalize well on new, unseen data [79]. Unlike rule-based
systems, these models do not rely on explicit programming
of rules but on identifying patterns from data.
Machine learning can broadly be categorized into three
types: (1) Supervised Learning: learning from labeled
datasets; (2) Unsupervised Learning: identifying hidden
patterns in unlabeled data; and (3) Semi-supervised and
178310 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
FIGURE 4. Comprehensive pipeline of a classical machine learning workflow for prediction or classification.
Reinforcement Learning: hybrid models and decision-making
through feedback, respectively [56].
In this section, we focus on classical supervised and
unsupervised learning, which form the backbone of early ML
development.
A. SUPERVISED LEARNING
Supervised learning involves training a model on a labeled
dataset, where each input is paired with an output label.
The model learns a function that maps inputs to outputs,
aiming to minimize prediction error on unseen data [56], [79].
It is foundational to classical machine learning and underpins
numerous practical applications in science, industry, and
healthcare [80].
1) LINEAR REGRESSION
Linear regression is one of the earliest and simplest super-
vised learning techniques, used for predicting a continuous
output [82]. The model assumes a linear relationship
between input variables (features) and the output (target).
Mathematically, the model is defined as:
y = β0 + β1x1 + β2x2 + . . .+ βnxn + ϵ
where y is the predicted value, xi are input features, βi are
model coefficients, and ϵ is the error term.
Linear regression became foundational in areas like
economics, forecasting, and healthcare analytics. Extensions
such as Ridge and Lasso regression add regularization to
handle multicollinearity and prevent overfitting [83].
2) LOGISTIC REGRESSION
Logistic regression, while sounding similar to linear regres-
sion, is a classification algorithm used when the output is
categorical (e.g., binary or multiclass) [84]. It models the
probability that a given input belongs to a specific class using
the logistic function (sigmoid):
σ(z) = 1
1 + e−z
Logistic regression is widely used for risk assessment in
medicine, fraud detection, and marketing. Its interpretability
and simplicity make it a strong baseline model.
3) DECISION TREES
Decision trees are non-parametric models that split the data
into subsets based on feature values, recursively partitioning
the data space and building a tree where each node represents
a decision rule [13], [85].
Advantages:
• Interpretability (transparent decision paths)
• Handles both numerical and categorical data
• Minimal data preprocessing
Limitations:
• Prone to overfitting, especially with deep trees
• Instability with small data changes (high variance)
4) SUPPORT VECTOR MACHINES (SVMs)
SVMs are powerful classifiers that find the hyperplane that
best separates data points of different classes by maximizing
the margin between them [14]. The kernel trick enables
SVMs to handle non-linear data by projecting features into
higher dimensions.
Key concepts:
• Support vectors (critical data points defining the margin)
• Kernel functions (e.g., polynomial, RBF)
• Robust to high-dimensional and sparse data
SVMs are used in bioinformatics, image recognition, and text
categorization.
5) K-NEAREST NEIGHBORS (k-NN)
k-NN is a simple, non-parametric, instance-based learning
algorithm that classifies new data points based on the majority
label among their k-nearest neighbors in feature space [86].
Strengths:
• Intuitive and easy to implement
• Effective on small, well-structured datasets
Weaknesses:
• Computationally expensive for large datasets
• Sensitive to feature scaling and irrelevant features
VOLUME 13, 2025 178311

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
6) ENSEMBLE METHODS
Ensemble techniques combine multiple weak learners to form
a strong model [87], [88]. Major approaches include:
• Bagging (e.g., Random Forests): Trains models on
random bootstrap samples, reducing variance [89].
• Boosting (e.g., AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting):
Sequentially focuses on errors made by previous models,
reducing bias [90], [91].
Ensemble models consistently outperform individual algo-
rithms in competitions and real-world applications. They
offer improved accuracy, robustness, and generalization,
at the cost of interpretability.
B. UNSUPERVISED LEARNING
Unsupervised learning involves extracting hidden structures
from unlabeled data, aiming to uncover patterns or groupings
without predefined labels [56], [79]. It is widely used in
exploratory data analysis, clustering, anomaly detection, and
dimensionality reduction [80].
1) CLUSTERING ALGORITHMS
Clustering is a technique to group similar data points together
based on intrinsic characteristics [92]. Major clustering
algorithms include:
• K-Means Clustering: Partitions data into k clusters by
minimizing intra-cluster variance [93].
• Hierarchical Clustering: Builds a hierarchy of clusters
using agglomerative (bottom-up) or divisive (top-down)
methods [94].
• DBSCAN: Density-based clustering that handles noise
and clusters of arbitrary shapes [95].
Common applications include customer segmentation,
document classification, and anomaly detection.
2) DIMENSIONALITY REDUCTION
Dimensionality reduction methods are essential for noise
removal, visualization, and improving computational effi-
ciency in high-dimensional data [96]. Key algorithms
include:
• Principal Component Analysis (PCA): Transforms
high-dimensional data into lower dimensions by find-
ing directions (principal components) that maximize
variance [97].
• t-SNE and UMAP: Non-linear dimensionality reduc-
tion techniques for visualizing high-dimensional data.
t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding)
preserves local structure for visualization [98], while
UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Pro-
jection) offers better scalability and global structure
preservation [99].
Dimensionality reduction is widely used for exploratory
data analysis, feature engineering, and visualizing complex
datasets.
C. EVALUATION METRICS IN CLASSICAL MACHINE
LEARNING
Performance evaluation is crucial for selecting, tuning, and
comparing machine learning models. The choice of metric
must align with the problem type (classification, regression,
or clustering) and the application context [56], [79], [100].
1) CLASSIFICATION METRICS
For classification tasks, commonly used metrics include:
• Accuracy: The proportion of correctly classified
instances among all samples.
Accuracy = TP + TN
TP + FP + TN + FN
where TP, TN, FP, FN are true positives, true negatives,
false positives, and false negatives, respectively. Accu-
racy can be misleading in imbalanced datasets.
• Precision: The proportion of true positives among all
predicted positives.
Precision = TP
TP + FP
• Recall (Sensitivity): The proportion of true positives
detected among all actual positives.
Recall = TP
TP + FN
• F1-Score: The harmonic mean of precision and recall,
especially useful with class imbalance.
F1 = 2 · Precision · Recall
Precision + Recall
• ROC-AUC: Area Under the Receiver Operating Char-
acteristic Curve, measuring the trade-off between true
positive rate and false positive rate at various thresh-
olds [101]. AUC is robust to class imbalance and
threshold choice.
Other metrics include Matthews Correlation Coefficient
(MCC), Cohen’s Kappa, and log-loss, which provide further
insights into model performance.
2) REGRESSION METRICS
Regression models are evaluated using metrics that quantify
the discrepancy between predicted and true values:
• Mean Squared Error (MSE): Average of squared
prediction errors.
MSE = 1
n
n∑
i=1
(yi − ˆyi)2
MSE penalizes larger errors more heavily.
• Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): The square root
of MSE, providing error in original units.
• Mean Absolute Error (MAE): The average absolute
difference between predicted and actual values.
MAE = 1
n
n∑
i=1
|yi − ˆyi|
MAE is less sensitive to outliers than MSE.

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 5. Common evaluation metrics in classical machine learning.
• R-squared ( R2) Score: Proportion of variance in the
dependent variable explained by the model.
R2 = 1 −
∑n

i=1(yi − ¯y)2
R2 close to 1 indicates good fit; values less than 0 mean
the model is worse than simply predicting the mean.
3) CLUSTERING METRICS
Unsupervised clustering requires metrics that do not rely on
ground truth labels (internal metrics), as well as those that do
(external metrics):
• Silhouette Score: Measures cohesion and separation;
ranges from −1 (poor clustering) to +1 (dense, well-
separated clusters) [102].
• Davies-Bouldin Index: Evaluates intra-cluster similar-
ity and inter-cluster differences; lower values indicate
better clustering [103].
• Adjusted Rand Index, Mutual Information Score:
Used when true cluster labels are available.
4) MODEL VALIDATION AND AVOIDING PITFALLS
Proper evaluation helps prevent underfitting, overfitting,
and model misinterpretation. Key validation techniques
include [79], [100]:
• Train/Test Split: Evaluating performance on held-out
data.
• Cross-Validation: Averaging performance across mul-
tiple splits (e.g., k-fold).
• Confusion Matrix: Visualization of predicted vs. actual
classifications.
• Learning Curves: Detecting bias-variance trade-off.
Selecting the right metric and validation strategy is
essential for building reliable ML models that generalize well
to new data.
D. KEY USE CASES AND APPLICATIONS
Classical machine learning has powered numerous real-world
applications, demonstrating its practicality and effectiveness
across industries [80], [81]. Below are some prominent
sectors and use cases:
• Finance: ML models are employed for credit scor-
ing, fraud detection, algorithmic trading, and risk
assessment [104], [105]. Predictive analytics improve
loan approval processes and enable early detection of
fraudulent transactions.
• Healthcare: Applications include disease prediction
and diagnosis, medical image analysis, drug discovery,
patient risk stratification, and personalized treatment
recommendations [106], [107]. ML aids in analyzing
large volumes of health records and biomedical data,
leading to earlier diagnoses and improved patient out-
comes. Classical machine learning methods have been
widely applied in healthcare, particularly in analyzing
electronic health records (EHRs) to predict patient
outcomes and support clinical decision-making. For
example, Goldstein et al. [108] provide a comprehensive
review of EHR-based predictive modeling, highlighting
VOLUME 13, 2025 178313

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
both opportunities and challenges in deploying ML for
real-world healthcare applications.
• Retail: Retailers leverage machine learning for rec-
ommendation systems, customer segmentation, demand
forecasting, dynamic pricing, and inventory manage-
ment [109]. Personalized recommendations, such as
those used by e-commerce giants, rely on collaborative
filtering and content-based filtering algorithms.
• Transportation:ML optimizes route planning, demand
forecasting, vehicle maintenance scheduling, and
autonomous driving [110], [111]. For example, ride-
sharing platforms use predictive modeling to match
supply and demand and to forecast traffic conditions.
• Other Domains: Applications extend to manufacturing
(predictive maintenance), energy (consumption fore-
casting), agriculture (crop yield prediction), and more.
These use cases highlight how classical ML laid the
groundwork for intelligent systems that address real-world
challenges across diverse domains.
E. LIMITATIONS OF CLASSICAL MACHINE LEARNING
Despite the broad success of classical machine learning,
several inherent limitations paved the way for the rise of
deep learning and modern representation learning approaches
[23], [81].
1) FEATURE ENGINEERING DEPENDENCY
Classical ML models often require extensive manual feature
engineering, relying heavily on domain knowledge to craft
informative input representations [79]. The success of many
algorithms depends more on the quality of the features than
on the learning algorithm itself. Feature selection, extraction,
and transformation can be labor-intensive, and suboptimal
feature sets limit model performance.
2) INABILITY TO PROCESS RAW DATA
Traditional algorithms struggle to process high-dimensional
raw data such as images, audio, and natural language
text [23]. Classical models typically require handcrafted
features and extensive preprocessing pipelines, which can
introduce biases and limit the model’s capacity to generalize
across data modalities.
3) SCALABILITY ISSUES
Many classical ML algorithms, such as k-nearest neighbors
and kernel-based methods, have high computational or mem-
ory costs, limiting their scalability to large datasets [112].
Model training and prediction may become infeasible as data
size and dimensionality grow, posing challenges for big data
applications.
4) LIMITED REPRESENTATION POWER
Linear and other simple models are inherently restricted in
capturing complex, non-linear relationships in data. Although
non-linear classical algorithms (like decision trees or SVMs
with kernel tricks) offer improvements, they often fall short
of the hierarchical feature learning and expressive power
demonstrated by deep neural networks [113].
5) LACK OF ADAPTABILITY
Classical models are generally trained on static datasets
and do not adapt well to changing data distributions or
dynamic environments [87]. Continual learning, transfer
learning, and domain adaptation are not natively supported,
requiring retraining or manual intervention to cope with new
scenarios.
Summary: These limitations motivated the shift towards
deep learning and end-to-end representation learning, which
automate feature extraction, scale efficiently with data and
computation, and demonstrate superior performance on
complex tasks across diverse domains.
F. TRANSITION TOWARD DEEP LEARNING
The limitations of classical machine learning (ML) set the
stage for the rise of deep learning in the 2010s, which
overcame many earlier bottlenecks and enabled dramatic
advances across multiple domains [23], [113], [114]. Several
key enablers fueled this paradigm shift:
• Massive Data Availability: The proliferation of digital
content, social media, sensors, and IoT devices led
to unprecedented volumes of labeled and unlabeled
data [115].
• Advancements in GPU Hardware: Graphics Pro-
cessing Units (GPUs) offered the parallel computing
capacity necessary to efficiently train large, deep neural
networks, greatly reducing model training times [116].
• Open-source Libraries: Frameworks such as Ten-
sorFlow and PyTorch provided accessible, flexible,
and scalable tools for deep learning research and
production [117], [118].
• Architectural Innovations: Novel deep learning
architectures-such as Convolutional Neural Networks
(CNNs) for vision [21], Recurrent Neural Networks
(RNNs) for sequence data [25], and Transformers for
NLP and multimodal tasks [119]-enabled automatic fea-
ture extraction and powerful representational learning.
Deep learning shifted the focus from manual feature
engineering to end-to-end, data-driven feature learning,
enabling breakthroughs in fields like computer vision, speech
recognition, and natural language processing [23], [120].
Models such as CNNs and Transformers can automatically
learn hierarchical representations from raw data, outperform-
ing classical approaches in many unstructured data scenarios.
1) CONTINUED RELEVANCE OF CLASSICAL ML
While deep learning dominates many current AI benchmarks,
classical machine learning remains foundational for a variety
of structured-data tasks due to its interpretability, simplicity,
and efficiency [80]. Classical ML algorithms continue to
be widely used in domains where data is limited or feature
interpretability is crucial.
178314 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 6. Comparison of common machine learning algorithms.
2) FROM FOUNDATIONS TO MODERN AI
The principles established by classical ML-generalization,
pattern recognition, and data-driven decision making-remain
central to modern AI. The next phase in AI’s evolution
expands on these foundations with deeper architectures,
hierarchical learning, and more autonomous, self-adaptive
systems, forming the basis for today’s deep learning and
reinforcement learning paradigms.
IV. RISE OF DEEP LEARNING (DL)
The rise of deep learning (DL) has fundamentally trans-
formed the landscape of artificial intelligence, empowering
systems to perform tasks previously considered exclusively
within the domain of human intelligence. From image
and speech recognition to natural language processing
and autonomous systems, deep learning has catalyzed an
AI renaissance, driving breakthroughs in both academic
research and commercial applications [23], [113], [114].
Unlike classical machine learning approaches, which require
manual feature engineering and struggle with complex
data representations, deep learning models are capable
of automatically learning hierarchical features from raw
data. This section explores the evolution of deep learning,
critical breakthroughs in neural network architectures such
as convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural
networks (RNNs), and long short-term memory (LSTM)
networks, and the technological advancements that fueled the
AI resurgence.
A. ORIGINS AND FOUNDATIONS OF DEEP LEARNING
Deep learning is a subfield of machine learning that focuses
on learning data representations using multiple layers of neu-
ral networks. While the concept of neural networks dates back
to the 1940s, deep learning did not gain widespread adoption
until the 2010s, due to earlier limitations in computing power,
data availability, and algorithmic inefficiency [114].
1) PERCEPTRON AND EARLY NEURAL NETWORKS
The perceptron, introduced by Frank Rosenblatt in 1958, was
one of the earliest models capable of binary classification.
FIGURE 5. Deep learning workflow.
It adjusted its weights based on input-output pairs using
a simple learning rule. However, its inability to solve
non-linearly separable problems (such as XOR) led to
skepticism about neural networks-especially after Minsky
and Papert’s critique in 1969, which contributed to the first
‘‘AI winter’’ [75].
2) MULTILAYER PERCEPTRON (MLP) AND
BACKPROPAGATION
In the 1980s, the development of the backpropagation algo-
rithm allowed for efficient training of multilayer perceptrons
(MLPs), making it possible to train deeper networks [15].
Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams demonstrated that back-
propagation could be used to adjust weights in hidden layers,
enabling networks to learn complex, non-linear functions.
Despite these advances, deep networks remained impractical
for several decades due to limited data, slow hardware, and
VOLUME 13, 2025 178315

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
FIGURE 6. Deep learning models architecture.
the vanishing gradient problem, in which gradients became
too small to effectively update weights in deeper layers [121].
B. BREAKTHROUGHS IN NEURAL NETWORK
ARCHITECTURES
1) CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORKS (CNNs)
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are specialized
architectures designed for processing data with a grid-like
topology, such as images [16]. Inspired by the structure of the
visual cortex, CNNs use convolutional filters to automatically
extract spatial hierarchies of features.
Key concepts in CNNs include:
• Convolutional layers: Learn local features using train-
able filters shared across spatial locations.
• Pooling layers: Downsample feature maps to reduce
dimensionality and computation.
• Fully connected layers: Integrate and classify
high-level features extracted by earlier layers.
The breakthrough moment for CNNs came in 2012 with
AlexNet [21], developed by Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and
Hinton. Trained on the large-scale ImageNet dataset, AlexNet
achieved a dramatic reduction in classification error and
reignited global interest in deep learning. Subsequent
CNN architectures, such as VGGNet [122], GoogLeNet
(Inception) [123], ResNet [124], and EfficientNet [125],
introduced deeper networks, skip connections, and improved
computational efficiency, further advancing the state of the
art.
2) RECURRENT NEURAL NETWORKS (RNNs)
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are designed for mod-
eling sequential data and time-series prediction. Unlike
feedforward networks, RNNs maintain internal hidden states
that allow the network to capture temporal dependencies
across time steps [126].
Common applications of RNNs include:
• Speech recognition
• Language modeling
• Machine translation
• Financial forecasting
However, standard RNNs suffer from vanishing and
exploding gradients, which limit their ability to learn
long-term dependencies in sequences [121].
3) LONG SHORT-TERM MEMORY (LSTM) NETWORKS
Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, introduced
by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber in 1997 [25], address the
limitations of traditional RNNs by using specialized gating
mechanisms to regulate the flow of information.
Key components of LSTM:
• Input gate: Controls which values from the input are
used to update the internal cell state.
• Forget gate: Determines which information to discard
from the cell state.
• Output gate: Modulates the output based on the internal
state.
LSTMs became the dominant architecture for NLP tasks
in the pre-transformer era, enabling state-of-the-art perfor-
mance in machine translation, question answering, and text
generation [127], [128].
C. APPLICATIONS IN COMPUTER VISION AND NATURAL
LANGUAGE PROCESSING
1) COMPUTER VISION
Deep learning has fundamentally transformed the field of
computer vision, enabling machines to perceive, interpret,
and analyze visual data with levels of accuracy that rival
and in some cases surpass human capabilities [21], [23].
One of the earliest demonstrations of this revolution came
through large-scale image classification benchmarks such as
ImageNet [17] and CIFAR-10 [129], where convolutional
neural networks (CNNs) achieved remarkable improvements
over traditional computer vision methods. Beyond classi-
fication, advances in architectures such as YOLO [130],
SSD [131], and Faster R-CNN [132] enabled accurate
object detection by not only identifying objects in images
but also localizing them within bounding boxes, making
large-scale applications in autonomous driving and video
surveillance feasible. Deep learning has enabled substantial
progress in computer-aided diagnosis and detection tasks.
Recent advances in medical imaging, such as convolutional
neural networks for endoscopy and colonoscopy, have shown
promise in improving early disease detection. Soleymanjahi
et al. [133] demonstrated state-of-the-art deep learning
models for polyp detection in colonoscopy, underscoring the
clinical potential of DL-based screening tools.
Another significant milestone was the introduction of deep
learning for image segmentation, which partitions images
into semantically meaningful regions. Architectures like
178316 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
U-Net [134] and DeepLab [135] provided robust solutions
for medical diagnostics and satellite image analysis by
offering pixel-level precision. Facial recognition, powered
by systems such as FaceNet [136] and DeepFace [137],
further demonstrated how deep representations could be
harnessed for highly accurate biometric verification and
identity recognition. Perhaps the most impactful domain of
application has been medical imaging, where deep neural
networks are used to analyze CT scans, MRIs, and X-rays
for tumor detection, anomaly identification, and automated
diagnosis [138]. Today, computer vision models are routinely
deployed in diverse areas ranging from industrial inspection
and quality assurance to agricultural crop monitoring and
defense, illustrating the profound impact of deep learning on
this domain.
2) NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING (NLP)
Natural language processing has similarly undergone a
dramatic transformation with the advent of deep learning.
Earlier generations of NLP systems relied on hand-crafted
linguistic features and statistical models, which were limited
in their ability to capture semantic nuance and contextual
relationships. Deep learning addressed these limitations
by enabling models to learn distributed representations
of language directly from raw text data [120]. A crucial
breakthrough was the development of word embeddings such
as Word2Vec [38] and GloVe [39], which represented words
as continuous vectors in high-dimensional space and captured
semantic similarity in a way that statistical approaches could
not.
Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), particularly Long
Short-Term Memory (LSTM) [25] and Gated Recurrent
Unit (GRU) [139] architectures, extended these capabilities
by modeling sequential dependencies in text, laying the
foundation for applications in machine translation, speech
recognition, and text generation. The encoder–decoder
framework introduced by Sutskever et al. [128] advanced
translation and summarization by enabling sequence-to-
sequence learning. However, it was the advent of attention
mechanisms and the Transformer architecture [119] that
revolutionized NLP, allowing models to capture long-range
dependencies, understand context more effectively, and scale
to unprecedented levels of performance.
These advancements made possible a wide range of
applications that are now integral to everyday technol-
ogy. Text classification systems categorize documents and
emails with high accuracy, while sentiment analysis tools
assess public opinion and consumer feedback across social
media platforms. Machine translation systems provide near
real-time multilingual communication, and named entity
recognition supports information extraction from legal,
medical, and financial documents. Perhaps most visible to
the general public, deep learning has powered the rise of
conversational agents and virtual assistants that can interact
in natural language, including chatbots, voice assistants, and
customer support automation. Together, these applications
highlight how deep learning has turned NLP into one of the
most commercially impactful areas of AI, bridging the gap
between human communication and computational systems.
D. GPU ACCELERATION AND DATA AVAILABILITY
1) HARDWARE ADVANCES: GPUs AND TPUs
The resurgence of deep learning has been fueled by major
advances in hardware, particularly the use of Graphics
Processing Units (GPUs) for training neural networks.
GPUs, with their highly parallel architecture, are ideally
suited for the intensive matrix operations required in deep
learning [116]. NVIDIA’s CUDA platform and the cuDNN
library have played a critical role in making GPU acceler-
ation accessible to researchers and practitioners [18]. More
recently, Google’s Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) have
provided additional performance optimizations for large-
scale models [140].
2) BIG DATA AND OPEN DATASETS
The rise of big data has provided the massive datasets needed
to train deep networks effectively. Large, publicly available
datasets such as ImageNet for image classification [17],
COCO for object detection [141], MNIST, CIFAR-10, and
Fashion-MNIST for digit and object recognition [129], [142],
and Wikipedia and Common Crawl for NLP tasks have
enabled consistent benchmarking and rapid progress in model
development.
Crowdsourcing platforms (e.g., Amazon Mechanical Turk)
and user-generated content from sources such as social
media, sensors, and web crawls have further increased the
availability of labeled data [143]. These developments have
played a pivotal role in scaling up the complexity and
accuracy of deep learning models.
E. THE ROLE OF DEEP LEARNING IN THE AI RESURGENCE
Deep learning has catalyzed a renaissance in artificial intelli-
gence, transforming the field from a predominantly academic
pursuit into a global driver of technological innovation [23],
[114]. Its rise was marked by high-profile breakthroughs
that showcased its power in perception, reasoning, and
autonomous decision-making.
1) MILESTONE ACHIEVEMENTS
The modern deep learning era began with the victory of
AlexNet in the 2012 ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recog-
nition Challenge, which demonstrated the superiority of
deep convolutional neural networks over handcrafted feature
approaches [21]. This success was followed by DeepMind’s
AlphaGo, which in 2016 defeated a world champion in
Go by combining deep neural networks with reinforcement
learning and Monte Carlo tree search [144]. Beyond research
milestones, deep learning has also enabled the development
of autonomous vehicles, with models powering real-time
perception systems that detect, classify, and track objects
VOLUME 13, 2025 178317

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
in complex environments [145]. At the consumer level,
deep learning has fueled virtual assistants such as Siri,
Alexa, and Google Assistant, which rely on advanced
speech recognition and natural language understanding for
conversational interactions [22].
2) INDUSTRY ADOPTION
Deep learning is now deeply embedded in the products of
major technology companies. It powers web search engines,
recommendation systems, content moderation, and machine
translation at scale [146]. Startups and enterprises outside of
big tech have also integrated deep learning into domains such
as healthcare, where models support medical imaging and
diagnostics; finance, where they are used for fraud detection
and algorithmic trading; and agriculture, where they monitor
crops and optimize yields. These deployments highlight the
versatility of deep learning and its direct impact on the global
economy.
3) ACADEMIC AND RESEARCH GROWTH
The research community has experienced exponential growth
in deep learning publications, particularly at conferences such
as NeurIPS, ICML, and CVPR. The development of open-
source frameworks, including TensorFlow, PyTorch, and
Keras, has democratized access to deep learning tools, reduc-
ing barriers for researchers and practitioners [117], [118],
[147]. This democratization has enabled unprecedented
collaboration between academia and industry, accelerating
progress in both theory and application.
F. LIMITATIONS AND CHALLENGES OF DEEP LEARNING
Despite its transformative successes, deep learning continues
to face important limitations [113], [148], [149], [150], [151].
These challenges highlight the need for new paradigms that
complement or extend deep neural networks.
1) DATA HUNGER AND COMPUTATIONAL COST
Deep learning models typically require massive labeled
datasets for training, making them impractical in domains
where data is limited, costly, or privacy-sensitive. Moreover,
training state-of-the-art models demands immense computa-
tional power, often measured in thousands of GPU or TPU
hours, along with significant energy consumption [152]. This
restricts participation to well-funded corporations and elite
research labs, raising concerns about accessibility and equity.
2) LACK OF INTERPRETABILITY
Deep networks often operate as opaque black boxes, offering
little insight into their decision-making processes. This lack
of interpretability poses serious risks in high-stakes domains
such as healthcare, finance, and law, where transparency
is necessary for accountability [149], [150]. Without clear
explanations, trust in AI systems may be undermined.
3) BIAS, FAIRNESS, AND GENERALIZATION
Because deep learning models learn directly from historical
data, they are susceptible to encoding and amplifying biases
present in the training set [151], [153]. This can lead
to discriminatory or unfair outcomes in applications such
as hiring, lending, and criminal justice. Generalization is
another concern: models often struggle when faced with
distribution shifts, adversarial inputs, or limited training data,
raising questions about robustness and reliability.
4) EMERGING MITIGATION STRATEGIES
Ongoing research seeks to address these challenges through
technical advances and new learning paradigms. Explainable
AI (XAI) methods aim to make black-box models more
interpretable, while fairness-aware algorithms and dataset
balancing strategies are being developed to reduce bias. Tech-
niques such as model compression, pruning, and quantization
aim to lower computational costs, making deep learning
more sustainable and widely accessible. In parallel, federated
learning and transfer learning are enabling the training
of models across distributed data sources and domains
with fewer labeled samples [154], [155]. These innovations
reflect a growing emphasis on responsible, efficient, and
inclusive AI.
In summary, deep learning has provided the foundation for
modern artificial intelligence, driving breakthroughs in per-
ception, cognition, and decision-making. At the same time,
its limitations underscore the need for continued innovation in
interpretability, efficiency, fairness, and robustness. As such,
deep learning is best understood not as an endpoint, but as
a cornerstone for the next wave of AI advances, including
large language models, reinforcement learning, and toward
the long-term goal of general intelligence.
V. REINFORCEMENT LEARNING (RL) AND
AUTONOMOUS AI
Reinforcement Learning (RL) represents a significant shift
in the artificial intelligence paradigm, moving beyond super-
vised and unsupervised learning toward an approach where
agents learn through interaction with their environment [156].
In RL, an agent seeks to learn optimal behaviors via trial and
error, receiving feedback in the form of rewards or penalties.
This framework has enabled major advances in autonomous
decision-making systems, robotics, and game-playing AI.
This section covers the foundational principles of RL, its
integration with deep learning, major milestones, and current
challenges.
A. BASICS OF REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
At its core, reinforcement learning is a feedback-driven learn-
ing paradigm. An agent selects actions within an environment
to maximize cumulative reward. The environment responds to
each action and updates its state accordingly.
178318 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
FIGURE 7. Reinforcement learning workflow.
1) MARKOV DECISION PROCESSES (MDPs)
RL problems are typically formulated as Markov Decision
Processes (MDPs), defined by:
• S: Set of states
• A: Set of actions
• P(s′|s, a): Transition probability from state s to s′ given
action a
• R(s, a): Reward function
• γ : Discount factor (0 ≤ γ ≤ 1) that determines the
importance of future rewards
The agent’s objective is to learn a policy π(a|s) that
maximizes expected cumulative reward over time.
2) Q-LEARNING
Q-learning is a model-free, off-policy RL algorithm that seeks
to learn the optimal action-value function, Q(s, a), estimating
the expected return of taking action a in state s and following
the optimal policy thereafter. The Q-learning update rule is:
Q(s, a) ← Q(s, a) + α
[
r + γ max
a′
Q(s′, a′) − Q(s, a)
]
where α is the learning rate, r is the immediate reward, and γ
is the discount factor [157]. Q-learning is simple and effective
but can struggle with large or continuous state-action spaces.
3) POLICY GRADIENTS
Unlike Q-learning, which estimates value functions, policy
gradient methods directly optimize the policy. The agent
learns a parameterized policy πθ (a|s) to maximize expected
rewards. A common method is the REINFORCE algorithm,
with the update:
1θ = α ∇θ log πθ (a|s) R
where R is the observed reward. Policy gradients handle
continuous action spaces and stochastic policies, but often
suffer from high variance [158].
B. MILESTONES IN REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
Reinforcement learning has powered some of the most
notable achievements in AI, especially in domains requiring
strategic planning, adaptability, and long-term reward
optimization.
1) AlphaGo AND AlphaZero (DeepMind)
AlphaGo, developed by DeepMind, marked a historic mile-
stone in artificial intelligence by defeating world champion
Lee Sedol in the ancient game of Go in 2016 [144].
This achievement showcased the power of combining rein-
forcement learning with Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)
and deep neural networks. The system employed policy
networks to identify promising moves and value networks
to evaluate board positions, both trained via reinforcement
learning and extensive self-play. The success of AlphaGo
demonstrated that AI systems could not only match but
exceed human performance in domains long considered
