resistant to automation due to their complexity and vast
search spaces.
Its successor, AlphaZero, pushed this paradigm further
by mastering Go, Chess, and Shogi without any human
gameplay data [159]. Unlike AlphaGo, which incorporated
expert games during training, AlphaZero relied exclusively
on reinforcement learning and self-play, learning strategies
from scratch to achieve superhuman performance across
multiple board games. These achievements illustrated the
transformative potential of self-play as a learning mechanism
and established deep reinforcement learning as a dominant
framework for strategic decision-making.
2) OpenAI FIVE
OpenAI Five extended reinforcement learning from board
games into highly dynamic and cooperative environments,
specifically the multiplayer online battle arena game Dota
2 [160]. Training required millions of simulated matches
played in parallel, where agents developed sophisticated
cooperative strategies, long-term planning capabilities, and
the ability to adapt to diverse and unpredictable human
opponents. The system utilized Proximal Policy Optimization
(PPO), a scalable reinforcement learning algorithm, and ben-
efited from large-scale distributed simulations to accelerate
the learning process.
OpenAI Five’s success was significant because it high-
lighted how reinforcement learning could generalize beyond
perfect-information games into environments that are par-
tially observable, multi-agent, and rich in stochastic events.
The ability to coordinate in team-based gameplay demon-
strated that reinforcement learning can capture not just
individual optimization but also collaborative strategies-an
essential step toward real-world autonomous systems.
3) ROBOTICS AND AUTONOMOUS SYSTEMS
Beyond games, reinforcement learning has shown grow-
ing promise in robotics and autonomous systems. Appli-
cations include navigation in unstructured environments,
robotic grasping and manipulation, and agile locomotion for
legged robots [161], [162]. Robots learn policies through
VOLUME 13, 2025 178319

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
FIGURE 8. Deep learning integration with reinforcement learning
workflow.
interactions with their environments, often starting in sim-
ulation where millions of episodes can be run safely and
efficiently. To ensure that policies trained in simulation
transfer effectively to the physical world, researchers employ
techniques such as domain randomization, where variability
is introduced into simulated environments, and fine-tuning,
where policies are adjusted through limited real-world
experience [163].
These methods are critical for overcoming the ‘‘sim-
to-real’’ gap, one of the central challenges in robotics.
Reinforcement learning has already enabled advances in
autonomous navigation, dexterous object manipulation, and
robust locomotion, pushing robots closer to human-like
adaptability. As computational resources expand and simula-
tion fidelity improves, reinforcement learning is expected to
play a central role in developing intelligent, general-purpose
robotic systems.
C. INTEGRATION OF DEEP LEARNING AND
REINFORCEMENT LEARNING (DEEP RL)
The convergence of deep learning and reinforcement learning
has produced a new class of powerful agents capable of
addressing tasks that involve high-dimensional and unstruc-
tured data such as raw images, audio, and video streams. This
approach, known as Deep Reinforcement Learning (Deep
RL), leverages neural networks as function approximators to
represent policies, value functions, or models of the envi-
ronment, thereby extending the scalability and applicability
of reinforcement learning to domains previously thought
intractable [164].
1) DEEP Q-NETWORKS (DQN)
One of the most significant breakthroughs in Deep RL was the
development of the Deep Q-Network (DQN) by DeepMind
in 2015, which achieved human-level performance across
a wide range of Atari 2600 video games using raw
pixels as input [165]. DQN merged traditional Q-learning
with convolutional neural networks to approximate the
action-value function Q(s, a), allowing the system to map
visual inputs directly to action choices. Its impact stemmed
from three innovations. First, the use of experience replay
broke temporal correlations by sampling from a buffer of
past transitions, improving stability. Second, the introduction
of target networks, which were updated less frequently than
the primary network, prevented divergence by stabilizing
Q-value estimates. Finally, its end-to-end learning approach
eliminated the need for manual feature engineering, enabling
the network to learn game strategies directly from raw
pixel streams. DQN not only solved previously intractable
reinforcement learning benchmarks but also inspired numer-
ous extensions, including Double DQN, Dueling DQN, and
Prioritized Experience Replay, which remain influential in
modern Deep RL.
2) ASYNCHRONOUS ADVANTAGE ACTOR-CRITIC (A3C)
Despite DQN’s success, its reliance on replay buffers and its
relatively slow training limited scalability. The Asynchronous
Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C) algorithm addressed these
limitations by introducing parallelism into reinforcement
learning [166]. A3C trained multiple agents simultaneously,
each interacting with its own copy of the environment, and
combined their updates asynchronously. This design reduced
the correlation between experiences, accelerated training, and
significantly improved robustness.
A3C employed an actor-critic framework, in which the
actor updated policy parameters to maximize expected return
while the critic estimated the value function to reduce
variance in the gradient estimates. By incorporating the
advantage function A(s, a) = Q(s, a) − V (s), A3C provided
more informative updates that stabilized learning. This
algorithm was particularly important because it demon-
strated that reinforcement learning could scale efficiently
on standard multi-core CPUs without the need for massive
GPU resources, thereby broadening accessibility. It also laid
the groundwork for later actor-critic methods that further
improved sample efficiency and stability.
3) PROXIMAL POLICY OPTIMIZATION (PPO)
The development of Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)
further enhanced the stability and efficiency of policy
gradient methods [167]. PPO was designed to prevent the
destructive large policy updates that had plagued earlier
algorithms. It achieved this through a clipped objective
function that restricted how much the new policy could
diverge from the old policy at each update step. By combining
this constraint with multiple epochs of minibatch updates on
the same trajectory data, PPO struck an effective balance
between sample efficiency and training stability.
The appeal of PPO lies in its simplicity and reliability.
It is comparatively easy to implement, robust across diverse
reinforcement learning tasks, and adaptable to both discrete
and continuous action spaces. These qualities have made PPO
the most widely adopted reinforcement learning algorithm
178320 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
in practice, used extensively in robotics, natural language
processing, autonomous driving, and video game AI.
4) CURRENT TRENDS AND EXTENSIONS
Deep RL continues to evolve rapidly as researchers address
persistent challenges of sample inefficiency, instability, and
poor generalization. Off-policy algorithms such as Deep
Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Soft Actor-Critic
(SAC), and Twin Delayed DDPG (TD3) have improved
learning efficiency in continuous control tasks by lever-
aging replay buffers and entropy maximization strategies.
Hierarchical reinforcement learning has enabled agents to
acquire multi-level skills for long-term planning, while
multi-agent reinforcement learning has unlocked cooperative
and competitive behaviors in complex environments. Trans-
fer learning and meta-learning have made it possible for
agents to adapt more rapidly to new tasks by leveraging prior
experience, a critical requirement for scaling toward real-
world autonomy.
The synergy of deep learning and reinforcement learning
has already transformed domains such as robotics, gaming,
and natural language processing. Robots trained with Deep
RL now demonstrate dexterous object manipulation and
agile locomotion, while agents in virtual environments
showcase unprecedented strategic and adaptive capabilities.
As research progresses, Deep RL is expected to play a pivotal
role in the development of autonomous systems that can
reason, adapt, and act effectively in real-world environments,
bringing AI closer to general intelligence.
D. CHALLENGES AND LIMITATIONS OF REINFORCEMENT
LEARNING
Despite remarkable progress, reinforcement learning (RL)
continues to face several significant challenges that limit its
widespread deployment, particularly in safety-critical and
real-world scenarios [168], [169], [170]. Key limitations
include:
1) SAMPLE INEFFICIENCY
Deep RL algorithms typically require millions of environ-
ment interactions to learn near-optimal policies, making them
highly data-inefficient [171]. This is particularly problematic
in domains such as robotics or autonomous driving, where
each trial can be costly, time-consuming, or dangerous to
collect.
2) SPARSE AND DELAYED REWARDS
Many real-world tasks provide sparse or significantly delayed
rewards, complicating the learning process. When feedback
is infrequent, RL agents struggle with the credit assignment
problem, i.e., determining which past actions were responsi-
ble for eventual success or failure [172].
3) STABILITY AND CONVERGENCE
Training deep RL agents is notoriously unstable and sensitive
to hyperparameter selection and initialization. Policies can
oscillate, collapse, or even diverge during training, requiring
careful tuning and extensive experimentation [173].
4) EXPLORATION VS. EXPLOITATION
Striking a balance between exploring new strategies and
exploiting known high-reward actions is a core RL challenge.
Ineffective exploration can lead to sub-optimal policies or
premature convergence, while excessive exploration wastes
valuable resources [174].
5) GENERALIZATION AND TRANSFER LEARNING
Policies learned in one environment often fail to generalize
to even slightly altered conditions or new tasks. Robust
transfer learning and domain adaptation remain open research
problems, impeding RL’s scalability across diverse real-
world applications [175], [176].
6) SAFETY AND REAL-WORLD CONSTRAINTS
Many RL algorithms lack inherent mechanisms for safe
exploration or constraint satisfaction. In fields such as
robotics, healthcare, or autonomous vehicles, unsafe actions
can result in irreparable damage. Research in safe RL seeks
to integrate safety constraints and risk awareness directly into
the learning process [177], [178].
In summary, reinforcement learning has transformed the
landscape of artificial intelligence by enabling agents to
autonomously learn complex sequential decision-making in
dynamic environments. The synergy of RL and deep learning
empowers agents to learn directly from unstructured sensory
data, catalyzing breakthroughs in games, robotics, and con-
trol. However, challenges related to data efficiency, stability,
generalization, and safety remain substantial barriers to prac-
tical, large-scale deployment. Ongoing research addressing
these challenges is crucial for advancing toward robust,
reliable, and general-purpose autonomous AI systems.
VI. THE TRANSFORMER REVOLUTION AND LARGE
LANGUAGE MODELS (LLMs)
A. INTRODUCTION TO ATTENTION MECHANISMS
The evolution of Natural Language Processing (NLP) has
been punctuated by several paradigm shifts, but none has
proven more transformative than the introduction of attention
mechanisms. Prior to attention, sequential models such as
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Long Short-Term Mem-
ory (LSTM) networks, and Convolutional Neural Networks
(CNNs) dominated the landscape. While these models made
considerable progress on sequential tasks, they struggled
with modeling long-range dependencies, suffered from
vanishing gradients, and faced challenges in parallelization
and scalability.
The attention mechanism was developed to overcome these
limitations. Rather than compressing an entire input sequence
VOLUME 13, 2025 178321

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 7. Key transformer models and variants (2018–2025): milestones, domains, and impact.
into a fixed-length vector, attention enables the model to
dynamically focus on relevant parts of the input at each step
of output generation. Mathematically, attention computes
a weighted sum over the input representations, where the
weights (often called attention scores) reflect the relevance
of each token to the current output step.
This concept was first widely adopted in neural machine
translation, where attention mechanisms allowed decoders to
align with specific words or phrases in the source language,
yielding substantial improvements in translation quality and
interpretability [191]. The attention paradigm has since
become foundational in modern NLP architectures, paving
the way for models capable of capturing global context,
supporting parallel computation, and achieving state-of-the-
art results across a variety of tasks.
B. THE TRANSFORMER MODEL
The seminal paper ‘‘Attention is All You Need’’ by
Vaswani et al. [119] introduced the Transformer architecture,
a model that revolutionized natural language processing
(NLP) by eliminating recurrence in favor of attention mecha-
nisms. Unlike earlier approaches, the Transformer processes
sequences in parallel, relying exclusively on attention and
position-wise feed-forward layers. This innovation enabled
faster training and set new benchmarks on sequence-to-
sequence tasks such as machine translation.
1) CORE COMPONENTS OF THE TRANSFORMER
The Transformer model is built on several key components
that work together to process and generate sequences of text.
The first of these is the multi-head self-attention mechanism,
which enables the model to weigh relationships between
words across a sentence. For example, in the sentence ‘‘The
cat sat on the mat because it was tired, ’’ the model must
recognize that the word ‘‘it’’ refers to ‘‘the cat.’’ Self-attention
achieves this by computing relevance scores between all
word pairs, while multiple attention heads capture different
contextual relationships simultaneously, allowing the model
to build a richer understanding of language structure.
A second critical element is positional encoding. Unlike
recurrent networks, Transformers do not process inputs
in order; instead, they treat the entire sequence simul-
taneously. Positional encodings are therefore added to
word embeddings, functioning like coordinates that tell the
model whether a word comes first or last in a sequence.
This allows the model to distinguish between semantically
178322 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
distinct sentences such as ‘‘dog bites man’’ and ‘‘man bites
dog. ’’
The architecture also includes position-wise feed-forward
layers, which independently transform the representation
of each token after the attention step. These layers add
non-linearity, enabling the model to capture more complex
relationships beyond what self-attention alone can provide.
Finally, residual connections and layer normalization ensure
stable training. By allowing gradients to flow more effectively
across multiple stacked layers, they prevent issues such
as vanishing or exploding signals and contribute to the
scalability of the architecture.
The complete Transformer is structured as an encoder-
decoder system, with both components built from repeated
layers combining multi-head attention and feed-forward sub-
networks. This modular design has proven highly flexible
and has become the foundation for modern large language
models.
2) ADVANTAGES OVER RNNs AND LSTMs
The shift from recurrent neural networks to Transformers
marked a turning point in natural language processing. RNNs,
including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated
Recurrent Unit (GRU) models, were once state-of-the-art for
tasks such as machine translation and sequence prediction.
However, these models processed input sequentially, one
token at a time, making training slow and limiting paral-
lelization. They also struggled with long-range dependencies;
as sequences grew longer, information often faded due to
vanishing gradients, making it difficult to model distant
relationships.
Transformers eliminated these barriers. Their attention
mechanisms directly connect words regardless of their
distance in a sentence, allowing them to capture long-term
dependencies more effectively than RNNs. Moreover,
because all tokens in a sequence are processed simultane-
ously, training can be massively parallelized, significantly
reducing computation time. The architecture also scales
smoothly with increases in data and model size, which
has driven consistent performance improvements across
natural language benchmarks. These advantages explain why
the Transformer has displaced RNNs and LSTMs as the
dominant paradigm and why it continues to underpin nearly
all large-scale NLP systems today.
3) RAG AND VECTOR-BASED INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
a: MOTIVATION FOR RAG
Large language models excel at generating fluent and con-
textually coherent responses, but they remain limited by their
parametric memory. Once trained, their knowledge is static,
frozen at the time of dataset collection, and cannot easily
be updated without retraining at extreme computational cost.
This limitation not only constrains factual accuracy but also
leads to hallucinations when models attempt to answer ques-
tions outside their training distribution. Retrieval-Augmented
FIGURE 9. RAG workflow.
Generation (RAG) [46] was proposed to overcome these
issues by coupling generative models with dynamic, non-
parametric memory in the form of vector databases.
b: HOW RAG WORKS
In a typical RAG pipeline, a user’s query is transformed into
a dense vector representation by a neural encoder such as
BERT, Sentence-BERT, or DPR. Instead of relying solely
on internal parameters, the system searches for semantically
similar embeddings in a large external knowledge base.
These retrieved passages are appended to the model’s input
prompt, enabling the language model to generate answers
that are grounded in verifiable evidence. In practice, this
framework allows a generative model to remain lightweight
while offloading factual recall to a scalable retrieval system.
Unlike static LLMs, RAG-based models can be updated
continuously by refreshing or extending the indexed corpus
without retraining the base model.
c: VECTOR DATABASES
Vector databases serve as the core infrastructure enabling
RAG. Systems such as FAISS [43], Pinecone [192], and
Weaviate efficiently store billions of embeddings and pro-
vide approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search in high-
dimensional space. Unlike keyword-based search engines,
which rely on lexical overlap, vector databases retrieve
documents based on semantic similarity, making them more
robust for natural language queries. Modern implementations
achieve sub-second retrieval latencies over corpora contain-
ing billions of vectors by leveraging data structures such
as inverted file systems, hierarchical navigable small-world
(HNSW) graphs, and product quantization. These methods
balance memory efficiency with retrieval accuracy, ensuring
that the generative model receives high-quality supporting
context.
d: COMPUTATIONAL AND MEMORY COSTS
While RAG improves factual grounding, it introduces
nontrivial computational overhead. Maintaining a vector
index of billions of documents may require terabytes
VOLUME 13, 2025 178323

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
of memory, with each embedding consuming hundreds
of bytes depending on dimensionality and precision. For
example, a corpus of 1 billion documents encoded with 768-
dimensional float32 vectors requires nearly 3 TB of storage.
Querying such indexes demands specialized ANN libraries
and high-throughput GPUs or CPUs optimized for similarity
search. Additionally, augmenting the LLM’s context with
retrieved passages increases inference cost: longer context
windows require more GPU memory and lead to quadratic
scaling of self-attention operations. Thus, production RAG
systems often combine memory-sharded vector DBs with
inference clusters, carefully balancing retrieval latency and
model throughput. The overall computational footprint can
exceed that of a standalone LLM, particularly when real-time
responses are required.
e: REAL-WORLD DEPLOYMENTS AND LIMITATIONS
Despite these costs, RAG has already proven transforma-
tive in several domains. In enterprise applications, com-
panies use RAG to index proprietary datasets such as
technical manuals, contracts, or customer support tickets,
enabling domain-specific conversational agents that deliver
grounded responses. In healthcare, RAG systems retrieve
clinical literature or patient history, supporting evidence-
based decision-making while reducing diagnostic errors.
In the legal sector, lawyers employ RAG-driven tools
for case summarization and precedent retrieval, improving
efficiency in legal research. However, integrating RAG at
scale poses challenges, including ensuring the freshness
of indexed knowledge, maintaining retrieval quality across
diverse domains, and managing infrastructure overhead.
Furthermore, RAG does not fully eliminate hallucinations,
as models may misinterpret retrieved evidence or blend
multiple sources incorrectly. Nevertheless, the combination
of vector-based retrieval with generative modeling represents
a critical step toward trustworthy, adaptable, and continually
updated AI systems.
C. CONTEXT LENGTH AND MEMORY IN TRANSFORMERS
Early transformer-based models, such as BERT and
GPT-2, were constrained by relatively short context windows
(typically 512 to 2048 tokens). This limitation restricted
their ability to reason over long documents, multi-turn
conversations, or extended sequences of code. As a
result, handling long-range dependencies required external
mechanisms such as chunking, retrieval-based augmentation,
or hierarchical encoders.
Recent advances, however, have significantly alleviated
these constraints. OpenAI’s GPT-4-turbo supports context
windows of up to 128k tokens, and Claude 2 extends this
further to 200k tokens, with Claude 3 models offering over
1 million tokens [30], [193]. Similarly, research on efficient
attention mechanisms—including sparse attention, linear-
time attention, and state space models—has enabled scaling
to million-token sequences while maintaining computational
feasibility [194], [195].
These breakthroughs mean that context length is no
longer the bottleneck it once was. Large language models
can now ingest entire books, codebases, or multi-session
dialogues, allowing for more coherent reasoning and richer
memory. Instead of being constrained by window size, the
challenge has shifted toward optimizing retrieval, summa-
rization, and selective attention over extremely long contexts.
For instance, hybrid architectures integrate long-context
transformers with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
pipelines to efficiently access relevant knowledge from
vector databases while still leveraging extended windows for
detailed reasoning [43], [46].
In summary, while early transformers struggled with
limited memory, modern long-context models have expanded
horizons dramatically. The frontier is now less about
raw sequence length and more about designing intelligent
mechanisms for navigating, prioritizing, and reasoning over
massive streams of information.
D. KEY LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS (LLMs): BERT, GPT-3,
PALM, CLAUDE, GEMINI
The introduction of the Transformer architecture provided
the foundation for modern large language models (LLMs),
which are trained on massive datasets and exhibit emergent
abilities such as reasoning, summarization, and few-shot
generalization [27]. Several landmark models exemplify the
rapid evolution of this field.
1) BERT (BIDIRECTIONAL ENCODER REPRESENTATIONS
FROM TRANSFORMERS)
BERT, introduced by Google in 2018, represented a
breakthrough in natural language processing [179]. Unlike
previous models that read text in a single direction, BERT
used a bidirectional Transformer encoder, enabling it to
capture context from both left and right simultaneously.
Its pre-training objectives, masked language modeling and
next sentence prediction, allowed it to learn deep contextual
representations of text. The fine-tuning strategy pioneered
by BERT transformed the NLP landscape, as the same
pre-trained model could be adapted to diverse tasks such
as sentiment analysis, question answering, and named entity
recognition, consistently achieving state-of-the-art results.
2) GPT-3 (GENERATIVE PRE-TRAINED TRANSFORMER 3)
OpenAI’s GPT-3, released in 2020, marked a paradigm
shift in scale and capability [26]. Based on the Transformer
decoder, GPT-3 was trained with 175 billion parameters using
causal (autoregressive) language modeling. This unprece-
dented scale unlocked emergent abilities, including zero-
shot, one-shot, and few-shot learning, where the model could
generalize to new tasks from minimal instructions. GPT-
3 demonstrated coherent essay writing, code generation,
translation, and even reasoning across diverse domains,
178324 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
catalyzing widespread adoption of LLMs in industry and
public discourse.
3) PALM (PATHWAYS LANGUAGE MODEL)
Google’s PaLM, announced in 2022, showcased the potential
of the Pathways infrastructure to train models efficiently
across thousands of TPUs in parallel [28]. With 540 billion
parameters, PaLM demonstrated strong performance in
reasoning, logical inference, translation, and programming
tasks. It also advanced the field of multilingual modeling,
showing the ability to generalize knowledge across lan-
guages. PaLM’s release highlighted the trend toward scaling
models beyond hundreds of billions of parameters to unlock
general-purpose capabilities.
4) CLAUDE (ANTHROPIC)
Anthropic introduced Claude in 2023 as part of its focus
on safe and aligned AI systems [196]. Unlike purely
performance-driven models, Claude was designed using the
principle of ‘‘constitutional AI,’’ which encodes ethical
guidelines into the training process. The result was a system
intended to generate helpful, harmless, and honest responses,
mitigating risks of bias and harmful outputs. Claude’s
emphasis on alignment positioned it as an important step
toward trustworthy deployment of LLMs in sensitive domains
such as education, healthcare, and legal assistance.
5) GEMINI (GOOGLE DEEPMIND)
Gemini, released by Google DeepMind in 2023, represents
the next generation of LLMs with a strong emphasis on
multimodality [197]. Unlike earlier models restricted to text,
Gemini integrates text, code, images, and audio within a
unified framework, enabling more natural human–computer
interaction. Positioned as the successor to the PaLM family,
Gemini reflects a shift toward models that combine general-
purpose reasoning, creativity, and multimodal understanding,
bringing AI closer to the vision of a generalist agent.
E. APPLICATIONS OF LLMs
The impact of large language models extends far beyond
benchmarks, with tangible applications across industries.
In customer-facing contexts, text generation underpins chat-
bots, digital assistants, and automated content creation,
enabling natural and engaging interactions. Multimodal
capabilities, introduced in models like GPT-4 and Gemini,
allow users to query images, generate captions, and ana-
lyze audio, enhancing accessibility and creative workflows.
Code-focused models such as GitHub Copilot accelerate
software development by suggesting snippets, debugging,
and explaining algorithms, reducing barriers for novice
programmers. In global communication, LLMs have set
new standards in translation and summarization, enabling
real-time multilingual dialogue and concise information
extraction from lengthy documents.
LLMs are also transforming specialized sectors. In health-
care, they support clinical documentation, decision support,
and patient interaction. In law, they enable document
review, legal research, and contract analysis. In education,
personalized tutoring, language learning, and automated
grading are becoming increasingly accessible. Across these
domains, LLMs function as both productivity enhancers and
creative collaborators, making them central to the next wave
of AI-driven transformation.
1) TEXT GENERATION AND CONVERSATIONAL AI
Text generation remains one of the most prominent ap- plica-
tions of LLMs. General-purpose models such as ChatGPT,
Google Bard, and Microsoft’s Bing Copilot are deployed
widely as conversational agents, supporting customer service,
productivity, and personal assistance. For instance, banking
and telecom companies integrate chatbots into customer
support workflows to reduce call center loads, while
healthcare providers use conversational AI to guide patients
through appointment scheduling and symptom triage. LLM-
based creative tools, such as Sudowrite, assist authors
with story generation, scriptwriting, and content ideation,
demonstrating that language models are increasingly used to
augment human creativity.
2) MULTIMODAL AI
Recent LLMs integrate modalities beyond text, bringing
together language, images, audio, and video for richer forms
of interaction. OpenAI’s GPT-4V and DeepMind’s Gemini
are examples of multimodal systems capable of interpreting
images, generating captions, or answering questions about
diagrams and charts. These models enable new accessibility
solutions, such as describing images to the visually impaired,
as well as new forms of creative content generation in
art, design, and marketing. Enterprise applications include
multimodal knowledge search, where a user may provide a
chart or diagram along with a query, and the system generates
a grounded textual analysis.
3) CODE GENERATION AND SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
Specialized LLMs trained on source code have trans- formed
software engineering practices. GitHub Copilot, Replit
Ghostwriter, and Tabnine assist developers by suggesting
code completions, generating boilerplate functions, and
detecting bugs. These tools improve developer productivity,
accelerate prototyping, and lower the barrier to entry for
novice programmers. In practice, entire development teams
now integrate Copilot directly into their IDEs, where the
model provides context-aware assistance during coding.
Moreover, enterprise organizations have begun fine-tuning
LLMs on proprietary codebases, enabling domain-specific
programming assistants that capture internal style guidelines
and security requirements.
VOLUME 13, 2025 178325

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
4) TRANSLATION AND SUMMARIZATION
LLMs have significantly advanced machine translation and
summarization. Google Translate and DeepL now leverage
LLM-based backends to deliver near real-time multilingual
communication, supporting global collabo- ration across
diverse languages. Summarization systems, such as those
embedded in GPT-4 and Claude, condense long policy
documents, legal contracts, or scientific reports into concise
outputs, saving professionals significant time in information
processing. For example, law firms employ LLM-based
summarizers for contract review, while scientific publishers
use them to generate lay summaries of research articles for
wider audiences.
5) QUESTION ANSWERING AND SEARCH
The integration of LLMs with retrieval-augmented generation
(RAG) pipelines has produced highly capable question
answering systems. Tools such as Perplexity AI, Kagi,
and enterprise RAG platforms (built on vector databases
like Pinecone and Weaviate) combine generative reasoning
with grounded retrieval. In practice, these systems are
used in enterprise knowledge bases to support employee
queries, in academic search to synthesize literature, and in
customer-facing help desks to provide accurate, real-time
answers. By combining parametric memory (the knowledge
encoded in the LLM’s parameters) with external databases,
RAG systems address hallucination issues and improve
factuality.
6) DOMAIN-SPECIFIC APPLICATIONS
Beyond these broad categories, LLMs are driving trans-
formation in specific professional domains. In health-
care, Epic Systems has integrated GPT-powered tools into
electronic health records, enabling automated clinical note
generation, patient engagement, and clinical decision sup-
port. In the legal sector, platforms such as Harvey AI use
LLMs for case summarization, contract review, and le- gal
research, reducing costs and improving efficiency for law
firms. In education, personalized tutoring systems based on
LLMs adapt lesson plans to individual students’ strengths,
support automated grading, and provide language-learning
assistance. In business automation, companies deploy
LLMs to process invoices, summarize emails, and automate
reporting tasks, streamlining day-to- day workflows.
Summary: The widespread deployment of LLMs is driving
innovation and efficiency across industries, from creative
writing and customer support to law, healthcare, and edu-
cation. Their ability to adapt across tasks, when combined
with retrieval and multimodal capabilities, positions LLMs
as central components of next-generation AI systems.
F. ETHICAL CONCERNS, BIASES, AND CHALLENGES
Despite their transformative potential, Large Language
Models (LLMs) raise a variety of ethical and practical
challenges that must be addressed for responsible and
equitable deployment.
1) BIAS AND FAIRNESS
LLMs are trained on vast corpora that reflect societal
language use—including underlying gender, racial, and
political biases. As a result, these models can uninten-
tionally perpetuate or amplify harmful stereotypes in their
outputs [198]. Mitigation strategies include curated data
selection, development of debiasing algorithms, and ongoing
fairness auditing [153].
2) HALLUCINATION AND FACTUAL INACCURACY
A significant challenge is the tendency of LLMs to
produce information that is syntactically plausible but
factually incorrect—a phenomenon known as ‘‘hallucina-
tion’’ [199]. Approaches to reduce hallucinations include
retrieval-augmented models (such as RAG), reinforcement
learning from human feedback (RLHF), and fine-tuning on
curated, factual datasets [200].
3) INTERPRETABILITY AND EXPLAINABILITY
The internal workings of LLMs are often opaque, making
it difficult to interpret or explain specific predictions [201].
Techniques such as attention visualization, feature attribu-
tion, and probing tasks provide partial insights but remain
an open research area for building user trust and regulatory
compliance.
4) RESOURCE INTENSITY
LLMs require massive computational and energy resources
for both training and deployment [152]. This raises concerns
about environmental sustainability, the carbon footprint
of AI research, and accessibility for resource-constrained
organizations.
5) MISUSE AND HARM
The power and fluency of LLMs can be misused to generate
misinformation, spam, or malicious content [202]. Ensuring
responsible use involves implementing robust access con-
trols, monitoring usage, and establishing clear policies for
deployment.
The Transformer model and its derivatives have revolution-
ized the field of AI, enabling machines to understand and
generate human language with unprecedented fluency and
coherence. Large language models such as BERT, GPT-3,
and Gemini represent the pinnacle of this evolution, offering
powerful tools for research, business, and communication.
However, as the capabilities of LLMs continue to grow,
so too must our efforts to address the ethical, social, and
environmental implications of their use. The next frontier in
AI will likely focus on building more aligned, interpretable,
and sustainable models that serve humanity responsibly and
effectively.
178326 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
G. ADVANCED TRAINING TECHNIQUES FOR LLMs
While pre-training on large corpora forms the foundation of
modern large language models, further refinement through
advanced training techniques is essential to align these
systems with human preferences, improve efficiency, and
extend their utility. Recent approaches emphasize supervised
fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback
(RLHF), knowledge distillation, and agentic frameworks.
1) SUPERVISED FINE-TUNING
After pre-training, models are often fine-tuned on task-
specific datasets with human-annotated examples. This
process guides the model to generate domain-appropriate
outputs, improving performance in applications such as
legal text summarization, biomedical question answering,
and customer service chatbots. Fine-tuning is also used
to reduce undesirable behaviors by incorporating curated
datasets emphasizing safe and helpful responses.
2) REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH HUMAN FEEDBACK
(RLHF)
RLHF [203] has become a cornerstone for aligning LLMs
with human expectations. By combining human preference
data with reinforcement learning, models learn to prioritize
helpful, truthful, and harmless outputs. This technique has
been central to the deployment of widely used systems such
as InstructGPT and ChatGPT, ensuring that the generative
capabilities of large models are better aligned with societal
values.
3) KNOWLEDGE DISTILLATION AND EFFICIENCY
Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from large,
computationally expensive teacher models into smaller
student models without significant loss in performance [204].
Recent frameworks such as DeepSeek [205] extend this
approach by combining distillation with structured sparsity,
modular architectures, and efficient training pipelines. These
techniques make powerful models more accessible for real-
time applications, edge devices, and low-resource settings
while reducing energy costs.
4) AGENTIC FRAMEWORKS
Beyond monolithic training, agentic frameworks treat LLMs
as modular reasoning components that can orchestrate
external tools, APIs, or specialized agents. Frameworks
such as ReAct [206], AutoGPT, and HuggingGPT [207]
demonstrate how LLMs can plan, delegate subtasks, and
integrate outputs from retrieval systems or computational
tools. This agentic paradigm moves beyond static text gen-
eration, enabling dynamic workflows for research assistance,
software engineering, and decision support.
In summary, advanced training techniques are shaping the
next generation of LLMs by improving alignment, efficiency,
and orchestration. Together, supervised fine-tuning, RLHF,
knowledge distillation, and agentic frameworks represent
critical steps in moving from raw pre-trained models
toward reliable, efficient, and safe AI systems suitable for
widespread adoption.
VII. CHALLENGES AND ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS IN
MODERN AI
As Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies continue to
evolve and permeate diverse sectors—from healthcare and
finance to transportation and communication—there is a
growing urgency to examine the challenges and ethical
considerations they bring. While modern AI systems such
as deep learning models and large language models (LLMs)
demonstrate remarkable capabilities, they also present signif-
icant risks and limitations. This section explores some of the
most pressing issues in contemporary AI, including data bias
and fairness, computational cost and energy consumption,
interpretability and explainability, and alignment and safety
concerns.
A. DATA BIAS AND FAIRNESS IN AI
1) UNDERSTANDING DATA BIAS
Bias in AI refers to systematic errors or prejudices embedded
in algorithms due to biased training data or flawed design.
Machine learning models, especially those based on large
datasets, learn patterns from historical data. If this data
reflects human biases or social inequalities, the model may
replicate or amplify them [153].
2) TYPES OF BIAS
Bias in artificial intelligence systems can take several forms,
often rooted in the data used for training. Historical bias
occurs when datasets capture and replicate inequities from
past human decision-making, such as biased hiring or
policing practices. Sampling bias arises when the dataset
fails to represent the diversity of the target population,
for example, through the underrepresentation of minorities.
Label bias is introduced when the process of annotating
data is subjective or inconsistent, leading to skewed model
training. Measurement bias, in contrast, is a product of the
data collection process itself, where the tools or methods used
to gather information distort the results. Collectively, these
forms of bias influence how models perceive, interpret, and
act on real-world data.
3) CONSEQUENCES OF BIASED AI
The consequences of unchecked bias in AI are significant
and far-reaching. In high-stakes domains such as hiring,
lending, and policing, biased systems can produce dis-
criminatory outcomes that reinforce systemic inequities.
In media and educational technologies, bias may perpetuate
harmful stereotypes, subtly influencing cultural narratives
and shaping perceptions. Healthcare and financial services
also face risks, where bias in predictive algorithms can result
in unequal access to critical resources, such as diagnos-
tic services or loan approvals [208]. These consequences
VOLUME 13, 2025 178327

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
highlight the urgent need for proactive bias management in
AI pipelines.
4) STRATEGIES FOR MITIGATING BIAS
Addressing bias requires a combination of technical, pro-
cedural, and social interventions. One of the foundational
strategies is curating diverse and representative datasets that
reflect the full range of populations and contexts in which
the system will be deployed. Beyond data collection, bias
detection and auditing frameworks are increasingly used to
monitor models for disparate impacts across groups [209].
Algorithmic fairness techniques, including approaches such
as equalized odds and demographic parity, provide mathe-
matical means to minimize unfair outcomes in predictions.
Complementing these methods, transparent model documen-
tation practices, such as model cards and datasheets for
datasets, offer clear accounts of model intent, training data,
performance characteristics, and limitations [210]. Together,
these strategies form the foundation for more equitable AI
development and deployment.
B. COMPUTATIONAL COST AND ENERGY CONSUMPTION
1) RESOURCE-INTENSIVE TRAINING
Modern AI models, particularly large language models
(LLMs) such as GPT-4 and diffusion models for images,
require enormous computational resources for both training
and deployment [152]. Training a single state-of-the-art
LLM may consume thousands of GPU hours and significant
megawatt-hours of electricity, resulting in substantial opera-
tional expenses and infrastructure demands.
2) ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT
The carbon footprint associated with AI research and
deployment is a growing concern [214]. Training large neural
networks generates considerable greenhouse gas emissions
due to energy-intensive data centers, which often require
extensive cooling systems and high-density power sources.
As AI models grow in size and popularity, their ecological
impact increasingly contradicts global sustainability and
climate goals.
3) ECONOMIC COST
The economic burden of training and maintaining advanced
AI models restricts access primarily to well-funded corpo-
rations and select academic consortia. This concentration
of resources exacerbates the technological divide, limiting
smaller startups, research labs, and developing regions from
participating fully in cutting-edge AI development [215].
4) MITIGATION STRATEGIES
Several approaches are actively being developed to reduce
the environmental and economic costs of modern AI. One
strategy involves the design of efficient model architec-
tures, such as DistilBERT and MobileNet, which achieve
competitive accuracy while using fewer parameters and
requiring less computation. Closely related to this is the
use of model compression and pruning techniques, which
simplify large models by removing redundant weights or
compressing layers, thereby reducing both storage and energy
consumption without substantially sacrificing performance.
Another important development is federated learning,
a decentralized training paradigm that distributes computa-
tion across edge devices. This approach minimizes the need
for centralized data transfer and reduces the compute load on
large data centers, directly lowering energy costs. In parallel,
Green AI initiatives have emerged to benchmark and
promote energy-efficient research. These efforts encourage
transparency in reporting computational budgets, energy
consumption, and associated carbon emissions, highlighting
the need for sustainability as a core AI design principle [216].
Together, these strategies emphasize efficiency not as an
afterthought, but as a fundamental requirement in the next
generation of AI systems.
C. INTERPRETABILITY AND EXPLAINABILITY
1) THE BLACK BOX PROBLEM
Modern AI models, particularly deep neural networks,
are often regarded as ‘‘black boxes.’’ While they yield
highly accurate predictions, they provide limited insight into
their internal decision-making processes. This opacity poses
significant challenges in critical domains. In healthcare, for
instance, trust and accountability are vital when models
assist in diagnoses or treatment recommendations. In finance,
regulatory requirements demand transparency in credit
scoring, lending, and trading algorithms to ensure fairness
and compliance. Similarly, in the legal system, the absence
of explainability undermines due process, as decisions
influenced by opaque models may lack justification and
accountability.
2) INTERPRETABILITY VS. EXPLAINABILITY
Interpretability and explainability, though often used inter-
changeably, represent distinct concepts. Interpretability refers
to the degree to which a human can understand the internal
mechanics of a model, such as how specific inputs are
mathematically transformed into outputs. Explainability,
in contrast, focuses on communicating the reasoning behind
model predictions in terms that are comprehensible to end
users, even when the internal workings remain complex. Both
are essential in ensuring that AI systems remain transparent,
trustworthy, and accountable.
3) TECHNIQUES FOR ENHANCING TRANSPARENCY
To address these concerns, a variety of methods have been
developed to enhance transparency. Feature importance
metrics, such as SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlana-
tions) [217] and LIME (Local Interpretable Model-
agnostic Explanations) [218], provide estimates of how
strongly individual input features contribute to predictions,
enabling users to trace outcomes back to specific variables.
178328 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
TABLE 8. Resource and infrastructure comparison for major LLM and RAG systems (2018–2025).
Model-agnostic tools extend this idea by applying post-hoc
interpretability techniques to any type of model, with-
out requiring access to its internal structure. Another
approach involves training rule-based surrogate models,
such as decision trees, to approximate the behavior of
complex models, offering human-readable if-then rules as
explanations. Finally, in high-stakes applications where
transparency is paramount, practitioners often rely on
inherently interpretable models-such as linear regression,
decision trees, or generalized additive models (GAMs)-
because their mechanics are straightforward and easily
understood.
4) TRADE-OFFS
There is an inherent trade-off between model accuracy
and interpretability. Highly interpretable models tend to be
simpler and may not capture complex, nonlinear relationships
in data. Conversely, models with superior accuracy, such
as deep neural networks or large ensembles, are often less
transparent and more difficult to interpret. Balancing these
considerations is a key challenge in deploying AI in high-
stakes environments.
D. AI ALIGNMENT AND SAFETY CONCERNS
1) THE AI ALIGNMENT PROBLEM
AI alignment is the challenge of ensuring that AI systems
behave in ways that are beneficial and consistent with human
values and intentions. As AI agents gain greater autonomy
and influence in decision-making, aligning their objectives
with complex human values becomes both more important
and more difficult. Misaligned AI can produce unintended
and potentially harmful outcomes, even if it is technically
competent at optimizing its programmed goals [219], [220].
2) MISALIGNMENT RISKS
Several specific risks emerge when AI systems are mis-
aligned with human values or societal goals. One common
issue is value misalignment, which occurs when AI agents
optimize for objectives that only partially reflect human
intent. This mismatch can produce outcomes that are techni-
cally correct according to the algorithm’s objective function,
yet undesirable or even harmful in practice. Closely related
are goal specification problems, where poorly defined or
incomplete objectives cause AI systems to pursue strategies
that achieve the letter, but not the spirit, of the task. Such
problems often manifest as reward hacking, in which an
AI exploits loopholes in its reward function to maximize
performance metrics while failing to solve the underlying
problem [221]. These risks highlight the importance of robust
objective design and careful alignment methodologies.
3) AUTONOMOUS WEAPONS AND SURVEILLANCE
The integration of AI into military and surveillance technolo-
gies introduces pressing ethical and societal challenges. The
development of lethal autonomous weapon systems (LAWS)
raises concerns about the erosion of human oversight in
warfare, the potential escalation of armed conflicts, and the
absence of clear accountability when autonomous systems
make life-or-death decisions [222]. Equally concerning is
the rise of AI-powered mass surveillance, which enables
large-scale monitoring of populations with unprecedented
precision. Without strong legal and ethical safeguards, such
systems risk infringing on civil liberties and undermining
privacy protections [223]. Together, these developments
illustrate the dual-use nature of AI technologies and the
urgent need for governance in sensitive applications.
4) LONG-TERM RISKS: AGI AND SUPERINTELLIGENCE
As research progresses toward Artificial General Intelligence
(AGI), the scale of potential risks expands significantly.
A central challenge is maintaining effective control over
systems that could surpass human cognitive abilities. Once
superhuman intelligence is achieved, traditional oversight
mechanisms may prove inadequate. This raises the possibility
of existential risks, in which superintelligent agents act
in ways that fundamentally threaten human welfare or
survival [224]. Such scenarios, while speculative, are taken
seriously by the research community, underscoring the
importance of precautionary alignment research.
5) AI SAFETY RESEARCH DIRECTIONS
To address these alignment and safety concerns, interdisci-
plinary research is focused on several promising directions.
One area is improving the robustness of AI models,
VOLUME 13, 2025 178329

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
ensuring they maintain reliable performance even under
adversarial attacks or when confronted with unfamiliar
inputs. Complementary to this are advances in verification
and validation, which use formal methods to mathematically
prove the correctness and safety of AI systems. Another
line of research emphasizes human-in-the-loop frameworks,
ensuring that human oversight remains central in high-
stakes decision-making processes. Finally, researchers are
developing explicit alignment frameworks, such as inverse
reinforcement learning, cooperative inverse reinforcement
learning, and constitutional AI, all of which aim to better cap-
ture and operationalize human values in AI objectives [203],
[225]. Collectively, these efforts seek to balance technical
capability with safeguards that prevent misaligned or unsafe
outcomes.
E. SOCIETAL AND ETHICAL FRAMEWORKS
As AI becomes more integrated into critical societal func-
tions, the need for robust ethical and governance frameworks
has grown. This subsection reviews major approaches,
regulatory efforts, and industry best practices for ensuring
that AI serves human interests and operates responsibly.
1) ETHICS BY DESIGN
Ethics by design emphasizes the proactive integration of
ethical principles throughout the AI development lifecycle,
rather than treating ethics as an afterthought. One approach is
value-sensitive design, which incorporates stakeholder values
such as privacy, autonomy, and fairness directly into technical
requirements and design decisions [226]. Another important
strategy involves the use of ethical checklists and structured
impact assessments, which provide developers with system-
atic tools to anticipate risks, identify potential harms, and
mitigate issues such as bias, discrimination, or unintended
consequences before deployment [227]. Embedding such
frameworks early in the pipeline ensures that ethical con-
siderations are treated as core design constraints alongside
accuracy and efficiency.
2) GLOBAL GOVERNANCE AND REGULATION
Policymakers worldwide are establishing regulatory frame-
works to guide the responsible use of AI. In the European
Union, the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) sets
strong rules on data privacy, algorithmic transparency, and
the right to explanation for automated decisions. In the
United States, the proposed AI Bill of Rights outlines
principles for civil rights protections, emphasizing the need
for safe, effective, and non-discriminatory AI systems [228].
At the international level, the Organisation for Economic
Co-operation and Development (OECD) introduced AI Prin-
ciples that stress transparency, accountability, and human-
centricity [229]. Together, these initiatives highlight a global
consensus on the need for robust governance mechanisms to
ensure AI development aligns with societal values.
3) INDUSTRY BEST PRACTICES
Beyond formal regulation, the AI community has developed
a set of industry best practices aimed at fostering ethical and
trustworthy systems. Transparent disclosures, such as model
cards and data sheets, provide stakeholders with critical
information on model intent, provenance, and limitations.
Organizations are also increasingly committed to building
diverse and inclusive teams, recognizing that heterogeneous
perspectives reduce the risk of groupthink and help identify
blind spots in system design [230]. Another cornerstone of
best practice is open research and reproducibility, where
companies and research groups publish code, datasets,
and benchmark results to enable independent verification
and continuous improvement. These voluntary measures
complement regulatory frameworks and help set industry
standards for accountability.
4) ETHICAL AI IN THE WORKPLACE
AI deployment in the workplace introduces unique challenges
related to employee rights and algorithmic accountability.
Systems designed for hiring, employee evaluation, or work-
place surveillance must respect privacy and autonomy,
ensuring that human dignity is not undermined in pursuit
of efficiency. At the same time, mechanisms for contesting
or appealing automated decisions are essential to maintain
fairness and due process in employment contexts [231].
By embedding such safeguards, organizations can leverage
AI to support, rather than constrain, workers, ensuring that
automation enhances productivity while upholding ethical
labor standards.
In summary, responsible AI requires a multi-pronged
approach that combines ethical design methodologies,
global regulatory frameworks, voluntary best practices, and
workplace-specific safeguards. By integrating these perspec-
tives, AI systems can be developed and deployed in ways
that maximize benefits while transparently and responsibly
managing risks.
F. CASE STUDIES ILLUSTRATING ETHICAL FAILURES
A number of high-profile failures have demonstrated the
urgent need for transparency, accountability, and fairness
in the deployment of AI systems. This subsection presents
illustrative case studies that highlight recurring challenges in
data bias, lack of oversight, and the social consequences of
automated decision-making.
1) COMPAS IN THE CRIMINAL JUSTICE SYSTEM
The Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alter-
native Sanctions (COMPAS) is a proprietary risk assessment
tool used in the U.S. criminal justice system to predict
recidivism. Investigations, notably by ProPublica [232],
revealed that COMPAS was significantly more likely to
misclassify Black defendants as high risk compared to white
defendants. This case underscored critical issues:
178330 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
• Lack of algorithmic transparency (proprietary models,
no public access to logic or training data).
• Perpetuation of historical biases, resulting in discrimi-
natory outcomes.
• The need for independent oversight and regular audit-
ing of automated decision systems in high-stakes
applications.
2) AMAZON HIRING ALGORITHM
In 2018, it was disclosed that Amazon discontinued an exper-
imental AI hiring tool after it was found to systematically
favor male candidates over female candidates [233]. The
model had learned from historical hiring data, which reflected
gender imbalances in the technology sector. Key takeaways
include:
• The risk of training models on biased or non-
representative historical data.
• How automated systems can amplify pre-existing social
inequalities.
• The necessity of careful feature selection, bias testing,
and human review in automated HR systems.
3) FACIAL RECOGNITION AND DEMOGRAPHIC BIAS
Numerous studies, including those by Buolamwini and
Gebru [234], have shown that commercial facial recognition
systems exhibit significantly higher error rates for people
of color and women compared to white males. Such biases
have led to wrongful arrests and heightened concerns about
the deployment of these systems in policing and public
surveillance. This case demonstrates:
• The importance of diverse and representative training
datasets.
• The risks of using AI for surveillance or law enforce-
ment without rigorous fairness and accuracy evaluations.
• The broader social implications of deploying opaque and
biased AI systems in sensitive societal contexts.
These case studies highlight the critical importance of
ethical design, ongoing auditing, and regulatory oversight in
AI applications, especially those affecting fundamental rights
and societal equity.
G. THE ROAD AHEAD: TOWARD RESPONSIBLE AI
The unprecedented growth in the capabilities and influence
of AI has made it essential to chart a path toward responsible
and ethical systems. As AI increasingly shapes decisions
in healthcare, finance, law, education, and government, the
stakes for ensuring these technologies act safely, fairly,
and transparently continue to rise. The next chapter in
AI must be defined not only by technical innovation, but
also by deliberate attention to ethical, legal, and social
dimensions, with concrete mitigation strategies integrated
into development.
1) HUMAN-CENTERED AND PARTICIPATORY AI
Future AI systems must be explicitly designed to serve human
values and societal goals. Human-centered AI prioritizes
user needs, accessibility, and inclusivity. Participatory AI
development actively involves end users, domain experts, and
affected communities throughout the design process. This
co-creation helps identify unintended consequences early,
aligns system objectives with user expectations, and fosters
trust. To counteract algorithmic bias, participatory practices
ensure diverse perspectives are included at every stage of data
collection, training, and deployment.
2) INTERDISCIPLINARY AND CROSS-SECTOR
COLLABORATION
Responsible AI requires collaboration across disciplines
and sectors. Computer scientists, ethicists, legal experts,
social scientists, and policymakers must work together to
identify harms, ensure fairness, and design safeguards.
Multidisciplinary AI ethics boards, public consultations, and
cross-sector partnerships provide mechanisms for inclusive
governance. In practice, this also means embedding fairness
auditing, impact assessments, and continuous monitoring into
institutional workflows rather than treating them as post-hoc
corrections.
3) EDUCATION, LITERACY, AND PUBLIC AWARENESS
A society equipped to critically engage with AI is vital for
democratic accountability. Incorporating ethics, fairness, and
responsible AI into STEM and humanities curricula will
foster technologists who understand societal implications.
Public awareness campaigns, transparent documentation,
and explainable interfaces empower non-expert users to
understand how AI works and what recourse is available
if harm occurs. Glossaries, simplified technical guides, and
explainable dashboards can help bridge gaps between expert
terminology (e.g., self-attention, vector databases) and public
understanding.
4) TRANSPARENT RESEARCH, AUDITING, AND OPEN TOOLS
Transparency is foundational to trustworthy AI. Open access
to code, datasets, and model architectures enables repro-
ducibility and external validation. Systematic documentation
such as model cards and datasheets highlight potential
biases, limitations, and intended use cases. Independent
audits and red-teaming exercises identify weaknesses and
help correct both bias and hallucinations before real-world
deployment. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), for
example, provides a practical technical solution by grounding
model responses in verifiable external databases rather than
relying solely on parametric memory.
5) REGULATORY AND ETHICAL FRAMEWORKS
Governments and international organizations are develop-
ing comprehensive frameworks such as the EU AI Act,
the U.S. AI Bill of Rights, and UNESCO’s AI ethics
recommendations. These policies emphasize transparency,
accountability, human oversight, and fairness. They also
increasingly mandate explicit safeguards against halluci-
nations, misinformation, and bias amplification. Industry
VOLUME 13, 2025 178331

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
self-regulation, ethical guidelines, and compliance audits
complement these policies by operationalizing standards for
everyday AI practice.
6) TECHNICAL ADVANCES FOR RESPONSIBLE AI
Ongoing technical research plays a key role in addressing
bias and hallucination. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques
improve transparency by making black-box predictions inter-
pretable. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
(RLHF) has been shown to align outputs more closely with
human expectations, thereby reducing harmful or misleading
completions. Retrieval-augmented models integrate vector
databases to ground responses in external facts, directly
mitigating hallucination. Fairness-aware algorithms, such
as adversarial debiasing and re-weighting, reduce bias in
both training data and outputs. Privacy-preserving AI tech-
niques (e.g., differential privacy, federated learning) ensure
compliance with data regulations, while energy-efficient
architectures and model distillation advance sustainability.
7) SOCIETAL ENGAGEMENT AND ONGOING OVERSIGHT
Responsible AI requires continued oversight after deploy-
ment. Mechanisms for user feedback, adaptive retraining,
and anomaly detection help ensure systems remain aligned
with societal values. Citizen assemblies, online harm-
reporting platforms, and participatory governance models
enhance accountability. For hallucination and misinformation
risks, continuous evaluation pipelines coupled with retrieval
grounding and human-in-the-loop verification can reduce
harm at scale.
In summary, the road ahead requires embedding
solutions-not just identifying challenges. By combining
participatory design, fairness-aware algorithms, retrieval
augmentation, RLHF, transparency frameworks, and global
governance, AI systems can evolve to be safe, fair, and
reliable. The future of AI will be transformative only if
progress in technical performance is matched with equal
progress in ethics, sustainability, and public trust.
VIII. FUTURE TRENDS AND THE ROAD TO ARTIFICIAL
GENERAL INTELLIGENCE (AGI)
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is advancing at an extraor-
dinary pace, and the journey toward Artificial General
Intelligence (AGI)-systems with broad, adaptable cognitive
abilities-remains a focal point for the research community.
Recent advances in multimodal learning, self-supervised and
zero-shot adaptation, neuro-symbolic reasoning, continual
learning, autonomous tool use, and advanced planning point
the way toward more general AI. This section outlines these
trends and discusses both technical and societal implications.
A. MULTIMODAL AI: INTEGRATING TEXT, VISION, AND
SPEECH
Multimodal AI systems process and reason across modal-
ities such as language, images, audio, and video. Modern
models, such as OpenAI’s GPT-4V [235], Gemini [186], and
Meta’s ImageBind [236], enable perception and generation
across diverse data streams, mimicking integrative human
cognition.
Key advances:
• Unified Transformer architectures for language, vision,
and audio (e.g., Flamingo [237], Gemini [186]).
• Cross-modal retrieval and generation, as in DALL·E
[238] and VideoLLaMA [188].
• Applications in healthcare (multi-source diagnostics),
robotics (sensor fusion), and creative AI [186], [236].
Key challenges include aligning modalities with different
structures, scalable model architectures, and
explainability [239].
B. SELF-SUPERVISED, FEW-SHOT, AND ZERO-SHOT
LEARNING
Self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a cornerstone
of modern AI, allowing models to learn directly from raw,
unlabeled data by designing tasks in which the supervision
is derived from the data itself. Techniques such as masked
modeling, contrastive learning, and autoregressive prediction
have enabled models to exploit massive corpora efficiently,
eliminating the prohibitive costs of manual annotation.
This paradigm underpins the success of large foundation
models, which exhibit remarkable zero-shot and few-shot
generalization by adapting to new tasks from prompts or only
a handful of examples [26], [185]. Notable breakthroughs
include CLIP [240], which aligned vision and language
representations for robust cross-modal retrieval, and wav2vec
2.0 for speech recognition, which extended SSL principles
to audio domains. More recently, models such as LLaMA
2 [185] and BEiT-3 [241] demonstrated how SSL can
be scaled to few-shot and cross-modal settings, further
reducing reliance on annotated datasets. These developments
mark SSL as a critical stepping stone toward AGI by
enabling flexible and generalized learning from diverse data
streams [242].
C. NEURO-SYMBOLIC AI AND REASONING
A key frontier in AI research is the integration of neural
networks with symbolic reasoning, often referred to as
neuro-symbolic AI. While deep learning excels at perception
tasks such as image recognition and language understanding,
it struggles with tasks requiring explicit logical reasoning or
structured knowledge representation. By combining neural
modules for perception with symbolic systems for logic, plan-
ning, and rule-based inference, neuro-symbolic approaches
aim to deliver both accuracy and interpretability [243].
Recent work explores differentiable logic layers, program
synthesis, and reasoning agents that combine symbolic oper-
ations with learned embeddings [244], [245]. Applications
span explainable math problem solving, scientific hypothesis
generation, and legal reasoning, demonstrating the potential
of hybrid systems to bridge the gap between statistical
learning and symbolic abstraction.
178332 VOLUME 13, 2025

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
D. CONTINUAL, LIFELONG, AND TRANSFER LEARNING
One of the persistent challenges in machine learning is catas-
trophic forgetting, where models lose previously acquired
knowledge when trained on new tasks. Research in continual
and lifelong learning seeks to develop algorithms capable
of adapting to new information without sacrificing past
knowledge [246], [247]. Online adaptation techniques are
increasingly vital for robotics and other dynamic environ-
ments, where agents must continuously integrate new sensory
data and adjust behaviors in real time. Complementing this,
meta-learning approaches-often described as ‘‘learning to
learn’’-aim to create models that can rapidly adapt to novel
tasks from minimal data, a property essential for general
intelligence [248]. These directions not only address practical
concerns in real-world systems but also move closer to the
adaptability required for AGI.
E. ADVANCES IN REASONING, PLANNING, AND TOOL USE
The ability to reason causally, plan over long horizons, and
interact with external tools has become a defining feature
of the most advanced AI systems. Models such as Causal-
BERT [249] begin to address causal inference, enabling
machines to go beyond correlation and identify cause-
effect relationships. In parallel, tool-augmented agents such
as HuggingGPT [207] and ReAct [206] demonstrate how
language models can invoke external APIs, search engines,
or calculators to extend their reasoning capabilities. Another
active line of research focuses on world modeling, where
agents construct and update internal simulations of their
environments to guide planning and decision-making [250].
These advances collectively represent a significant expansion
of the problem-solving capacity of AI, enabling models not
only to analyze data but to act and reason in structured, goal-
directed ways.
F. TOWARD AGI: SOCIETAL AND TECHNICAL
CONSIDERATIONS
The convergence of scalable multimodal architectures,
neuro-symbolic reasoning, continual adaptation, and tool-
augmented models points increasingly toward the possibility
of Artificial General Intelligence. However, the path to
AGI is not solely a technical endeavor. Urgent challenges
include the alignment of powerful systems with human
values, mitigation of risks such as bias and hallucination,
and the establishment of governance frameworks that ensure
equitable and safe deployment [251], [252]. Research pro-
totypes like DeepMind’s Gato [253] illustrate the promise
of generalist agents capable of multi-domain competence,
while international initiatives emphasize the importance of
transparency, accountability, and human-centered design.
The next era of AI is therefore expected to unify technical
innovation with ethical responsibility, ensuring that advances
in intelligence contribute to societal benefit rather than
unchecked risk.
IX. SELF-SUPERVISED LEARNING AND ZERO-SHOT
LEARNING
A. THE NEED FOR SELF-SUPERVISION
Traditional supervised learning has achieved remarkable
success, but its dependence on large, meticulously labeled
datasets creates significant bottlenecks. Collecting such
annotations is time-consuming, costly, and often infeasible in
domains such as medicine, law, and low-resource languages.
By contrast, self-supervised learning (SSL) leverages the
vast availability of raw, unlabeled data to create supervisory
signals automatically, allowing models to scale without
explicit manual labeling [254]. This paradigm has become
a cornerstone of modern AI, underpinning the pre-training
strategies used in most state-of-the-art models.
B. FOUNDATIONS OF SELF-SUPERVISED LEARNING
SSL formulates auxiliary or ‘‘pretext’’ tasks that guide
models to learn useful representations. Early breakthroughs
include contrastive learning, where models learn to cluster
semantically similar samples while pushing apart unrelated
ones. SimCLR [255] and MoCo [256] established this
principle in computer vision. Masked modeling methods,
such as BERT [179] in NLP and Masked Autoencoders
(MAE) [257] in vision, introduced a simple but powerful
approach: hide parts of the input and train the model to
reconstruct them. Generative SSL extends this idea further,
with autoregressive objectives like GPT [26] predicting the
next token in a sequence, or video models predicting the
next frame. These diverse approaches share a common goal:
to distill meaningful structure and semantics from unlabeled
data.
C. ADVANTAGES OF SSL
The advantages of SSL have made it a universal paradigm
across domains. First, SSL achieves label efficiency,
as models learn from web-scale corpora without costly
annotation pipelines. Second, SSL-trained representations
exhibit strong generalization, transferring effectively across
a wide variety of downstream tasks ranging from translation
to classification [185], [240]. Finally, SSL is inherently
scalable, since the internet continuously generates massive
amounts of new unlabeled text, images, audio, and video.
These properties explain why SSL forms the foundation of
virtually all large foundation models.
D. ZERO-SHOT AND FEW-SHOT LEARNING
One of the most striking consequences of SSL is its
ability to support zero-shot and few-shot learning. In zero-
shot settings, models generalize to tasks they were never
explicitly trained on. GPT-3, for example, demonstrated the
capacity to answer questions, summarize articles, or translate
languages purely from prompt instructions [26]. Few-shot
learning further extends this capability, enabling models
to adapt with only a handful of examples-an essential
VOLUME 13, 2025 178333

R. Mundlamuri et al.: Evolution of AI: From Classical ML to Modern Large Language Models
property for emerging domains such as pandemic-related
medical research or rare disease detection [185], [238].
These generalization abilities mark a significant departure
from traditional machine learning systems, which required
extensive retraining for every new task.
E. REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS
SSL and zero/few-shot learning have moved rapidly from
research into production across industries. In language
translation, systems can now perform zero-shot translation
between previously unseen language pairs, as demonstrated
by multilingual models like XLM-R [258]. In healthcare,
SSL has enabled few-shot diagnosis in medical imaging,
where annotated examples of rare conditions are lim-
ited [259]. In security and surveillance, models trained with
SSL can identify new or evolving threats by recognizing
anomalous patterns in video streams. Creative industries
benefit from SSL through text-to-image models such as
DALL·E and Stable Diffusion [238], which generate art
and visual content from textual prompts. Even robotics
and autonomous systems are adopting SSL to learn visual
and sensorimotor representations, reducing the need for
handcrafted features or expensive labeled trajectories.
F. CHALLENGES AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS
Despite its success, SSL faces challenges. Contrastive
methods often depend heavily on the design of augmen-
tations and negative sampling strategies. Masked modeling
requires careful balancing between task difficulty and
useful representation learning. Furthermore, the evaluation
of SSL representations remains non-trivial, as performance
may vary significantly across downstream tasks. Looking
forward, integrating SSL with external memory systems such
as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) [46] and vector
databases promises even greater adaptability and factual
reliability. Advances in multimodal SSL, combining text,
vision, audio, and structured data, are expected to play
a pivotal role in the journey toward AGI. By enabling
generalization from raw, unlabeled data and fostering zero-
shot reasoning, SSL is reshaping the foundation of artificial
intelligence.
X. THE ROAD TO ARTIFICIAL GENERAL INTELLIGENCE
(AGI)
A. WHAT IS AGI?
Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) refers to highly
autonomous systems capable of outperforming humans
across a broad range of tasks. Unlike narrow AI, which is
tailored to specific domains, AGI aspires to general cognitive
abilities such as contextual reasoning, long-term memory,
meta-learning, and common-sense understanding [260].
These qualities would allow AGI systems not only to adapt
to complex, novel situations, but also to learn continuously
and generalize knowledge across domains.
B. CHARACTERISTICS OF AGI
The defining characteristics of AGI extend beyond raw
computational capacity. True AGI would demonstrate auton-
omy by setting and achieving goals independently, gen-
eralization through robust transfer of knowledge across
unrelated domains [253], robustness in the face of uncertainty
and adversarial conditions, and self-improvement through
continual refinement of its own learning processes [261].
These traits distinguish AGI from current large-scale models
that, while powerful, remain specialized.
C. AGI ARCHITECTURES AND APPROACHES
Several promising research directions illustrate potential
pathways toward AGI. Neurosymbolic AI seeks to integrate
symbolic reasoning with neural perception, improving inter-
pretability and abstraction capabilities [262]. World models,
exemplified by MuZero and DreamerV3 [250], [263], enable
agents to build internal simulations of their environments,
allowing planning and counterfactual reasoning. Memory-
augmented networks, such as Neural Turing Machines and
enhanced transformers, provide mechanisms for storing and
retrieving complex information across time [264]. Finally,
multi-agent systems investigate how intelligence can emerge
from cooperation, competition, and communication among
autonomous agents [265]. Together, these approaches suggest
complementary building blocks for general intelligence.
D. AGI RESEARCH MILESTONES AND PROTOTYPES
Recent systems demonstrate incremental progress toward
general-purpose AI. OpenAI’s GPT-4 [30] showcases mul-
timodal reasoning and few-shot learning, while DeepMind’s
Gato [253] illustrates how a single agent can perform
hundreds of tasks across text, vision, and control. Anthropic’s
Claude emphasizes alignment and safety through constitu-
tional principles [187], whereas DeepMind’s Gemini family
of models combines multimodal perception with logical
reasoning [212]. These prototypes highlight different facets
of AGI, though none yet achieve the breadth and adaptability
associated with human-level intelligence.
E. ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS AND GOVERNANCE OF AGI
The rapid progress of AGI research raises profound ethical
and governance challenges [266], [267]. Safety concerns
include value misalignment, where system objectives diverge
from human values, loss of control over autonomous
decision-making [221], and unintended consequences arising
from emergent behavior. Equally pressing are issues of
bias and fairness: AGI systems trained on unbalanced data
could amplify existing inequalities [268], and accountability
becomes difficult when outcomes emerge from autonomous
learning processes [227]. Regulatory frameworks, including
the EU AI Act [269] and the U.S. AI Bill of Rights [270],
aim to establish oversight principles such as transparency,
accountability, and human oversight. Complementary
178334 VOLUME 13, 2025
